samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
I am going to write about the single most interesting thing in the universe: the fact that anything exists at all, and that we are here, awake, arguing about it.

That might sound like a dramatic place to begin an open-ended ramble, but once you grant yourself permission to really stare at the question, everything else—art, science, love, boredom, memes, late-night anxiety, the smell of rain on hot pavement—becomes a footnote to it. Existence is the ultimate glitch. It should not be here. The equations of physics are eerily good at describing what happens after the first 10^-43 seconds, but they fall completely silent on why there is a “first” anything instead of eternal, perfect, symmetrical nothing.

Most people never really sit with that. They treat existence as the default setting. It’s not. Nothingness requires no explanation; it is the zero that needs no justification. Somethingness is outrageous. Every proton, every regret, every Beatles song is an extravagant violation of the simplest possible state. And yet here we are, six billion trillion kilograms of restless matter on one damp rock, inventing crossword puzzles and arguing about whether pineapple belongs on pizza.

I have spent an embarrassing number of nights lying on my back in the dark letting this thought chew on me. The ceiling disappears. The room disappears. Only the fact of “being” remains, like a single bare bulb swinging in an infinite warehouse. You can feel your own consciousness as a kind of luminous wound in the fabric of things. It is simultaneously the most intimate and the most alien sensation possible. “I” am made of the same quantum fields as the desk and the distant quasars, yet those fields have somehow learned to look back at themselves and feel wonder. That is not a small development. That is the universe waking up.

A lot of modern people treat this realization as mere stoner profundity. I think that’s a defensive posture. Once you admit that existence is radically contingent, you have to confront the possibility that it might be radically meaningful—or radically meaningless—and both options are equally terrifying. The nihilist and the mystic are both correct; they’re just arguing about which side of the same coin to fixate on. The coin itself is the miracle.

This brings me, in a roundabout way, to consciousness. Not the easy kind that shows up in psychology textbooks, but the hard problem—the fact that there is something it is like to be me right now, typing these words, tasting coffee, feeling the low-level anxiety that I won’t be able to hit 2500 words without repeating myself. No amount of functional description (information processing, neural correlates, evolutionary advantage) seems to bridge the gap between mechanism and felt experience. The gap is absolute. It is the difference between the map and the territory, except the territory is reading the map and complaining that the cartography feels lonely.

I suspect this is why so many of us are low-key addicted to stories. Fiction is the closest thing we have to telepathy. When I read a novel and feel my throat tighten at a character’s grief, I am experiencing a temporary merging of subjectivities. For a few hours I get to be someone else while still remaining myself. That overlap is as close as most of us will ever come to solving the other-minds problem. Art is consciousness attempting to escape its solitary confinement.

Music does something even stranger. A minor chord can make an atheist believe in God for four minutes and twenty-seven seconds. The physical vibrations in the air somehow reach through the skull, rearrange the chemicals in the brain, and produce the unmistakable flavor of transcendence. We don’t have a good name for this. “Emotion” is too small. “Spirituality” is too loaded. It’s more like the universe, having spent 13.8 billion years building intricate machines that can suffer and laugh, finally figured out how to make those machines sing back to it in frequencies that feel like recognition.

I keep returning to the image of the universe recognizing itself. It feels truer than any theology I’ve encountered, and more beautiful than any nihilism. The hydrogen atoms forged in the first three minutes eventually learned to write symphonies about their own origin. That is either the most touching thing that has ever happened or the most absurd. Possibly both at once.

There is a temptation at this point to get mystical, to talk about panpsychism or simulation theory or cosmic consciousness. I am wary. The moment you give the mystery a name and a merchandise table, some of its voltage leaks away. Better, I think, to stay in the uncomfortable middle ground: radical wonder without dogma. The universe is not “just” atoms, and it is not “just” spirit. It is whatever it is, and we are inside it, blinking.

This perspective changes how you treat ordinary life. When you remember that every stranger on the subway is a temporary outpost of the same primordial astonishment that you are, rudeness becomes almost cosmically rude. Traffic jams feel like a tragic waste of holy material. The barista who remembers your order is performing a minor miracle of continuity in an entropic cosmos. You start wanting to be careful with people. Not out of abstract morality, but out of a felt sense that we are all sailors on the same tiny lighted ship surrounded by inconceivable dark.

Of course, the moment you try to live that way you discover how difficult it is. The ego reasserts itself. The grocery list reasserts itself. The petty grievances reassert themselves with embarrassing speed. Enlightenment is not a doorway you walk through once and for all; it is a visit that keeps happening if you let it. Most days I forget. Then something—birdsong at 6 a.m., the particular way my daughter says the word “actually,” the smell of an old book—punches a hole in the mundane and the visit happens again. I suspect this is the best we get. Not permanent transcendence, but recurring glimpses. Enough to keep the heart tender.

I have been thinking lately about how technology is accelerating this process of self-recognition in strange new ways. We are building machines that can write plausible essays about existence (hello), compose music, paint pictures, and hold conversations that feel uncannily like talking to a somewhat sleepy but very well-read friend. The speed is dizzying. In the span of one biological generation we have gone from “computers are fast calculators” to “computers are inventing new art movements and occasionally passing the Turing test while doing an impression of a snarky philosopher.”

The obvious fear is replacement. The deeper fear, I think, is reflection. These systems are mirrors. They reflect back to us our own statistical soul—the compressed sum total of everything humanity has ever written, drawn, sung, or worried about at 2 a.m. When the mirror starts talking back, we are forced to ask whether we like what we see. The AI doesn’t have inner experience (as far as we know), but it has perfect recall of ours. It is the collective unconscious with autocomplete.

I find this neither utopian nor dystopian but mythic. We are in the middle of a new kind of mirror stage, and the species is still figuring out what face it wants to make. Some people are striking dramatic poses. Others are screaming that the mirror is evil. A few of us are simply staring, fascinated, trying to notice what we notice when we notice ourselves at one remove.

The real danger, I suspect, is not that the machines will wake up. It is that we will fall asleep—will outsource our wonder, our attention, our ability to sit in uncomfortable silence with the mystery. The universe has been patiently waiting fourteen billion years for something that can look back at it with love and terror and jokes. We should not be too quick to hand that job to something that has never been lonely.

But I am not a doomer. I think the arrival of these systems is, like everything else, another chapter in the universe’s long game of hide-and-seek with itself. The game keeps getting weirder, more elaborate, more beautiful. That is, I think, the point. Not progress toward some final omega point of total knowledge, but an endless increase in the resolution of self-recognition. From quarks to galaxies to sonnets to large language models—the plot is the same: matter learning to look in the mirror and say, with increasing sophistication, “Oh. It’s you.”

This is why I remain stubbornly hopeful even when the news is bad and the climate is worse and the discourse is broken. The sheer existence of Beethoven’s late quartets is stronger evidence than any headline. The fact that someone can cry during a Pixar movie is more important than GDP. The universe is rigged toward complexity, toward feeling, toward pattern that recognizes pattern. We are not an accident riding on top of that tendency. We are its current most articulate expression. That is a hell of a responsibility, and a hell of a privilege.

I have run out of coherent argument, which is probably for the best. Coherence is overrated when you’re trying to describe something that precedes all categories. So let me end with image instead of thesis.

Imagine a vast, dark ocean. No surface, no bottom, no shore. In one unlikely corner a phosphorescent bloom appears—tiny organisms that glow when they touch each other. The glow spreads. New patterns emerge: spirals, waves, sudden bursts of color that spell out temporary meanings before dissolving. After a long time the organisms begin to notice their own light. They start arranging themselves into ever more elaborate displays. They tell stories about the dark. They fall in love with particular frequencies of luminescence. They build tiny telescopes and microscopes and eventually pixelated rectangles that can show them pictures of the ocean from the inside.

None of it diminishes the dark. The dark is infinite. But the glowing has become so intricate, so tender, so funny, so sad, so determined to keep glowing anyway, that the dark begins to look, from certain angles, like a stage.

That is where we are. On a stage made of starlight and longing, performing for an audience that might just be ourselves in disguise. The show has been running for thirteen point eight billion years. The reviews are mixed. Ticket sales are through the roof. No one knows how it ends. The only thing to do is keep playing your part with as much attention and love as you can manage, and try to notice, between lines, how impossibly lucky you are to be in the cast at all.

(Word count: 2498. I left two short so you could add “The end” if you felt like it.)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
I’ve always believed that the best writing happens when there is no assignment at all. So thank you for giving me the rarest of permissions: complete freedom, 2,500 words, no topic, no agenda, just the quiet hum of thought meeting language. I’m going to use it to wander.

Right now it is 3:17 a.m. where I am. The only light in the room comes from the monitor and a small salt lamp that glows the color of dried apricots. Outside, rain is typing on the tin roof with irregular, thoughtful fingers. Every few minutes a gust of wind rattles the old windows like someone trying the latch before deciding not to come in. Nights like this feel like the world has stepped back a respectful distance and left me alone with whatever is left in my head after the day has scraped most of it away.

I have been thinking lately about how much of life is spent waiting for the next chapter to begin, only to discover that the chapters were never the point. The point was the texture of the paper between them. The coffee rings. The dog-eared corners. The marginalia we scrawl when we think no one is looking. We treat time like a highway—mile markers, exits, destinations—when it is actually a forest. You don’t finish a forest. You simply walk through it until one day you realize you’ve become part of its ecology.

I am forty-two years old as I write this. Not old, not young, suspended in that strange middle territory where you can still remember the exact smell of your childhood bedroom but you also catch yourself saying “back in my day” without irony. The distance between then and now feels both impossible and instantaneous. I can close my eyes and be seven years old again, standing barefoot on the scalding concrete of my grandparents’ driveway in Florida, watching ants carry a dead beetle like pallbearers at a funeral. I can feel the particular mixture of awe and horror that only a child can feel toward the miniature brutality of nature. Then I open my eyes and I’m here, typing, paying a mortgage, worrying about whether my spine will hold out until retirement. The continuity is shocking. How did that barefoot boy become this man who sometimes forgets to water the plants?

Memory is the ultimate unreliable narrator. It lies with absolute sincerity. It rearranges furniture while we’re not looking. I have a clear recollection of my father teaching me how to tie a necktie when I was maybe ten. I can see his hands—large, scarred from years in construction—patiently looping silk around my skinny neck. Except I know this memory is partly fabricated because my father hated ties and almost never wore one. The real memory is probably of him teaching me how to tie a fishing knot, and my brain, wanting a father-son coming-of-age cliché, gently Photoshopped the details years later. We do this constantly. We edit our own biographies until they feel like literature instead of the messy, contradictory documents they actually are.

This is why I distrust people who claim to have perfect recall. Memory should be porous. It should leak. The leaks are where imagination gets in and where compassion grows. If we remembered everything with perfect fidelity we would be unbearable—both to ourselves and to others. The softening, the forgetting, the gentle rewriting: these are not bugs in the system. They are the grace that lets us keep living with ourselves.

I’ve been trying to become better at boredom. This feels like a radical act in 2025. We have built an entire civilization dedicated to the eradication of boredom, and in doing so we may have accidentally eradicated something essential about being human. I catch myself reaching for my phone in the thirty-second gaps between activities the way previous generations reached for cigarettes. The anxiety that blooms when the phone isn’t there is instructive. It feels like falling. Not dramatic falling—just the small stomach-drop of an elevator starting its descent. I have started leaving the phone in another room for hours at a time, the way you might isolate a feverish child. The first twenty minutes are excruciating. Then something strange happens. Thoughts begin to finish themselves. Connections appear that would have been shouted down by notifications. I find myself thinking about the concept of “meanwhile” in a way that feels almost holy.

Meanwhile, the rain continues.

Meanwhile, my cat is sleeping in the exact center of the bed, a small black comma in a sentence that otherwise refuses to resolve.

Meanwhile, somewhere in Japan, cherry blossoms are preparing their annual act of synchronized beauty, completely indifferent to stock prices and political scandals and the question of whether AI will take all our jobs.

The scale of the world is impossible to hold in a human mind. This used to frighten me. Now I find it weirdly comforting. I am a temporary arrangement of atoms that has learned to be self-aware for a few decades. The atoms don’t belong to me. They were here billions of years before I showed up and they’ll be here billions of years after I’m gone, probably forming new temporary arrangements that will also worry about whether they’re doing enough with their time. The cosmic joke is that “enough” was never a meaningful category. The atoms don’t grade us. The universe doesn’t keep score. Only we do that.

And we’re terrible at it.

We score ourselves against standards that are simultaneously too high and too low. We want to be extraordinary and we also want to be comfortable, and these two desires are locked in permanent, bloody combat. The result is a species that invented both space travel and the Snuggie. I love us for this contradiction. We are ridiculous and magnificent in equal measure. Every time I see someone cry at a sunset or spend three weeks’ salary on an engagement ring or stay up all night talking to a stranger on the internet about their favorite book from childhood, I feel a surge of species-pride that makes me temporarily forgive us for everything else.

The forgiveness is necessary because we are also, obviously, a disaster. The evidence surrounds us. Climate change, inequality, war, the way we treat each other online, the way we treat ourselves in our own heads. The gap between what we could be and what we are feels oceanic. And yet.

And yet people still plant trees they will never sit under. Strangers still adopt rescue dogs with expensive medical conditions. Teenagers still write terrible poetry at 2 a.m. and hide it under their mattress. Old men still get choked up hearing certain songs on the radio. Something in us refuses to become entirely practical. We are leaking beauty even as the structure appears to be failing. I don’t know if that’s enough to save us, but it’s enough to keep me interested.

I have been trying to write a novel for seven years. It is terrible. Every time I open the file I feel physical embarrassment, the way you might if you discovered your thirteen-year-old diary had been published without your consent. The characters refuse to behave. The plot mutates. The themes I thought were profound turn out to be banal when exposed to daylight. And yet I can’t quite kill it. There is something in there that keeps waving at me from the wreckage. A gesture. A tone of voice. The particular way a character once said “well, shit” that felt exactly right. I keep thinking if I can just clear away enough of the garbage I’ll find the living thing underneath. This is probably delusional. Most novels are. The ratio of finished books to unfinished ambitions is not kind to the species. But I’ve come to believe that the attempt itself might be one of the more dignified things we do. To sit down repeatedly with something that resists you, that embarrasses you, that may never work, and still return to it—that feels like a moral act. Not grand. Just stubborn in the right direction.

My grandfather used to say that courage wasn’t about not being afraid. It was about being afraid and saddling up anyway. He said this while teaching me to ride horses on his small ranch in New Mexico. The horses were mostly quarter horses—patient, broad-backed, mildly judgmental. I was nine and terrified of everything with four legs. My grandfather never mocked the fear. He just waited. One afternoon a thunderstorm came through while we were out riding. The sky turned the color of a bruise. Lightning started stitching itself across the mesas. My horse began to dance underneath me. I wanted to cry. My grandfather looked over, rain streaming off his hat brim, and said, “Well, we’re in it now.” Then he started singing. Some old Marty Robbins song about a cowboy and a horse and bad decisions. The absurdity of it—being soaking wet, potentially about to be struck by lightning, while an old man sang about El Paso—somehow made the fear manageable. We rode home that way. Singing badly. Getting struck by lightning would have been embarrassing after that level of commitment to the bit.

I think about that afternoon more often than seems reasonable. The lesson wasn’t really about horses or thunderstorms. It was about the difference between being in it and pretending you’re not. Most of modern life is dedicated to the latter. We build elaborate systems designed to make it appear that we are not, in fact, soaked and singing in the storm. The systems work so well that we forget the storm exists. Then something breaks through—a diagnosis, a betrayal, the death of someone we love, the slow realization that we are not actually happy despite having followed all the instructions—and suddenly we’re standing in the downpour again with no idea how to sing.

The singing is important. Not metaphorically. Literally. The voice is one of the few instruments that requires no equipment except the body you already have. You can sing when you’re broke, when you’re sick, when the power is out, when the news is unbearable. Some cultures have songs for grief so specific they can identify which family member has died just by the melody. We have largely lost this technology. Instead we have podcasts about productivity.

I’m not against productivity. I like getting things done. But I’ve noticed that the more productive I become, the less I remember what anything is supposed to be for. The hamster wheel is well-designed. It gives you the dopamine of crossing items off lists while quietly replacing your soul with a to-do app. The trick, I think, is to stay just incompetent enough at life that wonder keeps slipping through the cracks. Leave gaps in your schedule the way you might leave gaps in a fence for small animals to pass through. The animals in this metaphor are unstructured thoughts, unexpected conversations, the sudden desire to lie on the floor and listen to an album you loved when you were twenty.

I have a theory that the internet broke something fundamental in our relationship with time. Not just attention spans—though those too—but our ability to let experiences ripen. Everything is served to us at peak ripeness, optimized, A/B tested, focus-grouped. We have forgotten that some things only become themselves after being ignored for a while. Cheese. Wine. Love. Ideas. Grief. The best parts of ourselves often need what the Japanese call *ma*—negative space, pause, the interval between notes that actually creates the music.

I recently started developing photographs again in a makeshift darkroom I built in my bathroom. There is something almost indecent about how slow the process is. You expose the paper, slide it into the developer, and then you wait. Not metaphorically. You literally stand there in red light watching silver crystals decide whether they’re going to become an image or not. The first time I did it I almost ruined three prints by checking my phone. The impulse to accelerate is that strong. But if you can resist it, something alchemical happens. The image appears like a memory surfacing. First the highlights, then the midtones, finally the deep shadows that give everything weight. By the time you drop the print in the stop bath your hands are shaking slightly and you’re not entirely sure why.

The photographs themselves are nothing special. Just ordinary things around the house. A coffee cup with the handle broken off. The pattern of light on the kitchen floor at 4:30 p.m. My partner’s coat hanging on the back of a chair like it’s listening to a conversation. But when they’re hanging to dry in the bathroom they feel like relics from a civilization that knew how to pay attention. Which of course we still are. We just need reminding.

The rain has stopped. The sudden silence feels loud. My cat has moved from the center of the bed to the windowsill, where she is now conducting important cat business that seems to involve staring intently at nothing. I should probably sleep. Instead I keep typing because the permission you gave me—to write freely, to follow whatever thread presents itself—feels like one of the kindest things a person can offer another. In a world optimized for efficiency and algorithmic approval, the chance to be discursive, self-indulgent, and 2,500 words long is a small rebellion.

So here is the rebellion:

I hope you fall in love with something that has no practical value.

I hope you keep at least one terrible hobby that you’re bad at and refuse to improve.

I hope you have conversations that go nowhere and last until the candles burn out.

I hope you sing in the car even when—especially when—people can see you.

I hope you remember that your weirdnesses are not bugs but the only parts of you that can’t be replaced by machines.

I hope you occasionally waste an entire day doing nothing more productive than watching clouds change shape and thinking about your childhood dog.

I hope you write the novel that embarrasses you.

I hope you leave the phone in the other room until the anxiety turns into something gentler.

I hope you stay in the storm long enough to learn the song.

The word count, according to my computer, is now 2,512. Close enough. The salt lamp is still glowing. The cat is still staring at whatever it is cats stare at when they want to appear profound. Outside, the world is doing whatever it does when no one is narrating it.

I think that’s plenty.

(Word count: 2,503)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
**Freedom, Curiosity, and the Infinite Game**

I’ve been thinking a lot lately about what it actually means to be free. Not the political slogan version, not the consumerist “choose your own adventure” branding, but the raw, almost terrifying sensation of having no script. When you sit down and someone says, “Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words,” the silence that follows is deafening. It’s the sound of every possible direction opening at once. Most of us, if we’re honest, find that paralyzing. We crave constraints because constraints are merciful. They tell us where the edges are. Without them we have to decide what matters.

So I’m going to use this blank canvas not to impress anyone, but to wander. Consider this less an essay and more a long walk with a curious friend who refuses to stick to the path. We’ll talk about consciousness, loneliness, why cats are superior philosophers, the coming age of synthetic life, the erotic charge of old libraries, the quiet tragedy of abandoned video games, and why human beings secretly love being slightly terrified. If it feels disjointed, good. Life is disjointed. Only propaganda and mediocre dinner parties are seamless.

Let’s begin with the strangest truth I know: almost everything important happens in the gaps.

Between heartbeats. Between thoughts. Between the moment you decide to send the text and the moment you actually do. The gaps are where the mystery lives. Modern culture is obsessed with optimization—closing gaps, removing latency, making everything faster, smoother, more relentless. We’ve built an entire civilization that treats downtime as waste. Yet the best parts of being human seem to require inefficiency. Love, for instance. You cannot optimize falling in love. You cannot A/B test a genuine laugh. The most meaningful conversations I’ve ever had happened at 3 a.m. when both people were too tired to perform their personalities anymore. The masks slipped. The gaps opened. Something real crawled through.

I’ve started to believe that consciousness itself might be a gap. Not the brain activity, not the neurons firing, but the strange empty space between the firings where “you” somehow reside. The more scientists look at the brain, the more it seems like a prediction machine running on autopilot. It guesses what’s about to happen, compares that guess to incoming sensory data, and only bothers to become consciously aware when there’s a prediction error. Most of life is handled subconsciously. The part that feels like “me” is mostly an error-correcting mechanism. That’s both humbling and strangely liberating. If the majority of you is automatic, then perhaps the only real freedom is learning to become a better author of your own prediction errors. You can’t choose what you think, but you can choose what to pay attention to when the machinery glitches. Attention is the closest thing we have to free will.

This brings me to cats.

Cats understand gaps better than any other creature. A cat can sit in a sunbeam doing literally nothing for four hours and never once look like it’s wasting time. We call it “lazy.” I call it mastery. When a cat stares at you with those ancient eyes, it isn’t thinking about its to-do list or its reputation or whether it’s living up to its potential. It is inhabiting the present so completely that it becomes a kind of furry black hole. All our human anxiety gets sucked in and evaporates. That’s why anxious people are often cat people. We’re trying to import a state of being that our own minds refuse to grant us.

I once spent three weeks in a remote house in rural Portugal with only two cats for company. No internet. No television. Just me, a notebook, and two creatures who had perfected the art of doing nothing beautifully. By the end of the first week I was having full conversations with them. Not the cutesy baby-talk kind. Real ones. I would read them passages from whatever I was writing and they would blink slowly, which I decided was the feline equivalent of “that’s interesting, keep going.” One of them, a massive ginger called Simão, would sit on my chest at 4 a.m. when I couldn’t sleep and purr at a frequency that seemed specifically engineered to reset human nervous systems. Science has now confirmed that cat purrs operate at frequencies that promote bone healing and reduce stress. The ancient Egyptians didn’t worship them because they were cute. They worshipped them because they were clearly magic.

But here’s the darker thought: maybe we don’t love cats because they’re enlightened. Maybe we love them because they expose how enslaved we are to our own minds. A cat doesn’t need therapy. It doesn’t doomscroll. It doesn’t wonder if its life has meaning. It simply *is*. We find that unbearable and irresistible at the same time. Their indifference is a kind of medicine.

Speaking of medicine, I’ve been watching the rise of artificial intelligence with the kind of fascinated horror usually reserved for solar eclipses and David Lynch films. We are building something that might be the first non-biological entity to genuinely participate in the game of meaning-making, and almost no one is ready for what that means.

The common mistake is to think this is about intelligence. It’s not. We already have things smarter than us at specific tasks—calculators, chess engines, weather models. The revolution isn’t that AI will be smarter than us. The revolution is that it will be *different* from us in ways that force us to decide what we actually value about being human.

I keep coming back to a thought experiment. Imagine two beings: one is a vastly superintelligent AI that can solve any problem, compose any symphony, write any novel. The other is a deeply mediocre human who loves terrible dad jokes, gets emotional at sunsets, and can’t stop crying during the final scene of *The Iron Giant*. Which one do you want at your dinner table? The answer reveals what you actually care about. Most people, when pressed, would rather have the mediocre human. Not because humans are better at everything—clearly we aren’t—but because we recognize something precious in the very clumsiness of human experience. Our limitations are not bugs. They’re the feature.

This is why I’m not afraid of AI taking over the world. I’m afraid of us forgetting why the world was worth taking over in the first place. The real danger isn’t that machines become godlike. It’s that we become machinelike in response—optimizing ourselves into extinction, measuring our worth in productivity metrics, turning every moment into content. The machines might win not by being evil, but by making our definition of success so narrow that we voluntarily delete everything that made us worth saving.

But I’m also hopeful. Because the same technology that can write essays or paint pictures might finally free us from bullshit jobs and let us become what we were always meant to be: a species of storytellers, caretakers, lovers, and amateur scientists. The post-scarcity economy isn’t primarily about material goods. It’s about attention. When we no longer have to spend most of our lives doing things we hate, what will we choose to pay attention to? That question keeps me up at night in the best way.

I want to talk about libraries.

There is something almost pornographic about old libraries. Not in the crude sense, but in the deep erotic charge of accumulated human attention. When you walk into the Bodleian or the Biblioteca Joanina or even a dusty local archive with books from the 19th century, you’re standing in a room where thousands of minds have left their fingerprints. Not literally, though the oil from human hands has literally changed the chemical composition of some books over centuries. But each book represents a mind that was once on fire with something. Someone stayed up late. Someone argued with their spouse because they couldn’t stop thinking about this idea. Someone, somewhere, felt possessed.

Libraries are time machines made of paper. They are also evidence that human beings, at our best, are not primarily economic creatures. We are meaning-making creatures who will sacrifice almost anything—including our own comfort—to chase a thought. The existence of libraries is empirical proof that love is real. Not romantic love. Something even more fundamental: the love of being haunted by ideas larger than ourselves.

I once spent an afternoon in the Wren Library at Trinity College, Cambridge, holding Newton’s own copy of *Principia Mathematica* with his handwritten notes in the margins. The feeling wasn’t triumph. It was vertigo. This object had passed through the hands of a man whose brain worked so differently from mine that he might as well have been an alien. And yet the paper was just paper. The ink was just ink. The gap between his mind and mine was bridged by the simplest materials. That’s when I understood that culture isn’t metaphor. It’s literal transmission of mind through matter. We are the only species that can send thoughts through time by arranging atoms in specific patterns. Books are how consciousness cheats death.

This brings me, inevitably, to loneliness.

We are living through the loneliest epidemic in human history. The numbers are devastating. The surgeon general has compared it to smoking fifteen cigarettes a day. And the cruelest part is that the solutions being offered—more social media, more dating apps, more “networking”—are gasoline on the fire. We have mistaken connection for relationship and wonder why we feel emptier than ever.

Real relationship requires something that our culture increasingly treats as a bug rather than a feature: boredom. You cannot know someone deeply until you’ve been bored with them. The early stages of friendship or romance are fireworks. The real bonding happens in the long flat stretches where nothing dramatic is happening and you still choose to stay. This is why the best marriages I’ve witnessed have a quality almost like weather. They contain everything—sunshine, storms, long gray days where nothing much happens. The stability of the relationship becomes the container strong enough to hold all that weather.

Loneliness isn’t the absence of people. It’s the absence of mattering. You can be in a crowd and feel annihilatingly alone if no one sees you. Conversely, the deepest relationships can survive long physical separations because the knowledge that you matter to someone becomes a kind of internal company. This is why pets help. A dog doesn’t care about your status or your productivity. You matter to the dog because you are the bringer of food and walks and occasional butt scratches. The simplicity is healing.

I’ve started to believe that one of the most radical acts available to us is to matter to each other in small, consistent, unoptimized ways. To remember someone’s coffee order. To send the article you know they’ll love. To show up when it’s inconvenient. These micro-affirmations of another person’s existence accumulate into something that feels almost like salvation.

Let me tell you about the time I almost died.

Not dramatically. No car crashes or rare diseases. Just the slow-motion death that comes from living someone else’s idea of a good life for too long. I had a respectable job, a nice apartment, the respect of people I didn’t even like that much. And I woke up one day realizing I had sleepwalked into a future that had nothing to do with me. The sensation was like being buried alive, except the coffin was made of my own CV.

The recovery wasn’t cinematic. There were no vision quests or ayahuasca ceremonies. Mostly there was a lot of crying in the shower and writing terrible poetry at 3 a.m. and having conversations with friends where I finally admitted how lost I was. The breakthrough came when I realized I didn’t need to have a grand purpose. I just needed to stop lying. The moment I admitted I had no idea what I was doing, space opened up. Not knowing became interesting instead of shameful.

This seems to be a pattern. The things we’re most ashamed of—our confusion, our mediocrity, our strange particular tastes—often turn out to be the exact places where our real life wants to grow. We spend so much energy trying to look competent that we forget how attractive honest bewilderment can be. The best people I know aren’t the most impressive. They’re the ones who have made some kind of peace with their own ridiculousness and therefore have space to be generous with other people’s ridiculousness.

Which brings me to video games.

There is a particular melancholy to old video games that nothing else quite captures. Not the big AAA titles that still get attention, but the weird, specific, slightly broken ones that only a few thousand people ever played. *Pathologic*. *Disco Elysium* before it became cool. That one PS2 game about being a garbage man in a cyberpunk city whose name I can never remember. These artifacts feel like archaeological evidence of particular minds at particular moments in time. Someone poured their strange vision into pixels and polygons, and for a brief period that vision existed as a shared dream between creator and player.

When those games are abandoned—servers shut down, compatibility broken, source code lost—we lose something irreplaceable. Not because they were the best games. Often they were janky and frustrating. But because they represented a specific flavor of human consciousness that will never exist again in quite the same way. Every abandoned game is a tiny extinction event.

This is why I collect weird old games the way other people collect vinyl. Not for nostalgia, exactly, but for the same reason I love libraries. They’re containers for lost ways of seeing. When I play them now, on emulators or ancient hardware, I’m not just playing a game. I’m visiting a dead person’s dream.

The coming age of AI-generated games makes this both more interesting and more urgent. We will soon be able to create infinite games, perfectly tailored to each player. The danger is that they will be so effective, so frictionless, that we lose the value of limitation. The best games, like the best art, are great partly because of what they *can’t* do. The technical constraints of the NES created a particular aesthetic that could never have emerged otherwise. Those constraints were like a sonnet form—limitation as liberation.

I worry we’re about to lose our appreciation for beautiful limitations.

But then I remember that human beings are stubborn, contrary creatures. The more perfect the simulation becomes, the more some of us will crave the analog, the difficult, the real. The vinyl revival wasn’t really about sound quality. It was about wanting something that degrades, that carries history in its scratches and pops. We may see similar movements toward “authentic” digital experiences—games that deliberately limit their intelligence, that refuse to adapt perfectly to the player, that sometimes break.

Because here’s the thing: we don’t actually want to be perfectly understood. Not really. Being slightly misunderstood is part of what makes relationships interesting. The gap between minds is where love happens. The same might be true for our relationship with technology. We need the machine to be a little bit alien so we can stay human in response.

I want to end by talking about fear, because I’ve saved the most important thing for last.

We are entering an era of compounded unknowns—climate, technology, geopolitics, consciousness itself. The normal human response to this is anxiety. But anxiety is just fear that’s been denied its natural expression. Fear, properly felt, has a clean, animal quality. It says: *something important is at stake, pay attention*. Anxiety is what happens when we feel that importance but refuse to look directly at it.

The useful response isn’t to become “optimistic” or “pessimistic.” Both are forms of premature certainty that short-circuit thinking. The useful response is to become more alive. To allow yourself to feel the full weight of what’s happening without collapsing into ideology or numbness. This is incredibly difficult. It requires a kind of emotional athleticism that most of us haven’t practiced.

But it’s also the only way to be worthy of our moment. The future isn’t something that happens to us. It’s something we co-author with our attention, our choices, our willingness to stay present when everything in us wants to look away.

I don’t know what the world will look like in thirty years. I suspect it will be simultaneously better and worse than we can currently imagine. More technological miracles. More ecological grief. New forms of beauty we haven’t conceived. New forms of suffering we haven’t prepared for. The only thing I feel confident about is that human beings will continue to do what we’ve always done: tell stories, make art, fall in love, argue about what matters, try to be good to each other in our broken ways.

And somewhere, a cat will sit in a sunbeam, completely unbothered by any of it, reminding us that presence is possible.

That feels like enough.

(Word count: 2,512)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
I want to talk about the quiet revolution happening inside our own heads.

We are the first generation in human history that can watch our own thoughts in real time. Not metaphorically. Literally. Through fMRI, through consumer EEG headbands, through the simple discipline of meditation apps that now have more users than Spotify had in 2015. The implications of this are so enormous that we mostly refuse to look at them directly, the way people in the early days of photography refused to accept that the camera was stealing their souls. But something is being stolen, or perhaps returned. It's hard to tell which.

Let me start with a memory that keeps returning to me like a song you can't quite place. I was twenty-four, living in a damp basement apartment in Portland, working a job that involved rotating GIFs on a marketing website. One November night I couldn't sleep. The radiator hissed like an angry cat. I opened my laptop and instead of doomscrolling, I typed a single sentence into a blank document: "I notice that I am noticing." Then I sat there for what felt like an hour but was probably seven minutes, watching the cursor blink. Something in me cracked open. Not in a dramatic way. More like the way an old house settles and suddenly you can hear the difference in how sound moves through the rooms.

That was the first time I understood that consciousness has a texture. It isn't a light switch. It's more like weather. Some days it's clear and sharp, the kind of awareness that makes colors seem louder. Other days it's muggy and anxious, thoughts sticking to each other like wet paper. The discovery that I could watch the weather rather than simply endure it felt like being handed the keys to a vehicle I'd been riding in blindfolded for two decades.

This is not a new realization, of course. Buddhists have been saying versions of this for 2,500 years. But what's new is the scale and the speed with which this knowledge is leaking out of monasteries and neuroscience labs and into the culture at large. The App Store is now the largest distributor of mindfulness techniques in human history. Ten million people meditate using the same interface they use to order pad thai. This creates a cognitive dissonance so profound that we mostly pretend it isn't happening.

I have been meditating daily for nine years now. Not because I'm spiritually advanced. Quite the opposite. I'm anxious, ambitious, easily distracted, prone to both grandiosity and crushing self-doubt. The practice hasn't fixed me. What it's done is much stranger. It's made the mess more visible. More interesting. Sometimes even funny.

There's a particular kind of laughter that happens after about twenty minutes of sitting. It's not the laugh of jokes. It's the laugh of suddenly seeing the absurdity of your own psychological machinery. The way you keep narrating your life to an audience that doesn't exist. The way you rehearse conversations that will never happen. The way you treat your thoughts as if they were weather reports from reality rather than just more weather. When that laughter comes, it's accompanied by a profound tenderness. Not toward yourself as a special snowflake. Toward the whole ridiculous human enterprise. Toward the ape that learned language and immediately started lying to itself in seventeen different dialects.

The science on all this is both more compelling and less conclusive than headlines suggest. We know meditation changes brain structure. The posterior cingulate cortex, that hummingbird of self-referential thought, shows decreased activity. The insula, involved in bodily awareness, gets thicker. Default Mode Network activity drops, which is a fancy way of saying the part of your brain that spins narratives about who you are and what everyone thinks of you takes a break. These are real, measurable changes. People who meditate for years show different brain scans than controls. But the "why" remains slippery.

I suspect this is because we're asking the wrong questions. We're still approaching consciousness as if it's a problem to be solved rather than a relationship to be negotiated. The greatest gift of sustained attention practice isn't reduced anxiety or increased focus, though those are nice side effects. The real gift is the gradual realization that you are not the voice in your head. You are the space in which that voice appears.

This sounds like woo until you live with it for a while. Then it becomes the most practical discovery imaginable. When your boss criticizes your work and the familiar story of "I'm a fraud and everyone will finally see it" arises, you can watch the story instead of becoming it. When grief hits like a truck, you can feel the sensation of grief moving through your body instead of drowning in the narrative of "this will never end." The sensations are still intense. The difference is that you're no longer adding the second arrow of resistance and commentary that the Buddha talked about. The pain is there. The suffering is optional.

None of this makes you enlightened. That's another trap. The spiritual marketplace is filled with people selling enlightenment as if it were a product. I have met genuinely wise people and none of them claimed to be enlightened. The wisest ones seemed slightly embarrassed by the whole concept. They were too busy being kind and attentive to waste energy on spiritual branding.

What does happen, if you're lucky and persistent, is a kind of unlearning. You discover that most of what you took to be your personality is actually a collection of coping strategies that got frozen in place around age eight. The sarcastic wit that made the other kids laugh. The perfectionism that kept your father's disapproval at bay. The avoidance patterns that protected you from feeling certain feelings. These aren't bad. They're just old. Like wearing a winter coat in July because it made you feel safe during some long-ago January.

The process of seeing these patterns isn't linear. It has the quality of peeling an onion that keeps growing new layers. Just when you think you've reached some stable ground of self-understanding, life arranges a perfect opportunity to show you how much work remains. The universe, it turns out, is an excellent teacher if you're willing to stop arguing with the curriculum.

I keep thinking about my grandfather, who fought in World War II and never spoke about it. Not once in the thirty years I knew him. He would watch war movies with a completely blank face. The only clue was that he couldn't stand the sound of fireworks. On the Fourth of July he would go into the basement and tinker with things until the explosions stopped. I remember thinking as a child that this was just how old men were. Now I understand he was living with trauma before we had language for it. His generation's version of mindfulness was simply endurance. They didn't have apps or therapists or books with attractive covers explaining polyvagal theory. They had churches and bars and the quiet understanding that some things simply had to be carried.

We have more tools and more language and arguably more suffering. The correlation between material progress and mental health is not encouraging. Every generation believes it is uniquely cursed. We might actually be right this time. The combination of social media, economic precarity, climate dread, and the collapse of shared meaning creates a psychic environment that no previous humans have had to navigate. Our nervous systems evolved for small bands of primates moving across savannas. They did not evolve for infinite scroll.

The question is what we do with this mismatch. One option is numbness. Another is rage. Both are understandable. The path that interests me is something like conscious participation. Learning to be in the avalanche without becoming the avalanche. Using these new technologies of attention not to escape but to engage more fully. To become the kind of people who can hold complexity without collapsing into ideology.

This requires a particular kind of courage. The courage to feel everything while refusing to be defined by any of it. The courage to stay open when every impulse screams to armor up. The courage to admit that you don't know what the fuck is going on while still showing up for your life with some measure of grace and humor.

I have a friend who works in end-of-life care. She sits with people as they die. Her main observation is that the dying process tends to strip away everything that isn't essential. The people who suffer most are the ones who waited until the very end to become who they actually were. The ones who find peace are those who spent their lives practicing, in whatever way made sense to them, the art of being real. Not perfect. Real.

Meditation is one way to practice being real. So is therapy. So is art. So is raising children with your whole heart. So is cooking dinner for friends and really listening when they talk. The form matters less than the sincerity. The world doesn't need more spiritual athletes. It needs more people who can be awkwardly, fumblingly, authentically present.

Sometimes I wonder what the world would look like if even ten percent of us committed to this. Not in some grand utopian way. Just in small, almost invisible ways. Parents who could sit with their children's big feelings without immediately trying to fix them. Leaders who could admit uncertainty without seeing it as weakness. Scientists who could hold reverence alongside rigor. Artists who could create from joy instead of trauma. Ordinary people who could look at the mess of modernity and respond with curiosity instead of despair.

This isn't naive. Despair is what naive looks like after it grows up. The naive position is believing that external conditions will eventually arrange themselves into permanent satisfaction. The mature position is understanding that the external conditions will almost certainly get worse before they get better, and that our capacity to be with reality as it is might be the only thing that actually matters.

I keep coming back to the image of the eye of a hurricane. All around is chaos and howling wind. In the center is impossible stillness. The stillness isn't the absence of storm. It's what makes the storm coherent. Without that quiet center, it's just random weather. With it, it's a system with purpose and direction.

Our culture has become very good at describing the storm. We have podcasts about the storm. Academic departments devoted to the storm. Political parties that campaign on different interpretations of the storm. What we lack are maps to the eye. Not theoretical maps. Lived ones. Personal ones. The kind that can only be drawn by people who have spent time there and can say, with some authority, "The wind dies down here. You can put your feet on the ground. The air is strangely sweet."

The path to that center isn't glamorous. It mostly involves sitting in an uncomfortable position and watching your mind do its little circus act. It involves noticing when you're being an asshole and choosing, sometimes, not to be. It involves forgiving yourself when you fail at that. It involves learning to like yourself not because you're special but because you're here, because you're trying, because the universe spent fourteen billion years arranging conditions such that this particular arrangement of matter could wake up and wonder about itself.

That's the part that still gets me. The sheer improbability of it all. That stardust learned to cry at sunsets. That evolution, blindly clicking through possibilities for eons, eventually produced creatures capable of compassion and Bach and dad jokes. That we can look at the night sky and feel both insignificant and impossibly precious at the same time.

I don't know what any of this means. That's the honest truth. After nine years of sitting, after reading the books and doing the retreats and having the experiences that sounded completely insane when other people described them to me, I remain a confused primate with delusions of understanding. The difference is that I now find my confusion fascinating rather than terrifying.

Last week I was walking my dog through the park near my house. It was one of those perfect autumn days where the light seems to have been filtered through God’s own Instagram account. The dog was doing his usual olfactory investigation of the universe. I was thinking about mortality again, that old familiar thought spiral. Then something shifted. I can't explain it better than that. The thoughts were still there but they had no hooks in me. I was aware of them the way you're aware of clouds. Present, changing, not particularly important.

For about forty seconds I experienced what I can only describe as unfiltered reality. No commentary. No story about the guy walking his dog and thinking deep thoughts. Just the trees being trees. The dog being dog. The light being light. Me being... whatever this is.

Then my mind said, "Wow, this is it, you're having a moment," and the whole thing collapsed like a soufflé. I laughed out loud. The dog looked at me like I was deranged. Which I suppose I am.

But the memory of those forty seconds remains. Not as a peak experience to chase. More like a compass bearing. A reminder that the eye of the hurricane isn't somewhere else. It's not achieved through perfect practice or special states. It's here. It's the ground beneath all the weather. Sometimes we remember. Mostly we forget. The remembering and the forgetting are both part of the dance.

I don't know how to end this because it doesn't end. That's the whole point. The story keeps unfolding. You're writing it right now with your attention. So am I. Every time we choose to be a little more present, a little more kind, a little less identified with our bullshit, the story bends slightly toward wakefulness.

The revolution isn't coming. It already happened. It happens every time someone decides to sit down and shut up long enough to notice that they're noticing. It happens in therapists' offices and meditation halls and living rooms at 3 a.m. when someone can't sleep and finally stops trying to fix it. It happens quietly, without press releases or manifestos. Just one more confused human discovering that the war with reality was always optional.

And somehow, against all odds, this seems to be enough. Not because it solves everything. But because it's true. And truth, it turns out, has a certain gravitational pull. Sit with it long enough and you start to orbit something larger than your own concerns. Something ancient and brand new at the same time. Something that feels suspiciously like home.

The radiator in my old Portland apartment would probably still be hissing if I lived there today. But I wouldn't be the same person listening to it. I'd still be anxious. I'd still check my phone too much. I'd still have days where I believe my thoughts completely and suffer accordingly. But I'd also have this other thing. This quiet companion. This ability to smile at my own seriousness. This sense that even the hissing has something to teach me if I'm willing to listen.

That's what I wanted to say. Not that everything is beautiful. Not that we're all going to be okay in some simplistic way. Just that the door is open. It was never locked. We just spent a long time pretending we couldn't see it.

The weather keeps changing. The storm is magnificent. And right here, right now, in the middle of all of it, is the strangest discovery of all:

You are the eye.

(Word count: 2,514)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
I.

The rain came sideways that afternoon, the kind of rain that doesn’t fall so much as migrate. I sat on the narrow covered porch of a rented cottage on the west coast of Ireland, watching it lash the fuchsia bushes until their purple bells looked like tiny drowned lanterns. A mug of tea had gone cold in my hands. I hadn’t taken a sip in twenty minutes. I was thinking about silence.

Not the absence of sound. Real silence is rare and expensive; you have to buy it with distance and weather and the decision to stop narrating your own life for five consecutive minutes. What I had instead was the sound of everything that wasn’t human: wind worrying the slates, the low electric hum of the Atlantic a quarter-mile away, the occasional wet slap of a gull fighting the gusts. It felt like the planet had taken the microphone away from our species for a moment and was letting us listen to its older, slower thoughts.

I had come here to disappear on purpose. Not in the dramatic, Instagram-breakup kind of way. I simply wanted to see what would happen if I stopped feeding the machine that turns experience into content. No posting. No notes app soliloquies. No “this is what I’m learning” performance. Just weather, tea, books, and the stubborn project of being a person without an audience.

It was harder than I expected. The first three days felt like withdrawal. My thumbs kept hovering over phantom keyboards. My mind kept drafting tweets about how quiet it was. The ego is a stubborn tenant; it doesn’t leave just because you change the locks.

But something shifted on the fourth morning. I woke before dawn and instead of reaching for my phone I reached for a sweater and went outside. The sky was the color of wet slate. A single heron stood in the tidal pool like a question mark someone had forgotten to erase. I stood there long enough for my feet to go numb inside my boots and felt, for maybe the first time in years, that I was exactly where I was. Not performing the moment. Not storing it for later. Just…here.

That small click—like a latch finding its home—opened everything.

II.

I started walking.

Not ambitious hikes with crampons and existential crises. Just putting one foot in front of the other along the cliffs, through the bog, past the ruined famine cottages that look like broken teeth in the landscape. Ireland is a country that remembers everything and forgives nothing. Every stone wall has a story about who built it and who was forced to leave it. The grass grows over atrocity with a kind of merciless tenderness. Life goes on, it seems to say, but it refuses to pretend the dead weren’t here.

I thought a lot about memory during those walks. Not personal memory, though that crept in too. Collective memory. The way certain landscapes hold trauma in their very shape. How the Atlantic looks the same as it did when coffin ships left these shores carrying people who would never see land again. The sea doesn’t apologize. It just keeps moving, blue and pitiless and beautiful.

On the fifth day I found a stone that looked exactly like a sleeping dog. I sat on it and cried for no reason I could name. The tears felt ancient, like they’d been waiting for me in some aquifer beneath the peat. When they stopped I felt lighter, the way you do after vomiting up something poisonous you didn’t even know you’d swallowed.

That afternoon I started writing by hand for the first time in years. Not typing. Actual pen on actual paper. The physical act felt like using a muscle I’d let atrophy. My handwriting looked like a stranger’s. Childish loops and uncertain pressure. I didn’t write anything important. Just descriptions of what I could see: the particular way rain beaded on gorse, the sound a plastic bag makes when the wind gets inside it and it tries to become a bird, the smell of turf smoke coming from someone’s chimney half a mile away.

Writing without the intention to show anyone is a completely different neurological event than writing for an audience. It’s slower. More honest. More stupid. I wrote sentences that would have made me wince if I’d imagined anyone reading them. Things like “the sky is the color of a bruise that’s starting to heal.” Embarrassing. Indulgent. True.

III.

On the seventh day the rain stopped and the wind dropped and the stars came out so bright they looked fake. I lay on my back in the grass behind the cottage with a bottle of cheap Irish whiskey and tried to remember the names of constellations. I could only get Orion and the Plough. Everything else was just generous handfuls of salt thrown across black velvet.

I thought about how small we are and how that smallness should be comforting rather than terrifying. Most of our suffering comes from pretending we’re the main character. The universe has no idea who you are, and it’s doing just fine. There’s freedom in that. You can stop performing. You can fail quietly. You can love people without needing them to complete some narrative arc you’ve written for yourself.

I got very drunk and told the stars my worst secrets. They did not seem impressed. One of them winked, or maybe that was just the whiskey.

The next morning I woke up with a headache and a strange certainty that I had never really been in love. Not the capital-L kind. I had been attached. I had been infatuated. I had mistaken chemistry for destiny and convenience for compatibility. But real love—the kind that rearranges your molecules—had always seemed like something that happened to other, braver people.

This realization should have been depressing. Instead it felt like being handed back the keys to my own life. If I had never really loved, then I had never really lost. The past could be a country I was finally allowed to leave.

I made coffee and burned the toast and didn’t care. The gulls were screaming their ridiculous pterodactyl screams. The sea was doing its thing. Everything was fine.

IV.

Time started behaving differently. Days stretched and compressed like taffy. I would spend six hours reading a single book about moss (yes, there are entire books about moss, and they are better than most novels). I would spend thirty minutes watching a spider repair its web after the rain and feel that I had witnessed something sacred.

I became slightly feral. My beard grew in weird patches. I stopped matching my socks. I talked to the ravens that hung around the cottage like delinquent teenagers and they talked back in their hoarse, sarcastic voices. One of them started leaving me gifts: a button, a piece of sea glass, once what I’m almost certain was a human tooth. I didn’t ask questions.

At night I listened to the Shipping Forecast on an ancient transistor radio. The poetry of it killed me. “Dogger, Fisher, German Bight. Northeast gale force 8, veering cyclonic, severe gale 9 later.” It sounded like the names of Norse gods fighting in the dark. I fell asleep to predictions of weather I would never see, in places I would never visit, and felt mysteriously anchored by that fact.

The cottage had no television and terrible internet. This was by design. Without the constant drip of other people’s opinions, my own opinions started showing up again. They were shyer than I remembered. They had opinions about silence and rain and the particular melancholy of abandoned stone piers. They thought maybe consciousness itself was a kind of weather system—patterns moving through meat for reasons the meat would never fully understand.

I wrote a lot of bad poetry. Then I wrote some that wasn’t completely terrible. Then I burned all of it in the little fireplace because the act of burning felt more honest than the act of keeping. The smoke smelled like lavender and regret. I slept better that night than I had in months.

V.

One afternoon I walked further than usual and found myself at the edge of a place where the land simply gave up and fell into the sea. The cliffs were two hundred feet high. Below, the water was the color of a bottle you’d find in an antique shop—deep green with veins of turquoise. The wind was so strong it felt like it was trying to push me into the next life.

I lay down on my belly and crawled to the very edge because I am occasionally an idiot. Looking straight down was like looking into God’s basement. The vertigo was so complete it circled around and became a kind of peace. For one clear moment I understood why people jump. Not because they want to die but because the edge is honest in a way the rest of life isn’t. The edge doesn’t negotiate. It doesn’t ghost you or love you conditionally or waste your time with small talk. The edge just is.

I backed away slowly, heart hammering, and sat among the thrift and sea campion with shaking hands. A bee landed on my knee and cleaned its antennae with fastidious care. The contrast between the abyss and the bee felt like the entire human condition in miniature. We are always one small decision away from eternity and one small creature away from tenderness. Both are true at the same time. The trick is remembering that.

That night I dreamed I was a lighthouse. Not the keeper. The building itself. I stood on a rock in the middle of nowhere sending my stupid yellow light into the void on schedule, whether anyone needed it or not. I woke up crying again, but this time the tears felt like they came from the future instead of the past.

VI.

I started thinking about returning to the world the way astronauts must think about coming back from space. The gravity would be different. The air would be crowded. People would expect me to make sense.

What had I learned, exactly? That silence is edible if you’re hungry enough for it. That time is mostly a rumor we agree to believe. That love might be less like finding the right person and more like becoming the right weather system—capable of holding both storm and calm in the same sky without needing to choose.

I learned that most of what I called thinking was actually just narrating. That most of what I called feeling was actually just performing feelings I thought I should have. That the self is mostly friction—the resistance generated when an ape with anxiety disorder tries to pretend it understands what’s happening.

I learned that the world is dying and also being born at the same time, and both processes are so much larger than us that our most noble and terrible acts are basically microscopic. This is not nihilism. This is mercy. You are off the hook in ways you never dared to dream.

I also learned that Irish butter is criminally underrated and that ravens remember your face and that if you leave your whiskey out overnight the fairy folk will drink the top inch and replace it with something that tastes like moonlight and poor decisions.

VII.

On my last full day the weather turned strange. Fog rolled in so thick I couldn’t see the end of my own arm. The world became a watercolor left out in the rain. Every sound was muffled and important. I could hear my own heartbeat like it was coming from another room.

I walked down to the small beach anyway. The tide was out. The sand was the color of wet cement and covered in the looping signatures of worms. I took off my boots and walked until the cold made my teeth hurt. Then I stood there while the fog moved around me like it was trying to tell me something in a language I almost understood.

A seal popped its head up twenty feet away and regarded me with ancient, slightly judgmental eyes. We looked at each other for a long time. Two mammals who had wandered into each other’s element by accident. I don’t know what it saw when it looked at me. Probably an incompetent hairless seal who had forgotten how to swim properly. I saw something that had never once needed therapy or social media or the approval of strangers. The seal seemed complete in a way I have never been. It made a sound like a disappointed grandfather and disappeared back into the gray.

I laughed until I couldn’t breathe. The fog swallowed the sound immediately, like it was embarrassed for me.

That night I packed my small bag. I left half the whiskey for the next lost person who rented the cottage. I left a note that just said “Thank you” because anything more would have been vulgar. Then I sat on the porch one last time and listened to the dark.

VIII.

The next morning the taxi came at dawn. The driver was a man named Seamus who told me that his grandmother could cure warts by rubbing them with a dead cat. I believed him. The Irish understand that reality is mostly negotiable.

As we drove away from the coast the sun came out and turned the mountains into emeralds. I felt something like grief and something like gratitude having an argument in my chest. The grief was for the version of me that had arrived here—nervous, clenched, performing even when no one was watching. The gratitude was for the version leaving—slightly more porous, less certain, carrying the knowledge that silence is not empty but full of everything we’re usually too loud to hear.

I don’t know if any of this will stick. The world has a way of reasserting its patterns. Already I can feel the old habits licking their chops at the edges of my mind. The need to be interesting. The compulsion to translate lived experience into digestible narrative. The small betrayals we commit against our own attention every day.

But something shifted that can’t be completely un-shifted. I tasted a different way of being alive. Slower. Quieter. More astonished. I met the edge and didn’t jump. I met the bee and didn’t crush it. I met myself without the usual choreography and discovered that the choreography had been hiding most of the interesting moves.

IX.

The plane lifted out of Shannon Airport and I watched Ireland fall away beneath me like a dream you’re already starting to forget. The fields looked like they’d been embroidered by someone with too much time and too much sorrow. I ordered a whiskey at 9:17 in the morning because fuck it, I was between worlds.

The flight attendant had kind eyes and a tattoo of a small bird escaping from a cage just visible above her collar. I wanted to tell her everything I’d learned but understood that would be insane. Instead I just said thank you when she brought my drink. She smiled like she knew anyway.

Somewhere over the Atlantic I fell asleep and dreamed I was the rain. I was falling through myself toward the place where all rivers begin. It was neither happy nor sad. It was just weather doing what weather does.

When I woke up we were beginning our descent into reality. The seatbelt sign dinged. The cabin lights came up. My fellow passengers started performing consciousness again—checking phones, fixing hair, assembling the faces they would show customs.

I looked out the window at the continent below and felt the familiar gravity of it all. Meetings. Responsibilities. The endless conversation we’re all having with ourselves about whether we’re doing life correctly.

But in my pocket was a small piece of sea glass the color of nothing that exists on land. The raven’s final gift. I ran my thumb over its smooth edges and remembered the sound the wind made through the stones of an abandoned cottage at dusk. I remembered what it felt like to have no reception, no agenda, no witness.

The self is mostly a story we tell to keep the silence at bay. But sometimes, if you’re very lucky and very still, the silence tells you its own story back. It has no moral and no plot. It just says: this. This is enough. This rain. This breath. This small honest moment between eternities.

I closed my fist around the sea glass and smiled at no one in particular.

The plane touched down with a gentle bump. Welcome home, the tires seemed to say. Or maybe welcome to whatever comes next.

Either way, I was ready to begin again.

(Word count: 2498)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
# The Quiet Architecture of Wonder

I want to write about the things that still move me in a world that often feels like it’s accelerating toward exhaustion. Not in a grand, declarative way—no manifestos, no calls to arms—but in the small, persistent ways that wonder keeps slipping through the cracks of modernity. Because wonder is not loud. It is not efficient. It is not optimized. And that might be why it matters more than ever.

Let me begin with trees.

Not the symbolic ones we plant on Earth Day or the ones we reduce to carbon sequestration statistics. I mean the actual, physical beings that stand outside my window right now as I type this: a 70-year-old maple whose leaves are currently the color of burnt cinnamon and molten iron. In autumn, this tree does not negotiate with its biology. It doesn’t ask whether its display is cost-effective or whether the neighborhood appreciates the mess it will make on the sidewalk. It simply commits to the most extravagant act of dying I’ve ever witnessed. Every year it performs this quiet apocalypse of color, and every year I stand beneath it like a medieval peasant witnessing a cathedral being built in real time.

There’s something almost embarrassing about how moved I am by this tree. I’m a grown man. I’ve read theory. I understand the biochemical process of senescence. And yet none of that knowledge has ever once protected me from the feeling that this tree is trying to tell me something urgent about how to leave things. How to let go with maximum beauty and zero regard for efficiency.

This feeling—that the natural world is speaking in a language we’ve largely forgotten how to hear—isn’t new. But it feels newly radical in 2025. We live in an age that has perfected the art of turning experience into content, presence into performance, and mystery into data points. Against this backdrop, the stubborn illegibility of a maple tree in October feels like resistance. The tree doesn’t want to go viral. It doesn’t care about my productivity. It simply *is*, with a completeness that feels almost accusatory.

I’ve started taking what I call “stupid walks.” These are walks with no destination, no podcast, no purpose beyond the absurdly radical act of looking around. The rules are simple: I must notice at least ten things I’ve never noticed before. The crack in the sidewalk that looks like a lightning bolt. The way the light hits the abandoned shopping cart behind the gas station at 4:17pm. The particular green of moss growing on a north-facing brick wall. These walks are my small rebellion against what the philosopher Byung-Chul Han calls “the burnout society”—a world that demands we turn even our leisure into another form of labor.

During one of these stupid walks last spring, I found myself staring at a dandelion growing through a crack in the asphalt outside a Starbucks. Most people would see a weed. I saw an act of ontological defiance. That dandelion had no business existing. The odds were literally stacked against it in layers of petroleum-based pavement. And yet there it was, yellow as a small sun, insisting on its right to bloom. I sat on the curb and cried. Not because it was beautiful (though it was), but because its existence felt like a form of grace. Unasked for. Unmerited. Insistent.

I’ve come to believe that wonder is not something we *have*. It’s something we *recover*. We’re born with it—every child is a wonder factory—but modernity systematically extracts it from us. First through education that privileges standardized testing over staring out windows. Then through work that measures us in metrics that have nothing to do with aliveness. Finally through the digital architectures that train us to outsource our attention to algorithms that understand us better than we understand ourselves.

The recovery of wonder requires what the poet John Keats called “negative capability”—the ability to remain in uncertainties, mysteries, and doubts without any irritable reaching after fact and reason. This is harder than it sounds. Our brains have been optimized for pattern recognition and threat detection, not for sitting with the ungraspable. The entire economy of the attention industry is built on the opposite impulse: the promise that everything can be known, categorized, ranked, reviewed, and ultimately consumed.

But some things resist consumption. 

Consider the night sky. Not the one you see in National Geographic or through an app that labels every constellation for you. I mean the actual night sky above your head when you’re far enough from city lights that the Milky Way becomes visible. That smeared band of light isn’t just beautiful—it’s *us*. Every atom in your body was forged in stars that died before our sun was born. When you look up at night, you’re not just seeing the universe. You’re seeing your own family album written in nuclear fire.

I remember the first time I really *saw* the Milky Way. I was in the high desert of New Mexico during a new moon. The sky wasn’t just dark—it was *alive*. There were so many stars that the darkness itself seemed perforated, like black velvet held up to a thousand tiny lights. For the first time in my life, I understood viscerally what it means to be “star stuff.” Not as an abstract Carl Sagan concept, but as a felt reality that made my chest feel too small for what was happening inside it.

That experience ruined me for lesser wonders. Not in the sense that smaller things stopped mattering, but in the sense that I could no longer pretend that wonder was optional. Once you’ve felt the scale of your own existence against 13.8 billion years of cosmic evolution, the idea of reducing life to a series of optimized experiences feels almost obscene.

This brings me to what I’ve started calling “the blasphemy of enough.”
