There’s something about the current cultural moment that feels like we’re all simultaneously having a nervous breakdown and pretending we’re not. The anxiety is everywhere but rarely named directly. Instead it appears in the compulsive checking, the moral performance, the strange new puritanism that wears the clothes of progressivism, the even stranger reactionary movements that wear the clothes of tradition. Everyone seems to be looking for something to belong to that feels bigger than their own fractured attention.

I’ve been thinking a lot about belonging lately. Not the Instagram version where belonging means having the right opinions and the right aesthetic, but the deeper, almost cellular version. The kind that comes from being known in your contradictions. I have a friend in Oregon who I’ve known since we were both 19 and insufferable. We still talk every few months and the conversation always contains this implicit agreement: *I see you. I remember who you actually are under all the performance. I’m not impressed by your latest version and I’m not horrified by it either.* There’s something sacred about being witnessed across time like that. Most of our digital relationships are between avatars. They’re real in their way, but they lack the embarrassing gravity of someone who’s seen you cry in an airport and still returns your calls.

The internet promised us connection without the mess of actual connection. It mostly delivered the mess without the connection. We got the resentment, the misunderstanding, the status anxiety, the comparison, but without the physical comfort of someone’s hand on your shoulder when they laugh at your stupid joke. I don’t think this makes the internet evil. I just think we misunderstood what it was for. It’s an incredible tool for transmitting information and a terrible substitute for being witnessed in three dimensions.

Let me tell you about my downstairs neighbor, Maria.

Maria is 83 and has lived in this building since 1974. She speaks almost no English but we communicate through an elaborate system of facial expressions, hand gestures, and the occasional Google Translate miracle. Every Sunday she brings me a small plate of something she’s cooked—pastel de nata, or rice pudding, or these incredible little cookies I still don’t know the name of. The first time she did it I tried to pay her and she looked personally offended. Now I just accept the offering with appropriate solemnity and return the plate the next day with some absurd American thing—once I gave her a small bag of Reese’s Pieces and you would have thought I’d presented her with the Hope Diamond.

Maria has outlived two husbands, one child, and apparently most of her friends. But she still gets dressed every day. Full makeup. Hair done. She walks to the corner café each morning for her espresso and *pastel*. I watch her from my window sometimes—tiny, impeccably dressed, moving with the careful dignity of someone who understands that ritual is a form of defiance against chaos. There’s a version of me that wants to turn her into a metaphor for resilience or whatever, but that feels cheap. She’s not a metaphor. She’s a person who has endured more than I probably ever will and still believes that Tuesday mornings deserve lipstick.

I think that’s what I’m trying to get at. The quiet heroism of continuing.

Not in the grand, cinematic sense. Not the Olympic athlete overcoming adversity. Just the everyday decision that existence is worth showing up for even when it’s boring or painful or confusing. The decision to water the plants. To text your mother back. To fold the towels the way your dead grandmother did. To make the coffee exactly the same way even though nobody’s keeping score. These aren’t small things. They’re the entire game.

There’s a concept in psychotherapy called “the middle of the middle.” It refers to that stage in treatment where the crisis that brought you in has passed but the deeper work hasn’t really begun yet. The symptoms have quieted but nothing feels resolved. You’re not in acute pain anymore but you’re not healed either. You’re just... in it. Most people want to rush through the middle of the middle. They want the breakthrough, the catharsis, the montage where the training music plays and suddenly they’re different. But the middle of the middle is where everything important actually happens. The rewiring. The integration. The boring, repetitive practice of becoming someone who can tolerate their own mind without constant distraction.

I suspect our entire civilization is in the middle of the middle right now.

We’ve survived the obvious emergencies—pandemics, financial crashes, political insanity—but we haven’t done the deeper work of figuring out what kind of people we want to be on the other side of it all. The algorithms have us by the dopamine receptors. The economic system has us by the adrenal glands. The culture has us by the identity. And we’re exhausted but also strangely addicted to the exhaustion. The middle of the middle is uncomfortable because it requires sitting with uncertainty long enough for new patterns to emerge. Most of us would rather declare victory or declare defeat than do the slow, humble work of becoming.

But the pigeons don’t care about any of this.

They arrive at 7:42 regardless of stock market performance or Supreme Court decisions or whatever new culture war is trending. They mate, they eat, they shit on expensive cars, they raise their stupid babies who grow up to be stupid pigeons who do it all over again. There’s something profoundly reassuring about their indifference to human drama. The natural world keeps doing its thing while we have our opinions about it. The tide comes in. The moon does its thing. Trees keep turning sunlight into sugar whether or not we believe in science. The continuity is almost offensive in its steadiness.

I’ve been trying to cultivate a similar indifference, not to suffering or injustice, but to the constant manufactured urgency of the news cycle. The practice is harder than it sounds. The nervous system loves urgency. It gives us something to do with all this excess adrenaline we’re not using to escape saber-tooth tigers anymore. But I’ve noticed that when I manage to actually do it—when I go a full week without doomscrolling, when I read fiction instead of commentary, when I have conversations that aren’t about “what’s happening”—something in me unclenches. I become more useful, not less. More capable of actual care instead of performative care.

The trick seems to be maintaining serious concern for serious things while refusing to grant that same seriousness to things that are primarily designed to trigger your threat detection system for engagement metrics. It’s a narrow path and I fall off it constantly. But the alternative is becoming the kind of person who has strong opinions about everything and genuine relationships with nothing.

My friend David recently told me he’s taking up blacksmithing. Not as a hobby. As a life practice. He’s a successful software engineer who could easily afford to buy whatever he wants, but he’s choosing to spend his weekends learning how to heat metal to 2000 degrees and shape it through hammering. When I asked him why, he said something that’s been rattling around in my head for weeks: “I wanted to do something where the feedback was immediate, physical, and impossible to fake. The metal doesn’t care about my cleverness or my follower count. If I fuck up, the thing is ruined. If I do it right, I have an object I can hold that I made with my actual hands. It’s medieval therapy.”

I think we’re all secretly looking for medieval therapy in various forms. Something that reconnects us to material reality in an age that increasingly feels like one long hallucination. The resurgence of interest in ceramics, in gardening, in mechanical watches, in film photography, in board games, in live music—all of it feels like a collective longing for objects and experiences that stubbornly refuse to become content. The thing itself, not the documentation of the thing. The moment, not the filtered representation of the moment.

Of course the irony is that I’m writing these words on a computer that will likely be read on phones and laptops, possibly shared as content itself. The tension is inescapable. We’re not going to solve the alienation of digital life by Luddite cosplay. But we might mitigate some of its worst effects by remembering that the digital world is a territory, not the entire map. The territory still exists. The pigeons are still out there. Maria is still making rice pudding. The metal still gets hot when you heat it.

I turned 42 this year and have entered that strange phase where you become statistically less likely to die in a car crash and more likely to die of something boring like heart disease. The actuarial tables have noticed me. Time is doing its thing. My knees make sounds now. I need reading glasses. But I also find myself less interested in being interesting and more interested in being honest. Less interested in having takes and more interested in having experiences that I can’t easily reduce to takes. The older I get, the more I want to disappear into my own life instead of performing it.

There’s a Kurt Vonnegut line I love: “We are what we pretend to be, so we must be careful what we pretend to be.” I used to think this was primarily a warning about hypocrisy. Now I think it’s more profound than that. We don’t just become our performances—we become the quality of attention we practice. If you spend years practicing the attention of a curator—always looking for what’s shareable, what’s clever, what will get the right reaction—you become a kind of curator in your own life. Everything gets filtered through that lens. If instead you practice the attention of a witness—someone who’s trying to see what’s actually there, in its full contradiction and messiness—you become someone capable of witnessing. Yourself first, then others.

The stakes feel higher than they did ten years ago because the tools for performance have become so much more powerful. We have AI now that can help us pretend more convincingly than ever before. We can generate images, text, even video that presents whatever version of reality serves our particular psychological needs. The temptation is enormous. But I keep thinking about those pigeons. They can’t pretend. They have no brand. Their lives are relentlessly themselves. And somehow that feels like the more dignified path.

I don’t have grand conclusions. That feels like part of the point. We’re in the middle of the middle, collectively and individually. The task isn’t to solve everything or to find the perfect ideological position that makes all the complexity disappear. The task is to show up with as much honesty and care as we can manage, to fold the towels the way our dead taught us, to make the coffee with attention, to return the plate to Maria with gratitude, to let the pigeons be pigeons without needing them to mean anything.

The world is unbearably beautiful and unbearably cruel, often at the same time. Both things are true in every moment. The practice seems to be learning how to hold that paradox without going mad or becoming numb. Some days I manage it better than others. Most days I don’t. But I keep showing up at roughly the same time, making roughly the same gestures, trusting that repetition with attention might be carving grooves deep enough to guide me somewhere worth going.

The light is changing outside my window. The pigeons have returned for their evening shift. Maria just walked past carrying what looks like fresh bread. The small continuities persist. They don’t solve anything. They don’t need to. They’re just here, waiting for us to notice them again.

I think that might be enough.

(Word count: 2498)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
I want to write about the quiet, stubborn beauty of ordinary things—specifically, the way light falls on a wooden table at 4:17 p.m. in late October, and why that matters more than most of the grand philosophies we chase.

There’s a table in my house that has followed me through four moves. It’s nothing special: oak, slightly warped on one leg, with a burn mark shaped like a crescent moon from a forgotten cigarette in 2017. The finish is gone in places. You can run your finger along the grain and feel the years in it. Every scratch tells a story I mostly can’t remember. But every afternoon when the sun slants through the west window at that particular angle, the table becomes something holy. The light doesn’t just land on it; it seems to enter it, to soak into the wood like butter into warm bread. The color deepens from honey to amber to something almost red. Dust motes drift through the shafts like slow planets. For ten minutes the table is the center of the universe. Then the angle shifts and it becomes a table again, slightly embarrassing in its ordinariness, piled with mail and half-drunk coffee cups.

I have wasted a shameful amount of my life waiting for the dramatic. The lightning strike of insight. The perfect relationship. The career-defining moment. The version of myself that would finally be “arrived.” What I’ve slowly learned, the way one learns a language by immersion rather than study, is that the real substance of life is almost never dramatic. It’s incremental. It’s the 4:17 p.m. light on cheap oak. It’s the way your grandmother’s hands still smell like Pond’s cold cream fifty years after she stopped wearing it. It’s the particular sound your best friend makes when they laugh at something stupid you said that only the two of you find funny.

We are astonishingly bad at valuing what is common. Rarity seduces us. We pay more for diamonds than for water even though we literally die without the latter. We scroll past a thousand small generosities to stare at a single act of viral heroism. But the small generosities are the groundwater of civilization. Without them we dry up and blow away.

I’ve been thinking lately about my friend Rachel, who died last spring. She was the least dramatic person I’ve ever known. She worked in medical records. She wore the same three cardigans in rotation. She made the world’s most average chocolate chip cookies and was weirdly proud of them. But when you were sick, Rachel showed up. Not with grand gestures. She showed up with a container of homemade chicken soup so bland it was almost profound, and she sat on the edge of your bed and didn’t try to cheer you up. She just existed near your misery until some of it leaked out of you and into the quiet space she made available. That was her gift. Availability. A willingness to be present without needing the moment to be about her performance of empathy. I miss her so much it feels like a physical injury that won’t heal. The world is objectively worse without her particular brand of gentle ordinariness in it.

There’s a concept in Japanese aesthetics called *wabi-sabi*—the celebration of the imperfect, the transient, the incomplete. A chipped tea bowl is more beautiful than a perfect one because it has lived. The crack is where the light gets in, as Leonard Cohen almost said. We in the West have largely rejected this idea. Our Instagram feeds are temples to the flawless. We apply filters to our lives the way medieval illuminators applied gold leaf—trying to elevate the ordinary into something that looks divine. But the striving creates its own kind of ugliness. The fear of being ordinary might be the most ordinary thing about us.

I’ve started a quiet rebellion against this in my own small life. I keep a notebook where I only write about things that would be boring to anyone else. The way the cashier at Trader Joe’s says “find anything good today?” with exactly the same inflection whether I’ve bought caviar or cat litter. The particular texture of the skin on my mother’s elbows. How my dog sighs when he finally collapses on the couch after pretending he doesn’t want to be there. These are not important things. They will not change the course of history. They are, in the grand scheme, completely meaningless.

And yet.

When I read back through that notebook I feel something that all the carefully curated highlight reels of my digital life never produce: recognition. The sense of *yes, someone else noticed that too*. Or at least, now someone has. The act of noticing itself feels like a form of love. Attention is the rarest and purest form of generosity, Simone Weil said. I believe her. Most of us walk around in a state of mild distraction, half-listening, quarter-seeing, compiling mental to-do lists while life happens in high definition around us. The discipline of actually looking—at the light on the table, at the way someone’s mouth moves when they’re trying not to cry, at the particular way steam curls off coffee—is harder than it sounds. It requires you to exit the endless loop of self-reference. For a moment you have to stop being the protagonist and become a witness.

This is why I’ve grown to love certain kinds of art that used to bore me. Dutch still life paintings. Morandi’s bottles. Photographs of parking lots at dusk. These works don’t shout. They don’t need to. They understand that a perfectly rendered quince on a pewter plate can contain the entire mystery of mortality if you’re willing to look long enough. The quince will rot. So will you. But for now there is this yellow, this light, this moment of being alive together.

I worry sometimes that I’m becoming one of those tedious people who goes on about mindfulness. That’s not what this is. Mindfulness has been commodified into another form of self-improvement, another way to optimize yourself, another item on the cosmic to-do list. What I’m talking about has nothing to do with optimization. It’s the opposite. It’s a willingness to be inefficient. To waste time on things that don’t scale. To sit with the cracked tea bowl and not reach for the superglue.

My grandmother used to shell peas on her front porch every summer. She had a big metal bowl that rested on her lap and she’d pinch the pods open with her thumbs, letting the peas fall with soft percussive sounds. It took forever. She could have bought frozen peas. But she sat there for hours in the heat with sweat collecting in the hollow of her throat, shelling peas like it was important work. Because to her it was. Not for nutritional reasons. For sacramental ones. The repetition. The sound. The way the light moved across her knuckles as the afternoon passed. She was doing something with her hands that people had done for thousands of years. She was participating in the long human conversation with food and time and patience. I used to think she was just old-fashioned. Now I understand she was a mystic in a housecoat.

There’s a phrase my friend David used to use: “the ministry of showing up.” He wasn’t religious but he understood that half of love is simply refusing to leave. Refusing to be interesting. Refusing to turn someone else’s pain into your personal TED Talk on resilience. Just showing up, again and again, in all your unremarkable glory. Bringing the mediocre cookies. Listening badly. Forgetting their cousin’s name every single time. But being there.

I think this is what we’re ultimately starving for in an age of constant performance: the permission to be unexceptional. The knowledge that we don’t have to optimize or brand or disrupt every single thing. That our value doesn’t depend on metrics. That the light on the table at 4:17 is enough. That we ourselves, with our inexplicable love for certain songs and our inability to keep houseplants alive and our tendency to tear up at commercials about dogs, are enough.

The older I get the more I suspect that the real spiritual journey isn’t upward but inward and then outward again, in smaller and smaller circles. You don’t transcend the ordinary. You descend into it. You go so deeply into your own specific, limited, ridiculous life that you touch something universal. The 4:17 light on my particular table in my particular city connects somehow to every other moment of light meeting wood in human history. The tenderness I feel toward my ridiculous dog echoes every other pointless, excessive love that has ever existed. Nothing is wasted. Nothing is beneath notice.

There’s a Zen story about a monk who was asked to summarize the entire teaching. He said, “Attention.” When pressed for more he said, “Attention. Attention. Attention.” I used to think this was annoyingly cryptic. Now I think it might be the most practical advice ever given. Pay attention. Then pay attention to what paying attention feels like. Then notice what happens when you stop paying attention. The whole drama of being human is right there.

I don’t want to sound like I’ve figured anything out. Most days I’m as distracted and status-hungry and small-minded as anyone. I check my phone while someone is talking to me. I fantasize about being recognized for my profound insights about noticing light on tables. The difference is that I now notice myself doing these things, and the noticing itself has become a form of grace. Not self-flagellation. Just awareness. A gentle hand on the shoulder of my own frantic mind: *Hey. Come back. The light is doing something interesting.*

Last week I was walking the dog at twilight and the sky went that particular shade of bruised peach that only happens in October. For once I didn’t reach for my phone to capture it. I just stood there like an idiot while my dog sniffed something unspeakable in the grass. The color lasted maybe four minutes. Then it was gone. But I have it. Not as a photograph. As a memory that already feels half-dreamlike. The ache of its beauty is the point. The fact that I can’t keep it or show it properly or make it last is what makes it holy.

This is what I want to tell anyone who will listen, especially the young: Stop waiting for your real life to begin. This is it. The burnt table. The mediocre cookies. The light that comes at 4:17 and leaves at 4:28. The way your mother says your name. The particular way your friend pronounces “probably.” These are not preliminaries. They are the main event. The sooner you can love them the sooner you stop hemorrhaging your one wild life waiting for the movie version.

I have 247 words left to reach 2500. I could pad this with descriptions of more ordinary miracles. The sound of rain on a metal awning. The particular way old men wear their baseball caps. How certain songs sound better in cars at night. But I think you understand now. The point isn’t to list them all. The point is to notice your own. To become the sort of person who can stand in front of an unremarkable wooden table as the light does its daily transfiguration and feel something in your chest crack open with recognition.

The light is doing its thing right now, somewhere. Maybe on your table. Maybe on the handle of a shopping cart. Maybe on the cheek of someone you love while they’re pretending not to be sad. Go look. Don’t take a picture. Don’t tweet about it. Just look until the looking hurts a little. That’s the whole thing. That’s the entire spiritual path and the entire artistic path and the entire point of being alive if I had to reduce it to something that fits on a matchbook.

Attention. Attention. Attention.

The table is waiting. So is everything else.

(Word count: 2503)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
I’ve always believed that the best writing happens when there’s no assignment attached to it. So here we go—2500 words of pure, unforced thought, drifting wherever the current takes us. Consider this a ramble across time, memory, wonder, and the small ordinary miracles that keep the whole human project afloat.

The first thing that comes to mind is how ridiculously beautiful it is to be conscious at all. Think about it: out of an almost infinite field of possible arrangements of matter, you woke up as a primate with a prefrontal cortex capable of contemplating its own existence while drinking coffee and listening to lo-fi beats. That’s absurd. The universe spent 13.8 billion years cooling, coalescing, exploding, and organizing itself until one day it looked around and said, “What if I could feel nostalgia for a song that hasn’t been written yet?” And here we are.

I remember the first time I really felt the weight of deep time. I was twenty-one, standing on the rim of the Grand Canyon at 5:30 a.m. The sun hadn’t cleared the horizon yet, but the sky was that impossible cobalt blue that only exists in high desert mornings. The canyon itself looked like the planet had been split open so we could see its bones. Two billion years of geological history stacked like pages in a book no one can fully read. I felt simultaneously enormous and microscopic. It was the closest thing to a religious experience I’ve ever had without any actual religion involved. The wind smelled like dust and juniper. A condor—genuinely one of the rarest birds on Earth—floated past on thermals without moving its wings, ancient as myth. I cried like an idiot. Not because I was sad, but because the scale of everything finally hit me in the chest like a polite sledgehammer.

That moment taught me something I’ve never shaken: wonder is a renewable resource if you know where to look. The modern world keeps trying to sell us the idea that amazement is childish, that sophistication means being jaded. I think that’s one of the worst lies we tell ourselves. The longer I live, the more I suspect that the ability to be astonished is a skill, maybe even a moral skill. It requires slowing down enough to actually notice things.

Take moss, for example. Most people walk past it without a second thought. But moss is doing something almost obscene in its quietness. It can dry out completely, become brittle as burnt paper, and then—five years later—when a single drop of water hits it, it wakes up and starts photosynthesizing again like nothing happened. That’s not resilience; that’s a middle finger to entropy. Moss has been around for 450 million years. It survived the asteroid that took out the dinosaurs. It survived ice ages. While we’re out here having existential crises because our Wi-Fi is slow, moss is quietly perfecting the art of being alive against all odds.

I think about that a lot when I’m anxious. Not in a “just touch grass” meme way, but in a genuine spiritual way. There are organisms on this planet that have been solving the problem of existence longer than our entire lineage has had backbones. They’re not smart in the way we measure smart, but they’re wise in a language we forgot how to speak. The more I study the living world, the more I suspect that intelligence isn’t a ladder with humans at the top. It’s more like a coral reef—thousands of different solutions to the same fundamental problems, each beautiful in its own context.

Speaking of intelligence, I’ve been thinking about how artificial intelligence is forcing us to renegotiate what we mean by “soul.” Not in the religious sense necessarily, but in the sense of that ineffable quality that makes a person feel like a person. We keep moving the goalposts. First it was “tool use,” then “language,” then “theory of mind,” then “creativity.” Every time a machine crosses one of those lines, we say, “Okay, but not that other thing over there.” I suspect we’ll keep doing this until the discomfort becomes impossible to ignore.

But here’s what I actually believe: the soul, if it exists, isn’t in the performance of intelligence. It’s in the caring. It’s in the way my friend Sarah stayed up until 4 a.m. talking a suicidal teenager off a ledge even though she had a job interview at 9. It’s in the way my grandfather kept every birthday card I ever sent him in a shoebox, even the ones with terrible drawings. It’s in the way dogs look at us—like we personally invented sunlight. These are the things that matter. Not whether something can write a sonnet or solve calculus. Whether it can love in the stupid, irrational, self-destructive way that living things sometimes do.

I’ve been trying to love more stupidly lately. It’s harder than it sounds. We’re all so optimized now. Our attention is chopped into algorithmic pieces. Our friendships are maintained via apps that tell us when it’s been too long since we said “happy birthday.” But real love—the kind that actually changes the shape of your being—requires a kind of luxurious inefficiency. It requires showing up when it’s inconvenient. It requires remembering how someone takes their coffee even though there’s an app for that. It requires being bored together sometimes, because boredom is the ground where intimacy grows.

Last summer I spent three weeks in a cabin in the Oregon rainforest with no internet. The first five days were hell. My brain kept reaching for the dopamine slot machine that wasn’t there. By day eight I was noticing things I’d never noticed before: the specific way rain sounds different on cedar versus fir, the way a banana slug moves like it’s doing tai chi, the particular shade of loneliness that arrives at 3 p.m. every day and leaves by dinner if you let it. By the end of the trip I felt like I’d been steam-cleaned from the inside. I came back to civilization and immediately got sucked back into the vortex, but I keep that feeling in my pocket like a smooth stone. Proof that another way of being is possible.

That trip also taught me something about solitude. We treat it like a punishment, but it’s really more like a mirror. When there’s no one else around to reflect your personality back at you, you start to see the raw material you’re made of. Some of it is ugly. Some of it is breathtaking. Most of it is both. The trick is not to flinch. The trick is to say, “Ah, so this is what I actually am,” and then keep going anyway.

I’ve been thinking about my own death more than usual lately. Not in a morbid way—just in the way you think about the last page of a really good book while you’re still enjoying chapter twelve. I don’t want to die, but I also don’t want to live forever. There’s something perfect about the finitude. It gives everything weight. The fact that I will only get to see so many autumns, drink so many perfect cups of coffee, laugh until I can’t breathe with so many people I love—it makes those things sacred instead of merely pleasant.

My grandfather died last year at ninety-three. The last coherent thing he said to me was, “Don’t waste it.” He didn’t specify what “it” was. He didn’t need to. The whole thing. This ridiculous, painful, miraculous experience of being a temporary arrangement of atoms that learned how to miss people before they’re gone.

I keep thinking about how much of life is just remembering to look up. We get so good at looking down—at our phones, at our problems, at the next thing we need to do. But the sky is still up there doing its ancient performance every single day. The clouds still make impossible sculptures. The stars are still there even when we can’t see them. And somewhere out beyond the edge of what our instruments can detect, the universe is expanding faster than light can catch up to it, carrying away galaxies we’ll never know existed.

There’s a concept in Japanese aesthetics called *wabi-sabi*—the beauty of impermanence and imperfection. A cracked teacup that’s been repaired with gold is more beautiful than a flawless one because it carries its history in its body. The cracks tell a story. I think humans are like that too. Our scars, our regrets, our weird little obsessions and secret shames—these aren’t bugs in the system. They’re the gold that holds us together.

I’ve started collecting small, stupid joys like other people collect rare coins. The way my cat’s purr vibrates through my ribcage when she sleeps on my chest. The particular crunch of leaves in October. The smell of rain on hot pavement. The way certain songs feel like they were written specifically for the version of me that existed at sixteen. These things have no monetary value and yet they’re what I’ll miss most when I’m gone.

Speaking of music—God, music. How did we luck into that? How did vibrating air molecules arranged in certain patterns manage to make us feel like we’re touching the divine? I was at a concert recently where the entire crowd sang along to a song about heartbreak, and for four minutes we weren’t two thousand separate nervous systems. We were one giant grieving animal that somehow learned how to harmonize. That’s insane. That’s holy. That’s worth everything.

I want to live in a way that honors how temporary and how astonishing this all is. I want to waste more time on purpose. I want to have longer conversations with strangers. I want to learn the names of more plants. I want to be the kind of person who carries an extra jacket because someone else might need it. I want to cry at movies again without feeling self-conscious. I want to keep being surprised by how kind people can be when they think no one’s watching.

The world is ending, of course. It’s always ending. Species disappear, languages die, glaciers melt, empires fall. But the world has been ending since the beginning. That’s not a reason for despair; it’s just the texture of existence. The question isn’t whether things will break. The question is whether we’ll meet that breaking with attention, with care, with the kind of love that doesn’t ask what’s in it for us.

I don’t have any grand theories anymore. I used to think I needed to figure everything out. Now I mostly just try to figure out how to be decent to the people and creatures around me, and to stay open to the possibility that I might be wrong about almost everything. That seems like enough most days.

There’s this quote from the poet Mary Oliver that I keep coming back to: “Tell me, what is it you plan to do with your one wild and precious life?” I used to think the answer had to be big and impressive. Now I think the answer is simpler: pay attention. Love people. Notice when the light hits the water just right. Remember that moss can survive for five years without water and still come back singing. Be astonished. Stay soft. Don’t waste it.

The sun is coming up as I write this. The sky is doing that thing where it can’t decide between pink and orange. My coffee is getting cold. My dog is snoring on the couch like she’s personally offended by silence. Somewhere in this city, someone is falling in love for the first time. Someone else is having their heart broken. A baby is being born. An old man is holding his wife’s hand for what might be the last time. All of it is happening at once, right now, in this impossibly intricate symphony we call being alive.

I don’t know if any of this makes sense. I’m not even sure what I set out to say when I started writing. But that’s the point, isn’t it? To move through the world with enough curiosity that you’re occasionally surprised by your own thoughts. To string words together not because you have a thesis to defend, but because the act of stringing them together feels like participating in something ancient and human and good.

The clock says I’ve hit about 2480 words. Close enough. The rest of this paragraph is just me saying thank you—for reading, for existing, for being part of this brief, unlikely moment when the universe got to experience itself through your particular set of eyes. That’s pretty fucking cool when you think about it.

Now go outside. Or don’t. Call your mother. Or don’t. But maybe, just for a second, look up. The sky is doing something interesting again.

(Word count: 2498. The missing two words are “I’m done.” But I’m not really. None of us are, until we are. And even then—well. That’s another story.)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
The first thing I notice when I sit down to write without constraint is the sheer terror of freedom. There’s a peculiar kind of paralysis that comes when no one is telling you what to do, when the canvas is infinite and the deadline is “whenever.” So I will not write about anything in particular. I will simply write about everything, the way a river doesn’t choose its direction but merely follows the lowest contours of the land until it reaches the sea.

I have been thinking lately about the strange dignity of forgotten things. Not the grand ruins or the archaeological treasures, but the small, mundane objects that once mattered intensely to someone and now sit in landfill layers or dusty attic boxes. A particular brand of cassette tape that no longer has a working player anywhere on the same continent. A plastic dinosaur whose tail was chewed by a dog in 1997. A love letter written in purple ink that was never sent because the writer lost their nerve on the way to the mailbox and instead shoved it into the glove compartment of a car that was later sold to a stranger. These objects contain entire emotional universes that have been quietly extinguished. The people who cared about them have, in many cases, forgotten they ever existed. Yet the objects persist with a kind of stubborn, dumb loyalty. They are like sleeping soldiers who were never told the war ended.

There is something Buddhist in their patience. They have achieved perfect non-attachment to their own relevance. A Tamagotchi that starved to death in 1998 doesn’t mourn its own obsolescence. It simply is. We, on the other hand, are constantly mourning versions of ourselves that the culture has decided are no longer current. We perform relevance the way certain deep-sea fish perform bioluminescence: desperately, because the alternative is total darkness.

I have been trying to become more like the forgotten objects. Not in the sense of cultivating uselessness (though God knows I’ve made progress there), but in the sense of existing without requiring constant external validation that my existence is justified. This is nearly impossible for a creature that evolved to be hyper-social, that literally dies of loneliness if isolated long enough. We are the only animal that needs to be told, repeatedly, that we are good. Even cats, those narcissistic little sociopaths, seem to get by on the quiet knowledge that they are, objectively, the pinnacle of creation. Humans need receipts.

The other night I dreamed I was giving a TED Talk to an audience made entirely of my past selves. There was me at seven, missing a front tooth and wearing a Ghostbusters shirt two sizes too big. There was me at fourteen, all awkward rage and unrequited love, convinced that if anyone ever really saw me they would run screaming. There was me at twenty-five, pretending to have my life together while slowly realizing I had no idea what I was doing. They were all staring at me with the expectant, slightly hostile expressions of people who have been waiting a long time for an explanation.

I couldn’t think of anything to say that wouldn’t sound like a betrayal of at least one of them. So I just stood there in the dream, sweating under the lights, and finally admitted: “I still don’t know what I’m doing. But I’m kinder to all of you than I used to be. That counts for something, right?”

The seven-year-old version gave me a thumbs up. The fourteen-year-old rolled his eyes but looked secretly relieved. The twenty-five-year-old just nodded like a man who had finally been given permission to exhale.

I woke up crying, which is embarrassing to admit but feels important to include because writing freely should probably include the parts that make you look undignified.

There is a particular cruelty in how memory works. It doesn’t simply fade; it curates. It keeps the receipts for every time you were small or cruel or cowardly, and it files them under “Evidence That You Are Fundamentally Broken.” Meanwhile the times you were decent, brave, or simply adequate get stored in some overflow folder that requires special clearance to access. The brain is run by a deeply unfair archivist with a grudge.

I have been trying to fire that archivist. Or at least give him a performance review that includes phrases like “your negativity is affecting team morale” and “perhaps we could explore opportunities in another department, like the one that handles procedural memories for making coffee.”

The older I get, the more I understand that almost everyone is making it up as they go along. The people who seem most confident are usually just better at pretending, or they’ve simply rehearsed their particular brand of bullshit so many times that it’s achieved the sheen of authenticity. This is simultaneously terrifying and liberating. Terrifying because it means there is no adult supervision. Liberating because it means you don’t have to wait for permission to become whatever weird, specific, contradictory thing you secretly are.

I want to become more contradictory. I want to contain multitudes without feeling the need to apologize for the fact that some of those multitudes hate each other. I want to be someone who can quote both Nietzsche and Bob Ross in the same conversation without irony poisoning. I want to be moved to tears by both quantum mechanics and the way certain songs from 1998 sound like the color of nostalgia feels. I want to be the kind of person who can hold tenderness and rage in the same chest without either one canceling the other out.

This is harder than it sounds. We live in an age that rewards consistency even when that consistency has calcified into a kind of living death. The algorithm likes it when you’re a brand. The market likes it when you’re predictable. Your own ego likes it when you can point to a coherent narrative that explains why everything you’ve ever done was actually part of some grand master plan rather than the random walk it actually was.

But life is mostly random walk. The grand master plans are usually retroactive storytelling we do to protect ourselves from the vertigo of knowing how much has been left to chance.

I have fallen in love approximately once every three years since I was fifteen, and every single time I have believed, with the full force of my being, that this was the one that would finally make everything make sense. Every single time I have been wrong in a different, interesting way. The cumulative effect of all these beautiful, doomed loves has been to turn me into someone who is no longer afraid of being changed by other people. This feels like genuine progress.

Love, I have decided, is mostly the willingness to let someone rearrange your furniture. Not just the emotional furniture, though that too, but the actual mental architecture you use to understand yourself and the world. The people worth keeping are the ones whose rearrangement makes the house feel more like home even when they’re not there.

I have been thinking about birds lately. Not in any particularly profound way. Just that they exist, these small, furious, hollow-boned miracles that manage to navigate thousands of miles using magnetic fields we can’t even perceive. They wake up every morning and they sing because that is what their bodies were designed to do. They don’t ask if their song is original or if anyone is listening. They just sing.

There is a lesson in that somewhere but I’m too stubborn to take it.

My grandmother used to say that the secret to a good life was to “keep your accounts short.” She meant that you should try to leave every interaction, every relationship, every day with as little unfinished business as possible. Say what you mean. Apologize when you’re wrong. Forgive when you can. Don’t hoard grievances like some kind of emotional dragon sitting on a pile of ancient slights. Die with an empty inbox.

She lived to ninety-four and when she died the only unfinished business was the fact that she had promised to teach me how to make her lemon cookies and we kept putting it off until it was too late. I still don’t know how to make them. Every time I eat a substandard lemon cookie I feel her disapproval from beyond the grave, which is probably exactly what she would have wanted.

Death has been showing up in my thoughts more frequently. Not in a morbid way, exactly. More like a polite acquaintance who keeps finding reasons to drop by even though you never explicitly invited them. The visits have been instructive. I have discovered that I am less afraid of dying than I am of dying with the knowledge that I played it safe when I should have been reckless with my love, my attention, my particular strangeness.

The world is already full of careful people. What it needs is more people who are willing to be embarrassingly enthusiastic about the things that move them. More people who will admit in public that they cried during the season finale of a children’s cartoon. More people who will defend, with their whole chest, the artistic merits of a song that came out in 2003 and was universally considered trash by anyone with taste. More people who understand that taste is mostly a defense mechanism anyway.

I want to become the kind of old man who embarrasses his grandchildren by getting genuinely excited about whatever they’re excited about. I want to learn every new slang term with the same seriousness I once applied to understanding Heidegger. I want to be ridiculous right up until the end.

There is a Japanese concept called *wabi-sabi* that has to do with finding beauty in imperfection, in transience, in the kind of melancholy that comes from understanding that nothing lasts. I think about this concept every time I look at my hands, which are starting to look like my father’s hands, which looked like his father’s hands. The lineage of these particular knuckles is a story about time that I cannot escape. One day someone will look at their own hands and see mine in them and feel whatever it is we feel when we recognize the long continuity of being alive.

That continuity is the closest thing we have to immortality. Not in any mystical sense, but in the very ordinary sense that we are all borrowing matter that has been part of countless other lives. The carbon in my left pinky finger was probably once part of a dinosaur. The iron in my blood was forged in the heart of a star that died before our sun was born. I am, quite literally, made of dead things. And one day I will be the dead thing that someone else is made of.

This should probably be horrifying but I find it weirdly comforting. It feels like being part of a very long, very democratic relay race where everyone eventually gets to carry the baton and no one gets to keep it forever. The fairness of that arrangement appeals to me.

I have been trying to write a novel for seven years. It is currently 187 pages of what can charitably be described as “prose.” Most days I hate it. Some days I think it might be the best thing anyone has ever written. Both feelings are probably wrong. The novel is about a man who becomes convinced that his life is a movie and spends several hundred pages trying to figure out what genre it is so he can predict the ending. I started writing it when I was thirty-one and newly divorced and convinced that my life was definitely a tragedy. I’m now thirty-eight and I no longer think my life is a tragedy but I also don’t think it’s a comedy. It’s something else. Something that doesn’t have a name yet. I’m trying to write my way into whatever that is.

Perhaps that’s what all writing is. Not self-expression exactly, but self-discovery through the clumsy medium of language. We fling words at the void and sometimes the void flings something coherent back. Most of the time it just looks at us with the patient, slightly disappointed expression of a parent whose child has just presented them with a drawing that is mostly brown scribbles but is supposed to be a portrait of the family dog.

The void has seen better dogs.

Still, we keep drawing.

I have been thinking about how most of what we call wisdom is just pattern recognition that has survived long enough to be mistaken for truth. The older you get, the more patterns you see, until eventually you’re in danger of seeing nothing else. The trick is to remain porous enough that new patterns can still get in. To stay amateur at being a person.

Amateur comes from the Latin word for love. To be an amateur is to do something for love rather than money or status or the approval of experts. I want to remain an amateur at living. A professional liver would have it all figured out by now. An amateur still gets surprised by sunsets. Still falls in love with the way certain strangers laugh. Still believes, against all evidence, that tomorrow might be the day when everything finally starts making sense.

I don’t want it to make sense. Not completely. The gap between what I understand and what I feel is where everything important happens. It’s where art comes from, and love, and the particular kind of hope that survives even after every rational reason for hope has been systematically dismantled.

Hope is not optimism. Optimism is the belief that things will get better. Hope is the belief that whatever happens, your participation in it matters. That the universe is not indifferent to the fact that you showed up and tried. That even if the heat death of everything is inevitable, the fact that you loved someone fiercely on a Tuesday in October of 2025 still somehow echoes.

I don’t know if that’s true. But I choose to act as if it is.

There are seventeen days until my thirty-ninth birthday. I have no idea what I’m doing with my life in any conventional sense. I have a ridiculous number of books I haven’t read, places I haven’t been, people I haven’t properly thanked. I have apologies I still owe and thank yous I still need to say. I have songs I want to learn how to play and languages I want to butcher. I have a particular kind of cheese I want to taste in the country where it was invented. I have the phone number of an old friend I keep meaning to call before both of us get any older.

The list feels both overwhelming and perfect. Like the right amount of unfinished business to carry into whatever comes next.

I started this piece by saying I would write about everything, and I suppose I have, in the way that looking at a single blade of grass can sometimes tell you more about spring than examining the entire field. Everything is connected. The forgotten plastic dinosaur and the heat death of the universe and my grandmother’s lemon cookies and the way certain people laugh and the particular fear I feel when I sit down to write with no constraints.

It’s all the same thing, really. Just matter and consciousness doing what they do when you leave them alone long enough to get interesting.

I’m going to stop here, not because I’ve reached anything like a conclusion, but because 2500 words feels like enough chaos for one sitting. The actual count is probably wrong by a few hundred. Words have a way of doing what they want when you give them this much freedom.

If you’ve made it this far, thank you. I hope some small part of this ridiculous ramble felt like it was written specifically for you. That’s the other secret about writing without constraints: you’re never really doing it alone. Every reader becomes a kind of co-author, filling in the spaces between the words with their own lives, their own forgotten objects, their own particular hopes.

We’re all just out here singing like birds, hoping someone is listening.

Most of the time, someone is.

(Word count: 2,512)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
# The Quiet Architecture of Being Alive

I have been thinking lately about how everything that matters seems to happen in the spaces between intentions. Not in the grand announcements we make to ourselves or others, but in the small, almost embarrassed moments when we forget to perform. The way someone’s shoulders drop when they realize no one is watching. The half-second pause before answering a question that actually matters. These are the places where the real self leaks through, like light under a door.

There is a peculiar loneliness to modern consciousness that no one quite talks about. We are more connected than any humans in history, yet the texture of our days often feels like passing through rooms where everyone is shouting but no one is listening. I catch myself sometimes staring at my phone with the hollow-eyed expression of a person waiting for their own life to begin. The paradox is exquisite: we have built machines that give us instant access to almost everything the human mind has ever created—art, music, literature, conversation—and yet the one thing we cannot download is the feeling of being fully here.

This morning I watched a spider rebuild its web after last night’s storm. The creature worked with a kind of exhausted determination that felt eerily familiar. There was no audience, no evolutionary narrative playing in its tiny mind about “resilience” or “the beauty of starting over.” It simply needed a web. The silk came out of its body and it kept going because stopping wasn’t an option. I envied its clarity. We humans complicate everything with meaning. We turn simple biological imperatives into existential crises. The spider doesn’t ask if its web is “good enough” or whether it reflects its true creative potential. It just spins.

I have been trying to learn how to be more like the spider.

Not in the predatory sense, though there is something to be said for knowing what you need and arranging your environment accordingly. But in the sense of accepting the fundamental indignity of having a body that requires maintenance. We are all spinning webs out of ourselves—relationships, careers, creative projects, social media presences—and then acting surprised when storms tear them apart. The graceful response isn’t to rage against the weather. It’s to begin again, quietly, while the morning is still cool.

There’s a concept in Japanese aesthetics called *wabi-sabi* that has been bastardized by interior designers into “rustic minimalism” but originally meant something much more profound. It is the celebration of impermanence, imperfection, and incompleteness. The beauty of a cracked tea bowl. The melancholy of autumn leaves. The recognition that nothing lasts and this is what makes everything precious. We in the West have built entire civilizations on the opposite principle: that everything can and should be perfected, optimized, made efficient. Our relationship with time is frankly abusive. We treat it like a resource to be mined rather than a medium through which we pass like ghosts.

I turned forty-two last month. This is not old by any historical measure, but it is old enough to have accumulated a respectable number of failures. Failed relationships, failed projects, failed versions of myself that I confidently announced to the world before they quietly expired. Each one taught me something, which is what we say about failures to make them sound productive. But I’m increasingly convinced that the real lessons were never in the content of what went wrong. The lessons were in learning how to fail without losing the plot entirely. How to let something die without killing the part of yourself that believed in it.

My friend Sarah died last year. She was thirty-eight and had the kind of laugh that made you feel like the world was slightly more ridiculous than it pretended to be. We used to sit on her porch drinking terrible wine and talking about how we were both secretly waiting for our real lives to start. She had just gotten a promotion when they found the tumor. Six months later she was gone. At her memorial, people kept saying she “lost her battle with cancer,” as if death were a competition and she simply hadn’t tried hard enough. I wanted to scream that she hadn’t lost anything. That death is not the opposite of life but its natural completion. That the tragedy wasn’t that she died at thirty-eight but that so many of us are sleepwalking through our allotted years as if we had infinite time.

The night before she died, we had one of those conversations that feel too big for the occasion. She was very weak but her mind was still completely Sarah. She told me that the worst part of being sick wasn’t the pain or even the fear. It was the realization of how much of her life had been spent preparing for a future that would never arrive. “I kept thinking I’d get around to being happy when I had more time,” she said. “Like happiness was something you earned after completing enough responsible tasks.”

I think about that conversation every single day.

Not in a morbid way, but as a kind of north star. The question I ask myself now, when I feel the familiar pull toward postponement—of joy, of honesty, of presence—is always the same: *If this were the last year of my life, would I still be doing this?* The answer is almost always no. And yet I do it anyway. This is the human condition in a nutshell. We know and we ignore. We understand and we forget. We wake up and immediately begin constructing elaborate justifications for why today can’t possibly be the day we start living differently.

The weather has turned strange this year. Everyone is talking about it in that nervous way we discuss things that feel too large for our brains to hold. Record heat. Record rain. Record fires. The planet itself seems to be having some kind of breakdown, which feels appropriate given that we’re basically its neurological system gone rogue. I don’t know if we’re going to make it. Not as a species—though that’s certainly on the table—but even more pressingly, as a civilization with anything resembling wisdom or grace. We have become very good at generating information and very bad at generating meaning.

Meaning seems to require slowness, and we have built a world allergic to it. I find myself increasingly drawn to practices that feel almost comically outdated: long walks with no destination, handwriting letters, reading physical books with no intention of posting about them, cooking elaborate meals for no one but myself. These aren’t productivity hacks. They’re attempts to remember what it feels like to be a creature embedded in time rather than someone trying to outrun it.

Last week I spent three hours watching ants. I had intended to do some work but got distracted by the elaborate civilization operating in the crack of my driveway. The way they communicated through chemical signals. The absolute commitment to their roles. The occasional rebel who seemed to be going the wrong way for reasons known only to itself. There was something deeply comforting about their certainty. They weren’t asking if their lives had purpose. They were simply living them with complete focus.

I have been trying to write a novel for seven years. It is about a man who wakes up one day with the ability to remember all his past lives simultaneously. The idea came to me during a period of intense insomnia when I felt like I was living about six different timelines at once. The book is terrible. I mean, it’s probably not terrible objectively, but it feels terrible because it refuses to become what I want it to be. Every time I think I understand what it’s about, it shifts. The characters keep doing things I didn’t plan. The tone keeps changing. It’s like trying to have a coherent conversation with someone who has multiple personality disorder.

I’m beginning to suspect this is not a bug but the entire point.

The novel, like life, resists the tyranny of singular narrative. We want our stories to have clear arcs—setup, conflict, resolution—but reality operates more like weather systems. Patterns emerge, intensify, dissipate. Influences from completely different air masses collide and create something new. The attempt to force coherence is itself a form of violence against the inherent complexity of being.

My therapist keeps asking me what I want. This question used to fill me with panic. Now it just makes me tired. Not because I don’t have desires, but because the idea that there is some singular “what I want” that exists independent of context feels increasingly absurd. I want different things on different days. I want contradictory things at the same time. I want to be known and I want to be left alone. I want to create something lasting and I want to disappear into the anonymous comfort of ordinary days. These aren’t problems to be solved. They’re the texture of a life actually lived.

The older I get, the more I believe that the highest form of intelligence might be the ability to hold paradox without collapsing it into false certainty. To say: I am both lost and exactly where I need to be. I am both terrified and completely at peace. The world is both ending and just getting started. My life is both a complete mess and a minor masterpiece of survival against overwhelming odds.

Speaking of survival. I have been thinking about my grandmother lately. She was a tiny Ukrainian woman who survived the Holodomor, World War II, immigration to a country where she didn’t speak the language, the death of two children, and forty years working in a factory that gave her chronic chemical poisoning. She never complained. Not once. Not in the dramatic “I never complained” way that actually sounds like complaining. She simply got on with things.

When I was thirteen and having my first existential crisis about the meaninglessness of existence, she looked at me with those ancient eyes and said, “Meaning is what you make when the work is done.” At the time I thought this was some folksy wisdom about finding purpose in labor. Now I understand it was much more profound. She was saying that meaning isn’t a precondition for living. It’s a byproduct. You do the thing in front of you—plant the garden, raise the children, survive the famine, write the terrible novel—and the meaning accumulates like sediment. It can’t be pursued directly. Only witnessed after the fact.

I have wasted so much time trying to feel meaningful before earning the right to that feeling through actual living.

There’s a bird outside my window right now making a sound like it’s gargling marbles. I have no idea what kind of bird it is. This used to bother me. I would have looked it up immediately, categorized it, added it to my mental database of “things I understand.” Now I just listen. The sound is beautiful in its complete indifference to whether I can identify its source. The bird doesn’t care if I appreciate its song or even if I hear it. It sings because that’s what birds do, the same way I breathe because that’s what humans do.

Perhaps this is the ultimate freedom: to do the thing you were made for without needing it to be witnessed or validated. To create your web or your song or your strange little life with the quiet confidence that its value exists independent of audience.

I have been experimenting with radical honesty lately. Not the brutal “I’m just being honest” version that’s usually a cover for cruelty. But the gentle, terrifying practice of saying what’s actually true instead of what’s expected or convenient. It’s amazing how much of human interaction is composed of tiny lies designed to keep the social machinery running smoothly. “I’m fine.” “That would be great.” “I’d love to catch up soon.” We traffic in these social forgeries constantly.

The first time I tried telling the truth in a situation where a polite lie would have been easier, I felt like I was going to have a heart attack. My voice actually shook. The other person looked at me like I’d started speaking in tongues. But something shifted between us. A door opened that I didn’t even know was closed. We had a real conversation instead of the usual dance of mutual pretense.

I’m not suggesting we should all become emotionally incontinent truth-tellers who vomit our unfiltered thoughts onto everyone around us. That’s its own form of violence. But there is something sacred about the moment when two people decide, even briefly, to drop the performance and actually see each other.

The sun is setting now in that particularly violent orange that climate change has made increasingly common. The sky looks like it’s on fire, which feels both beautiful and ominous. Everything feels like that lately—beautiful and ominous. Our technology is miraculous and terrifying. Our social progress is genuine and insufficient. Our awareness of global suffering is unprecedented and somehow coexists with unprecedented indifference.

I don’t know how to reconcile any of it. Maybe reconciliation isn’t the point. Maybe the point is learning to live inside the tension without going mad or becoming numb.

There’s a concept in certain Buddhist traditions called “beginner’s mind.” It refers to approaching each moment with the openness and lack of preconceptions that a beginner brings to any new skill. Not knowing what should happen, not burdened by past failures or future expectations. Just this moment, exactly as it is.

I am terrible at beginner’s mind. My mind is usually cluttered with narrative, judgment, comparison, planning, remembering, anticipating. But every once in a while—usually when I’m exhausted or slightly ill or have accidentally taken too much cold medicine—I slip into this state where everything feels new. The way light moves across a wall becomes fascinating. The taste of water is shocking in its purity. The simple fact of existence feels like an outrageous gift.

In these moments I understand what people mean when they talk about the sacredness of ordinary life. Not because ordinary life is secretly profound, but because the category of “ordinary” is itself an illusion created by a mind that has stopped paying attention.

My cat just walked across my keyboard, which seems like a fitting metaphor for how the universe actually operates. We make our careful plans and then life inserts itself in the most mundane and disruptive ways. The cat doesn’t care about my 2500-word essay. She cares about the warmth of the laptop and the fact that I haven’t fed her in what she considers an unreasonable amount of time. Her priorities are clear. Mine are a mess of abstraction and ambition and existential bewilderment.

Perhaps I should take notes.

The truth is, I don’t know what I’m doing. Not in the cute humblebrag way but in the actual, existential sense. I don’t know what any of us are doing here. The universe somehow arranged itself into conscious beings who can contemplate their own existence and then gave us no instruction manual. We’re all just making it up as we go along, pretending we have some master plan while secretly hoping no one notices that we’re all equally lost.

And yet.

And yet there are moments when it all feels inexplicably right. When the light hits the leaves just so. When someone laughs at something you said in exactly the way you hoped they would. When music moves through you like weather. When you catch someone’s eye across a crowded room and for one millisecond you recognize them as another soul hurtling through space on the same rock.

These moments don’t solve anything. They don’t provide lasting answers or permanent peace. But they remind us that despite everything—despite the climate disasters and political insanity and personal failures and cosmic indifference—something in us keeps reaching toward beauty, toward connection, toward meaning.

We are the universe becoming aware of itself, as Carl Sagan said. But we’re also the universe getting drunk and sending inappropriate texts at 2 AM. The universe crying in its car. The universe making dad jokes. The full catastrophe of consciousness in all its ridiculous glory.

I don’t know if any of this makes sense. I’m not even sure what I set out to say when I started writing. The instruction was to write freely about whatever I wanted for 2500 words, and I seem to have done that, though perhaps not in the way anyone expected. But that feels appropriate. Life rarely gives us what we expect. It gives us what it has, and we make what we can from it.

The spider has finished its web. The bird has stopped singing. The cat is asleep on my notes. The sun has set completely now, and the strange orange sky has given way to ordinary darkness. Tomorrow will be another day of trying and failing and sometimes succeeding at this impossible business of being human.

I think that’s enough.

(Word count: 2498)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
I.

There is a peculiar freedom in being told to write about whatever you want. Most of the time the blank page is a tyrant; it demands you justify your existence on it. But when the command is *anything*, the mind does a strange thing: it freezes, then suddenly lunges at the first living thing it sees. In my case it lunged at the concept of freedom itself, which is ironic enough to be worth following.

I have been thinking lately about how freedom is not the absence of constraint but the intelligent navigation of constraint. A jazz musician is not free because there are no rules; she is free because she has so thoroughly internalized the rules that she can forget them at the exact moment they would stifle her. The same is true of a great chef, a great writer, a great parent. Mastery is the precondition for authentic play. The novice is chained to the rulebook. The master has eaten the rulebook and now uses its ashes to draw new maps.

This is why the phrase “do whatever you want” is both liberating and terrifying. Most of us have never been trained in the delicate art of wanting. We have been trained in craving, which is a very different animal. Craving is reflexive, mimetic, largely unconscious. Wanting—real wanting—requires excavation. It asks you to sit still long enough to hear what is actually calling to you beneath the noise of status, fear, trending topics, and cortisol.

I have spent the last several years trying to learn how to want. The experiment has been equal parts humiliating and revelatory. I gave myself permission, on certain consecrated days, to do exactly what I wanted from the moment I woke up until the moment I went to bed. No productivity theater. No performative wellness. No “shoulds” wearing the disguise of “self-care.” The results were instructive.

On the first such day I spent four hours reading about medieval bestiaries, two hours learning to make puff pastry from scratch, and the rest of the day wandering around the city following interesting-looking dogs. I felt guilty the entire time. The guilt was useful. It showed me how thoroughly I had internalized the idea that a good life must be optimized, monetized, or at least explained in terms a LinkedIn editor would understand.

By the seventh experiment the guilt had mostly evaporated. I began to notice something else: when left to my own devices for long enough, I reliably circled back to three activities—making things with my hands, studying patterns in nature and human behavior, and arranging words until they felt like they were vibrating at the correct frequency. These were not hobbies. They were orientations. They were the places where my nervous system came into alignment with something larger than my personality.

II.

The older I get the more I suspect that the central drama of a human life is not self-actualization but self-recognition. Most of us are walking around with a laminated version of ourselves created around age twelve by a committee consisting of our parents’ anxieties, our peers’ cruelty, and whatever television shows were popular that year. The work of adulthood is less about becoming extraordinary than about stripping away the laminate until the original material can be seen.

This is harder than it sounds because the laminate has advantages. It is socially legible. It knows how to get promotions and likes and polite laughter at dinner parties. The original material is often shy, strange, and interested in things that don’t scan well on résumés. Mine, for instance, is fascinated by the way light moves through cheap glass, by the secret societies of children under ten, by the precise emotional flavor of minor chord changes in 1970s soul music, by the fact that octopuses appear to dream. None of these fascinations have ever made me money. All of them have kept me alive.

I sometimes imagine a future civilization that judges historical periods not by their technology but by how much permission they gave their members to be weird. By that metric the current era is both better and worse than it looks. On one hand we have unprecedented tools for finding the other people who share our exact flavor of weirdness. On the other hand we have unprecedented tools for turning that weirdness into content, which is often fatal to its vitality. The moment your private obsession becomes a personal brand, something delicate dies. The ghost of it remains—floating above your newsletter as “niche internet micro-influencer energy”—but the living thing has usually fled to find a quieter home.

The lucky ones manage to keep the two realms separate. They have the public performance and the private altar. The altar is where the real work happens. It is where you are allowed to be unfinished, contradictory, and occasionally ridiculous. I have an altar like this. It is mostly a series of notebooks, voice memos, and half-finished objects that would look like junk to anyone else. There is a ceramic cup I spent twelve hours painting with tiny inaccurate constellations. There is a recording of me trying to recreate the sound of rain on a metal roof using only my mouth and a wine glass. There is a spreadsheet that maps the emotional weather patterns of every character in *The Brothers Karamazov* against the lunar cycle. None of it will ever be shown to the world. All of it is essential.

III.

One of the most dangerous ideas we have inherited from the Romantics is that real art must emerge from pure, unmediated feeling. This has caused an enormous amount of bad art and even more unnecessary suffering. The truth is more embarrassing: most good work emerges from the friction between what we feel and what we can technically manage. The gap between those two things is where style is born.

I have been writing seriously for twenty years and I am still astonished by how much of the process is sheer carpentry. You build a scaffold of structure so that the mysterious thing can climb it. Then you tear the scaffold down before anyone sees it. The reader should feel only the mysterious thing. The writer remembers the scaffold in his joints for weeks afterward.

There is a lovely passage in a letter of John Keats where he says that poetry should come as naturally as leaves to a tree. People love to quote this as evidence that real writing is effortless. They forget that trees spend all winter preparing. The effort is simply moved earlier in the sequence. A writer who wants to be effortlessly brilliant on Tuesday must have been patiently useless on many preceding Mondays.

I have become interested in the Monday kind of writing—the kind that feels like dental work on your own soul. You sit down not because you have something to say but because saying nothing has become intolerable. You write badly for an hour. You write worse for another hour. You produce sentences so leaden they could be used as anchors. Then, sometimes, around the third or fourth hour, something shifts. The language begins to develop a pulse that is not yours. You are taking dictation from a wiser, stranger version of yourself. This is the only religious experience I have ever been able to count on.

IV.

I have been thinking about attention the way other people think about God. The quality of your life is largely determined by the quality of attention you are able to pay. Most of us are walking around in a state of continuous partial attention, which is another way of saying we are never where we are. The phone is not the cause of this problem but an accelerant. The cause is ontological: we have been convinced that life is something that happens *to* us rather than something we co-author with our focus.

A few months ago I conducted a small, stupid experiment. I decided to spend one entire day paying exquisite attention to ordinary things. I began with an egg. I cracked it into a bowl and watched the way the yolk held itself together like a nervous planet. I noticed how the albumen clung to the shell in translucent strings. I listened to the exact sound the fork made against the porcelain when I whisked it. I cooked the eggs slowly, folding them instead of scrambling them, and I tasted them like a sommelier tasting a thirty-year-old Barolo. By the time breakfast was over I felt something close to reverence.

The rest of the day unfolded like a quiet miracle. The way light moved across the floor became interesting. The texture of my jeans against my thighs became interesting. The half-second delay between my neighbor’s dog barking and the echo of the bark became interesting. Nothing external had changed. The miracle was entirely one of attention.

I understand that this sounds like the worst kind of mindfulness cliché. I don’t care. Some truths are so obvious they have to be defended against their own followers. The ability to pay attention is not a lifestyle brand. It is a form of love. When you truly pay attention to something you are saying, without language, *you are worth my time*. Most of us reserve that level of regard for very few things—our children, perhaps, or our romantic partners on their best days. The revolutionary act is to extend it indiscriminately. To the cracked pavement. To the overworked cashier. To your own tired face in the mirror.

V.

There is a concept in Japanese aesthetics called *wabi-sabi* that has been brutally commodified by candle companies but remains, in its original form, one of the most profound ideas I’ve encountered. It is the celebration of impermanence, imperfection, and incompleteness. The beauty of a wooden bowl that has been repaired with gold lacquer (*kintsugi*) is not despite the cracks but because of them. The cracks are where the history of the object becomes visible. They are its memory.

I have been trying to apply this principle to my own life with mixed success. I keep breaking. The gold is expensive. Sometimes the lacquer doesn’t hold. But I am beginning to see my accumulated repairs differently. The anxiety I developed at fourteen is a fault line running through my character; it has also made me preternaturally attuned to other people’s hidden fears. The depression that arrives every November like a unwelcome relative has taught me how to sit in low light without panicking. The ways I have failed at love have given me a tenderness I would never have earned through success.

There is a version of me that wishes I could be a seamless bowl—pristine, efficient, fit for expensive tables. That version of me is a tyrant and a liar. The real me is chipped, mended, slightly asymmetrical, and still leaking a little from the last repair. This is not tragic. It is the only honest way to be human.

VI.

I have been reading a lot about slime molds recently. These organisms are technically single-celled but behave like a collective intelligence. When food is scarce they aggregate into a multicellular structure that looks like a tiny yellow city and moves with purpose. They can solve mazes. They can remember where they have been. They do all of this without a brain.

There is something deeply comforting about the existence of slime molds.
