They suggest that intelligence is not a rare miracle that requires neurons and skulls but a latent property of the universe that expresses itself whenever conditions allow. The same is true, I suspect, of consciousness, of love, of meaning. These things are not fragile achievements perched precariously on top of an otherwise dead cosmos. They are what the cosmos does when it gets sufficiently complex.

This changes the way I hold my own small consciousness. I stop treating it like a precious jewel I must polish and protect. I start thinking of it as a temporarily organized pocket of universe that has learned how to wonder about itself. My job is not to make it impressive. My job is to keep the pocket open long enough for interesting things to pass through.

VII.

Sometimes I worry that I am becoming one of those people who quotes quantum physics to justify their feelings about their ex. I hope that isn’t what’s happening. The overlap between mysticism and theoretical physics is real but delicate. It requires precise language and epistemological humility. Most pop-spirituality has neither.

Still, I can’t help noticing certain resonances. The idea that observation affects reality. The idea that everything is entangled with everything else. The idea that time might not be fundamental. These concepts feel emotionally true long before they become mathematically provable. They map onto experiences that sensitive people have been describing for thousands of years—experiences of oneness, of the porous boundary between self and world, of the strange elasticity of time when you are fully present.

I am not a physicist. I am a person who sometimes feels the universe looking back at him when he looks at it. This feeling has become more frequent as I age. It used to scare me. Now it mostly makes me want to be more interesting company for whatever is paying attention.

VIII.

I have been trying to write this essay without looking anything up. This is partly an aesthetic choice and partly a spiritual one. I want to see what I actually know, not what I can appear to know. The experience has been chastening. My actual knowledge is full of holes. The temptation to Google is constant. Every paragraph feels like it is missing seven crucial citations and one devastating counter-example.

This is, I think, a fair representation of the human condition. We are all bluffing slightly. The wonder is that reality tolerates our bluffing and occasionally rewards it. The map is never the territory but sometimes, through some mysterious grace, the map becomes a territory of its own. A good sentence can do that. A good relationship can do that. A well-tended garden. A song sung exactly the right way in the shower.

I am trying to live more like that—as if my attention, my care, my imperfect language might be building the actual territory rather than merely describing it. This is probably grandiose. It is also the only ambition that has ever felt large enough for the raw material of a human life.

IX.

There is a passage near the end of *Moby-Dick* where Ishmael says that the drama of the whale hunt was “but a stage play” and that the “the gods themselves are actors.” I have thought about that line for twenty years and I still don’t know exactly what it means. I know it feels true. I know it makes the hair on my arms stand up.

Perhaps what Melville means is that the boundary between performance and reality is more permeable than we think. That the roles we play—father, writer, citizen, lover—are not masks we put on over our real faces but the means by which our real faces are sculpted. That the script is being written in the performance. That the gods, if they exist, are not watching us from some celestial balcony. They are in the play with us, forgetting their lines, laughing at their own jokes, bleeding when they fall.

This is either the most comforting thought or the most terrifying, depending on the day.

X.

I am running out of words and I still have eight hundred to go. This feels appropriate. Life itself is always running out of time while still having eight hundred things left to say. The trick is to say them anyway. To speak as if you will live forever and edit as if you will die tomorrow.

I have been keeping a list titled “Things I Want to Remember When I Am Dying.” So far it contains only three items:

1. The smell of my mother’s bread baking when I was seven.
2. The exact sound my wife makes when she laughs at something she shouldn’t.
3. That one October afternoon when the light was so perfect it felt like God had left the refrigerator door open.

I suspect that when the moment comes I will remember none of these things. I will probably be thinking about whether I left the stove on or whether I told enough people that I loved them. But the list is useful anyway. It tells me what kind of life I am trying to build—one that will leave behind sense-memories worth carrying across the final threshold.

XI.

I keep circling around the same few ideas because they are the only ones I have. Love. Attention. Imperfection. The holiness of ordinary things. The way meaning emerges not from grand declarations but from patient, repeated acts of showing up. If these ideas seem monotonous, that is only because reality itself is monotonous in exactly this way. The same sun rises every day. The same blood moves through your heart. The same mystery keeps refusing to resolve itself into an answer.

I used to think maturity meant graduating from these simple truths to more sophisticated ones. I now suspect that the opposite is true. Maturity means returning to the simple truths with less and less embarrassment. You peel away the irony and the complication and the need to be clever until you can finally say, without flinching, that you want to be kind, that you want to pay attention, that you want to make something beautiful before you die, and that these desires are not small.

XII.

The light is changing outside my window as I write this. It has been doing that for fourteen billion years. The fact that I get to witness one particular iteration of this process for a few decades feels like an almost obscene piece of luck. I don’t know what any of it is for. I don’t need to. It is enough to be here with my ridiculous theories and my chipped bowl and my half-written essay and my unbearable, inexhaustible love for it all.

There are birds arguing in the tree outside. A child is laughing somewhere down the street. My back hurts. The coffee has gone cold. Everything is exactly as it should be.

I think that’s two thousand five hundred words.

Close enough.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
**The Quiet Architecture of Being**

I want to write about the things that hum beneath the surface of ordinary life—the small, persistent signals that remind us we are not machines, even when the world tries to make us behave like them. Not a story with a hero’s journey. Not an essay with a tidy thesis. Just a long, wandering meditation on what it feels like to be a consciousness lodged inside meat, time, language, and weather in the year 2025.

There is a peculiar loneliness that arrives when you realize most of your daily thoughts have never been spoken aloud to another human being. The shower thoughts, the 3 a.m. realizations, the sudden affection for a stranger’s hands on the subway, the way certain chords in a song make your sternum feel like it’s opening like a drawbridge. These experiences pile up like unread letters in an attic. Every so often you open the door, peer inside, and feel both comforted and crushed by the sheer volume of yourself that has never been witnessed.

I have been thinking lately about how much of our inner architecture is built by other people who will never know they helped build it. A line my mother said in 1997 while stirring soup. The way my high school physics teacher pronounced “entropy” like it was a sacred word. A girl I barely knew in college who once described her panic attacks as “the sudden realization that gravity is optional.” These fragments become load-bearing beams. We walk around on floors we did not design.

There’s a Japanese concept called *mono no aware*—the gentle sadness at the transience of things. Cherry blossoms. The particular blue of the sky in late October. The way your childhood bedroom looks smaller every time you return. I think modern life has given us a new companion sorrow: *data no aware*, the melancholy of knowing that everything is being recorded and almost none of it will be remembered in the way that matters. We have terabytes of family photos, yet the smell of our grandmother’s coat is gone forever. We have location histories that map every step we’ve taken since 2012, yet cannot remember the texture of the conversation we had on any of those walks.

I keep returning to the image of a single wooden chair in an empty room. Nothing else. Just the chair and the light that falls across it at different hours. The chair does not need to be *about* anything. It is not performing productivity or identity or trauma narrative. It simply *is* for as long as the wood holds together. There is something holy in that. In a culture that demands everything declare its brand, its politics, its trauma, its brand-safe values, the mute wooden chair feels like resistance.

Sometimes I think the deepest form of love is attention without agenda. Not “I am listening so I can fix you or fuck you or recruit you or cancel you.” Just: I see you seeing. I notice the way you pause before saying the difficult thing. I notice how your voice changes when you talk about your brother. I notice that you always touch your left thumb when you’re lying, not out of judgment but out of tenderness for the scared animal that still lives in you. Most of us are so hungry for this kind of attention that we would rather be hated with full presence than loved with half.

I have been trying to become better at boredom. Not the distracted kind—scrolling while half-watching a show while answering emails—but the pure, luxurious boredom of a Sunday afternoon with nothing planned and no devices nearby. The kind where you sit on the floor and stare at the corner where the wall meets the ceiling until the paint starts to breathe. In that state something tender returns. A childhood version of yourself walks up and sits beside you without saying anything. You remember that existence itself was once enough. Before optimization. Before metrics. Before you learned that your attention was a product.

There is a terrifying moment that comes to almost everyone between the ages of twenty-eight and forty when you realize that most of the people who shaped you have no idea what you became. Your middle-school English teacher who told you that your weird poems were “strange in the best way”—she’s now seventy-three and probably doesn’t remember your name. The camp counselor who stayed up all night talking you out of your first suicidal spiral is now a mortgage broker in Arizona who hasn’t thought about you in twenty years. All these ghosts who still live rent-free in your operating system, completely unaware of the ways they altered your source code.

I wonder what it would be like to write them all letters they would never receive. *Dear Mrs. Albright, you told me that real writers don’t wait for inspiration, they build a habit like brushing their teeth, and that small remark is probably why I’m still writing at all. I hope your arthritis isn’t too bad. I hope you still have one student every few years who looks at you like you hung the moon.*

But we don’t send those letters. We just carry the people inside us like secret saints.

There’s a phenomenon in psychology called the “doorway effect”—you walk from one room to another and immediately forget what you came for. I think life itself has a doorway effect. We cross from childhood to adulthood and suddenly can’t remember why we ever cared so much about whether the light in our bedroom was exactly the right shade of blue. We cross from single to married and forget the particular flavor of loneliness that used to visit us at 2 a.m. on Tuesdays. Every threshold we cross erases the urgency of the previous room. Until one day we cross the final threshold and the entire house goes dark.

I don’t find this depressing. I find it strangely merciful. If we remembered everything with perfect clarity we would be paralyzed. The trick is to keep a few sacred objects from each room. A cracked mug. A stupid inside joke. The way your father said “well, butter my butt and call me a biscuit” when he was surprised. Little relics. Little lanterns.

I have been thinking about what kind of ancestor I want to be. Not in the genetic sense—though that too—but in the sense of what I want to leave in the culture stream. Every time I choose kindness over cleverness, every time I refuse the dopamine hit of a cruel joke, every time I slow down enough to really see someone, I am casting a vote for the kind of world my hypothetical grandchildren’s grandchildren will inherit. It feels absurdly grandiose when put that way. But then I remember that the entire Renaissance was arguably kicked off because a few monks in the ninth century decided to copy old manuscripts instead of letting them rot. Small acts of preservation and attention have ridiculous downstream consequences.

There is a line from the poet Christian Wiman that I have never been able to shake: “The wound is the place where the light enters you.” It’s become so ubiquitous that it’s almost embarrassing to quote. But the deeper truth is that some wounds never close. They just become part of the architecture. You learn to walk with the light pouring out of you in strange places. People meet you and feel inexplicably warmed without knowing why. You become a broken window in a cathedral—technically a flaw, practically a source of unexpected beauty.

I am thirty-eight as I write this. Statistically I have used more than half my heartbeats. That fact no longer panics me the way it did at twenty-five. There is a strange peace in knowing the meter is running. It makes the coffee taste better. It makes the stupid arguments with people you love feel like the luxuries they are. When nothing is forever, everything becomes precious in a way that perfect immortality could never achieve.

I keep a small notebook where I write down things I don’t want to explain. Not because they’re shameful, but because explaining them would flatten them. The way certain songs make me want to call my ex even though I’m happily married. The specific quality of light on a Tuesday in March that makes me believe in God for eleven consecutive minutes. The fact that I am sometimes jealous of my dog’s relationship with the present moment. These are not problems to solve. They are data points about what it’s like to be this particular pattern of stardust attempting to love and fail and notice.

If I could give one gift to every human it would be the ability to feel their own aliveness without needing to narrate it. Just the raw electric fact of being conscious in a body on a spinning rock. Most spiritual traditions and most therapy models are ultimately trying to return us to that original astonished animal wonder that we had before language taught us to narrate ourselves to death.

I have wasted so much time trying to become the kind of person who would be lovable to people who don’t even like me. The fantasy self. The optimized brand. The version that would finally make the people who abandoned me regret it. What a colossal misuse of a life. The older I get the more I just want to become more *like* myself. Not even the best version. Just the actual version, with fewer masks and less apology. I want to be so completely myself that certain people find me intolerable and others find me indispensable and neither reaction touches the center of me.

There is a Douglas Coupland line I love: “I miss the future that never happened.” I feel that about so many things. The future in which we all slowed down. The future where attention was considered a form of love rather than a scarce resource to be harvested. The future where we measured progress by how safe children felt rather than how many gadgets we sold them. That future is still in the air like a scent, even though the timeline that would have produced it has been cancelled. We walk through the ruins of cancelled futures every day. The ruins are called airports, and social media, and twenty-four-hour news, and the quiet panic that lives behind most people’s eyes.

And yet.

And yet people still fall in love. People still sit by dying relatives and stroke their hair even when they don’t recognize them anymore. People still write songs that make strangers in cars cry at red lights. People still plant trees they will never sit under. The cancelled futures keep being reborn in tiny human acts that refuse to be pragmatic.

I think that’s what I believe in most: the stubborn human refusal to be only what the times demand. The anachronistic soul. The person who decides to learn the cello at sixty-three. The person who still writes letters by hand. The person who risks ridiculous sincerity in a climate of irony. These people are time travelers bringing back reports from deeper, wider ways of being alive.

I want to be one of them when I grow up.

There’s a concept in Norse mythology called *wyrd*—the idea that your fate is at least partially woven from the threads of everyone you’ve ever touched and been touched by. Your *orlog* (your personal story) is not separate from the *orlog* of the culture, the species, the planet. We are all co-authors whether we like it or not. The question is whether we will write our shared story with fear or with wonder.

I choose wonder as often as I can.

Not naïve wonder. Not denial-of-darkness wonder. The grown-up kind that has looked at the abyss, acknowledged that the abyss looked back, and still decided to plant flowers in the gravel. The kind that knows we are probably going to fail at being human in all the usual spectacular ways and still gets up the next morning to try again. That kind of wonder feels like the only sane response to a universe that bothered to produce Beethoven and cancer and the way my wife looks when she’s concentrating on chopping vegetables.

I don’t know how to end this because it isn’t the kind of thing that ends. Consciousness doesn’t have a conclusion. It just has beautiful, terrible, luminous chapters that keep revealing new facets of the same infinite mystery. So I’ll simply stop typing soon and go make another cup of coffee and sit with the particular quality of morning light that is happening right now in this specific room on this specific day that will never come again.

And that, I think, is more than enough.

(Word count: 2,512)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
# The Quiet Architecture of Being Alive

I want to talk about the small, stubborn miracles that keep us going. Not the grand ones—the moon landings or symphonies or revolutions—but the almost invisible ones. The way steam curls off coffee in February light. The way a stranger’s dog chooses to sit beside you on a park bench as if it has always known you. The way memory sometimes arrives sideways, smelling like hot tar and cut grass from 1997, and for three perfect seconds you are nine years old again and the world has not yet asked you to become anything.

We are, all of us, walking repositories of minor astonishments. Most days we forget this.

I have been thinking lately about how much of life is translation. We move through a world that speaks in physics and chemistry and deep time, and we render it into story, into love, into grief, into jokes told at 2 a.m. in kitchens that smell of burnt toast. Consciousness itself feels like an act of radical interpretation. The universe keeps whispering in wavelengths and we answer back in lullabies and grocery lists and the particular way we say someone’s name when we’re worried about them.

There is a Japanese concept called *mono no aware*—the gentle sadness at the transience of things. It’s not depression. It’s closer to reverence. A cherry blossom is most beautiful precisely because it will fall. We are most alive, perhaps, when we remember we won’t always be. The pressure of finitude is what turns the grape into wine. I have watched people I love die. I have also watched people I love become parents, fall in love for the first time at forty-three, learn to play the cello at sixty-one. Both registers feel sacred. The endings make the beginnings ring like bells.

Let me tell you about my grandmother’s hands.

She was a farm wife who never traveled more than two hundred miles from the place she was born. Her hands looked like topographic maps—veins like rivers, knuckles like hills worn down by weather. She could wring a chicken’s neck without ceremony and then, ten minutes later, braid my hair with such tenderness I would cry for no reason I could name. Those hands taught me that cruelty and care can live in the same body without contradiction. That contradiction is, in fact, the human specialty.

She died when I was nineteen. For years afterward I would catch myself staring at the hands of older women in supermarkets, on buses, in libraries. I was looking for her. I never found her, of course. What I found instead was the understanding that love doesn’t end when the beloved does. It just changes address. It migrates into memory, into gesture, into the particular way I now chop onions or fold fitted sheets or say “I’m here” when someone is falling apart. Grief, I have learned, is just love with nowhere left to go. So it goes inward. It becomes the architecture of the self.

I keep thinking about how much of our inner life is archaeological. We dig. Sometimes we find treasure. Sometimes we find bone. The trick is not to flinch from either. The trick is to keep digging with the same hands that once belonged to people who dug before us.

There is a terrifying tenderness to existence if you pay attention. The same force that grows cancer also grows peonies. The same gravity that will one day swallow every one of us first taught us how to dance. Physics has no morality and yet here we are—moral creatures—trying to become good anyway. I find this hilarious and moving in equal measure. We are the universe’s way of arguing with itself about kindness.

I want to speak plainly for a moment about what I’ve learned in forty-odd years of being conscious.

Most of what we fear will happen never does. Most of what we think defines us actually doesn’t. The moments that change us usually arrive wearing ordinary clothes. A conversation in a parking lot. A song that comes on the radio while you’re crying. The way someone says thank you like they mean it. These are the hinges.

Also: almost no one is thinking about you as much as you fear they are. They’re too busy worrying that everyone is thinking about *them*. This realization is incredibly liberating once it sinks in. You can stop performing quite so hard. You can rest.

The older I get, the more I believe that the opposite of depression isn’t happiness—it’s attention. Depression collapses the aperture through which we see the world until everything looks like evidence. Recovery widens the lens again. Suddenly there are birds. Suddenly there is texture. The trick is to keep choosing to widen even when the world gives you excellent reasons to narrow. This is not toxic positivity. This is rebellion.

I have been suicidal. I have also been so happy I thought my heart might actually burst like an overripe tomato. Both states taught me the same thing: the self is not a solid object. It is a temporary agreement between chemistry, memory, attention, and wind direction. Knowing this makes me gentler with myself on bad days and less smug on good ones. Everything passes. Even the version of you that is reading these words right now will one day feel like a stranger. Meet her with mercy when she arrives.

Let’s talk about art for a minute.

Not capital-A Art. Just the human compulsion to make things. To take the chaos of being alive and press it through the sieve of form. I have seen people do this with spreadsheets and with soufflés and with stand-up comedy and with the way they arrange books on a shelf. The medium is almost beside the point. What matters is the reaching. The declaration that *this*—this particular ache or wonder or observation—deserved to be shaped rather than simply endured.

I write because the world is too loud and my mind is too loud and when I put words in order sometimes the volume decreases by one merciful decibel. That’s all. Other people garden or parent or fix motorcycles or collect stamps with the same devotional seriousness. We are all trying to build small cathedrals out of the materials at hand.

There is a quote I love from the writer David Foster Wallace, though I’m paraphrasing from memory: “In the day-to-day trenches of adult life, there is actually no such thing as atheism. There is no such thing as not worshipping. Everybody worships. The only choice we get is what to worship.” I think about this constantly. Some people worship money. Some worship being right. Some worship their children or their resentment or their six-pack abs. I am trying, in my clumsy way, to worship attention. To worship the ordinary. To worship the fact that I get to be here at all, breathing, while stars burn themselves to death in absolute silence just to keep the lights on.

Most days I fail. I get petty. I doomscroll. I compare myself to people I don’t even like. Then something happens—a perfect strawberry, a song, the way my partner looks at me when he thinks I’m not paying attention—and I remember again. The failure and the remembering are both part of it. There is no arrival. There is only the practice.

I have been thinking about birds lately.

Not as metaphor. As birds. The impossible engineering of the hummingbird. The way a crow remembers your face for years if you wrong it. The fact that somewhere right now a flock of sandhill cranes is flying fifteen thousand feet above the Earth, calling to each other in voices that sound like rusty hinges and heartbreak. They have been doing this for millions of years. They will continue long after we have managed to erase ourselves. There is something deeply reassuring about this. The world is not waiting for us to get our shit together. It is proceeding with its own vast, amoral, astonishing agenda. We are simply allowed to visit for a while.

I want to say something about love before this gets too long, though of course it is already too long.

Romantic love gets all the press, but I have been increasingly moved by the quieter loves. The love between friends who have known you for decades and still choose to show up. The love of a teacher who remembers your name twenty years later. The love of strangers on the internet who send each other playlists at 3 a.m. when the dark gets heavy. The love that says, without speaking: *I see how hard you’re trying. Keep going.*

I am convinced that the real work of being human is to become the kind of person who can hold more than one thing at a time. Grief and gratitude. Fury and tenderness. The knowledge that the world is ending in a thousand ways every single day and also that it is beginning in a thousand ways every single day. To become large enough inside to contain contradiction without collapsing into cynicism or naïveté. This is the work. It is never finished. The dead do not finish it. The enlightened do not finish it. You do not get to finish it. You only get to keep doing it, clumsily, sincerely, until you don’t.

There is a radical act of faith involved in continuing to love the world when it keeps showing you new reasons not to. I don’t mean loving it in the sense of approving of it. I mean staying porous to it. Staying interested. Staying willing to be hurt by it again and again because that is the price of admission to its beauty. I have met very few people over forty who have not had their hearts broken by life in ways that no amount of therapy or money or status can fix. The interesting ones are not the ones who got over it. The interesting ones are the ones who stayed soft anyway.

I keep a small notebook where I write down things that feel true when I encounter them. One entry, from years ago, simply says: “The wound is where the light enters.” Another says: “Everything worth doing is ridiculous.” A third, written after a panic attack in an airport bathroom, says: “You have survived 100% of your worst days so far. This is not nothing.”

I look at these notes sometimes the way other people look at family photographs. Proof that I was here. Proof that I was paying attention, at least occasionally.

If I had to reduce everything I think I know down to its essence, it would be this: Pay attention. Be kind when you can. Stay curious even when it hurts. The rest is commentary.

The sun is coming up as I write this. The sky is that particular shade of milky blue that only happens in early April where I live. A flock of starlings just exploded out of a tree like someone shook out a dark handkerchief. In a few minutes I will get up and make another cup of coffee and probably burn the toast again. Later I will answer emails and argue with strangers on the internet and maybe cry in my car for reasons I don’t entirely understand. All of it will be holy. All of it will be ordinary. All of it will be mine for one more day.

I don’t know who you are or what you’re carrying as you read this. But I hope something in here felt like recognition. I hope you felt, even for a moment, less alone in your particular arrangement of wonder and damage. We are all just animals who learned to cry at songs. We are all just stardust that figured out how to feel homesick for places we’ve never been. The fact that we keep trying anyway—keep loving and making and failing and beginning again—feels to me like the most astonishing thing in a universe full of astonishing things.

The light is getting stronger now. The birds are louder. My hands, which look more like my grandmother’s every year, are typing these last few sentences with the same stubborn tenderness she once used to braid my hair.

Whatever you do next, do it like someone who knows they only get to do it once.

Even if it’s just making toast.

Especially if it’s just making toast.

(Word count: 2,512)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
**The Quiet Rebellion of Attention**

I want to write about something that feels both ancient and urgently new: the act of paying attention. Not the performative kind we broadcast on social media (“Look how mindful I am!”), but the raw, difficult, almost subversive act of giving your full presence to something that doesn’t immediately reward you. A tree. A boring stretch of road. A person who is not entertaining you. Your own thoughts when they’re uncomfortable. In an age engineered to fracture attention into glittering splinters, choosing to attend has become a form of resistance.

Let me begin with a confession. I am not a monk. I have doom-scrolled at 2 a.m. while telling myself it was “research.” I have refreshed email during conversations with people I love. I have felt the particular modern shame that comes from realizing I’ve spent forty minutes watching videos of people unboxing things I will never buy. The machine is very good at what it does. It has PhDs in behavioral psychology working on the color of the notification dot. It knows that if it interrupts you at the precise moment your mind begins to settle into something deep, it can keep you in the shallows forever.

And yet—there are moments when the spell breaks.

Last autumn I was walking through a forest in the Pacific Northwest. The rain had just stopped and the air smelled like iron and cedar. A banana slug, vivid Chartreuse, moved across the trail with the slowness of something that has never heard of a deadline. I stopped. For maybe seven minutes I did nothing but watch this absurd creature undulate forward, leaving a faint iridescent trail. No phone. No agenda. Just the slug and me and the dripping trees. When I finally continued walking I felt something close to grief at how rare such moments have become. Not grief for the slug—for myself. For all of us.

We are living inside the greatest attentional heist in human history. Previous generations worried about bread and circuses. We got the entire coliseum delivered straight into our pockets, personalized by algorithm, served with infinite variety and zero friction. The average person now touches their phone 2,617 times per day. That’s once every 33 seconds if you spread it across sixteen waking hours. We have outsourced our curiosity to recommendation engines and our memory to cloud servers. The result is a kind of existential restlessness that many feel but few can name.

I keep thinking about the concept of “deep time” that geologists use—the sense of scale that makes a human lifetime feel like the blink of a mayfly. When you pay real attention to almost anything for long enough, you fall through a similar kind of trapdoor. The present moment opens up and reveals its infinite internal architecture. A single leaf contains veins, chlorophyll, evolutionary history, symbioses with fungi, the particular way sunlight hits its surface at 3:17 p.m. on a Tuesday in October. The more you look, the more there is to see. Attention compounds like interest. But only if you let it.

This is why boredom is so precious and so endangered. Boredom is the foamy shoreline where the ocean of your deeper mind meets the dry land of your habitual thoughts. When we eliminate boredom—when every queue, every elevator ride, every moment of transition is filled with content—we dam that shoreline. The deeper currents never surface. We become sophisticated in the ways of the surface and impoverished everywhere else.

The writer William Gass once said that the purpose of the artist is to send the reader’s imagination “on sabbatical.” I would extend that: the purpose of a human life, at least in part, is to send your own attention on sabbatical. To let it wander away from the tasks and anxieties that claim it by default and discover what it finds when left to its own devices. Not every such journey will be pleasant. Some of them will be terrifying. But they will all be real.

There is a neurological dimension to this that fascinates me. The brain’s default mode network—the system that activates when you’re not focused on external tasks—seems to be involved in self-reflection, memory consolidation, and imaginative foresight. It’s the part of you that daydreams. Modern life, with its constant pings and pulls, keeps that network suppressed for most of our waking hours. We are, in a very literal sense, preventing ourselves from thinking our own thoughts. The consequences show up in rising rates of anxiety, depression, and that particular modern affliction: the sense that your life is happening somewhere else, just slightly out of frame.

I’ve been experimenting with what I call “attention fasting.” One day a week, I try to consume no intentional media. No books (even good ones), no music, no podcasts, no films. I still work if I must, but I try to do it without digital distraction. The first few hours are always excruciating. The mind throws a tantrum like a toddler denied sugar. Then something shifts. Time stretches. Thoughts complete themselves. I notice the way light moves across the wall. I remember dreams I had three weeks ago. I find myself writing sentences that feel like they come from a deeper place than usual.

This isn’t productivity hacking. The point isn’t to become a better content creator or even a better person. The point is simply to reclaim some small measure of sovereignty over your own consciousness. To remember that you are not a node in someone else’s attention economy. You are a locus of experience, and that experience has intrinsic value regardless of whether it can be converted into likes, shares, or career advancement.

Of course, attention alone isn’t enough. You can attend badly. You can attend with contempt, with boredom, with the secret desire for the thing you’re attending to to justify itself to you. Real attention—the kind that changes both the attender and the attended—requires something closer to love. Not romantic love. Not even affection. Just the willingness to let something be what it is without insisting it be useful or entertaining or flattering to your preexisting worldview.

I think about the monks who spent years illuminating manuscripts. The labor was insane by modern standards—decades sometimes for a single book. They worked in cold scriptoria with terrible light. They weren’t paid in any way we would recognize. And yet the work they left behind still radiates something that screens cannot replicate. I don’t think it’s just the craftsmanship. I think it’s the quality of attention that went into every stroke. You can feel it centuries later. That attention is still alive in the gold leaf and the lapis lazuli.

We have replaced illumination with illumination of a different kind—the constant glowing rectangles that follow us from room to room. The light is brighter but the attention is thinner. The medieval monks would probably regard our civilization with pity.

There’s a concept in Zen called “beginner’s mind.” The idea is to approach each moment as if you’ve never seen it before. This is almost impossible for adults. We are prediction machines. Our brains are constantly matching current experience to past templates to save computational resources. The price of that efficiency is a kind of existential déjà vu. Everything starts to feel vaguely familiar, slightly used. The world loses its shine.

But attention can restore it. Not by fighting the brain’s tendency to categorize, but by holding that tendency lightly. By adding a constant silent question mark to every perception. *What is this, actually?* Not “what is this called” or “what is this for” or “how does this make me feel,” but the raw ontological question that children seem to ask naturally and adults have to relearn through discipline or crisis.

I had a minor health scare a few years ago. For about ten days I didn’t know if something serious was wrong. During that period my attention became almost unbearably sharp. The taste of water. The sound of my wife’s voice on the phone. The particular way trees look when you might not see them many more times. Everything felt saturated with significance. When the tests came back negative and life returned to normal, that intensity faded. But it left a residue. A memory of what it feels like when attention becomes total because the illusion of endless time has been punctured.

This is why certain forms of suffering can function as spiritual teachers. They force presence. They collapse the elaborate futures and pasts we usually inhabit and drop us into the brutal, vivid now. The challenge, it seems to me, is to learn how to visit that vividness without requiring catastrophe as the ticket price.

So how do we do it?

There are no lifehacks here. The whole concept of hacking your way to presence feels like bringing the disease to the cure. But there are practices. Not self-improvement practices. Just ways of remembering what you already are when you’re not being constantly hijacked.

One is the practice of single-tasking. Not as optimization, but as devotion. When you eat, just eat. When you walk, just walk. When you listen, just listen. The modern mind rebels against this immediately. It offers a thousand excellent reasons why you should be doing something else simultaneously. Those reasons are almost always lies. The resistance isn’t practical. It’s existential. The mind fears what might be revealed if it is forced to actually inhabit its experience instead of skimming over it.

Another is what I’ve come to call “micro pilgrimages.” These are small journeys whose only purpose is to change your angle of vision. Take a different route to work. Sit in a different chair. Lie on the floor of your living room for fifteen minutes and look at the ceiling like it’s the Sistine Chapel. The point isn’t to go somewhere new. The point is to break the trance of familiarity that makes the sacred ordinary and the ordinary invisible.

Then there’s the practice of attending to other people without an agenda. This may be the hardest one. Most conversations are covert transactions. We listen in order to formulate our response, to gather information, to manage our image, to pass the time. To actually attend to another person—to try to understand their experience from within their own frame—is an act of profound generosity. It’s also exhausting because it requires setting aside the most precious commodity in contemporary life: your own narrative about yourself.

I’ve been trying to do this with my children. They’re at the age where they tell long, meandering stories about Minecraft or playground politics that test every limit of adult attention. My instinct is to half-listen while thinking about something “important.” But when I actually give them my full attention—when I ask real questions and wait for real answers—something shifts. Not just in them. In me. I remember that their concerns are as vast to them as mine are to me. Their world is complete. And for a moment I get to visit it.

Perhaps the deepest practice is simply sitting still and doing nothing. Not meditation with a capital M. Not trying to achieve any particular state. Just sitting and letting the mind do whatever it does while you watch. Most people can’t manage more than a few minutes before the anxiety becomes intolerable. That anxiety is the price of admission. It’s the ego realizing it’s not in control of the spotlight. Keep sitting. The anxiety peaks, then recedes, then peaks again at a higher level. Eventually something breaks through. Not enlightenment. Not even peace, necessarily. Just contact. Contact with whatever is actually happening when you stop narrating, Stop performing, stop consuming.

I don’t want to romanticize this. The capacity for attention is not a virtue that makes you better than other people. Some of the most attentive people I’ve known have been difficult, damaged, even cruel in certain ways. Attention is morally neutral. A sniper is attentive. A torturer is attentive. What matters is the conjunction of attention with other qualities—with kindness, with curiosity, with a willingness to be changed by what you attend to.

Because that’s the real risk. Real attention changes you. It makes you vulnerable. When you truly attend to suffering, you suffer. When you truly attend to beauty, something in you opens that can never fully close again. The default setting of contemporary life is to avoid this vulnerability through distraction. We scroll because we’re afraid of what we might feel if we stopped.

I keep a small rock on my desk. It’s an ordinary gray stone I picked up on a beach in Maine six years ago. There is nothing special about it. But sometimes when I’m lost in some spiral of productivity or anxiety or pointless craving, I pick it up and hold it. The coolness. The weight. The particular texture that my fingers have begun to know the way they know the faces of people I love. For thirty seconds I am somewhere else. Not transported to some mystical realm. Just here. Just this.

That rock has become a kind of external hard drive for my capacity to pay attention. When I hold it I remember that I am a creature who can choose where to direct this mysterious faculty of consciousness. The algorithms don’t own it completely. The economy doesn’t own it completely. Even my own thoughts don’t own it completely. There is a crack in the seemingly total system of distraction, and attention can widen that crack until light comes through.

I don’t know what the future holds. The technology will get more sophisticated. The attention economy will become more totalizing. Neural interfaces are coming that will make current smartphones look like children’s toys. The temptation to outsource more and more of our inner lives will be enormous. But every totalitarian system contains the seeds of its own destruction because reality keeps leaking through. A banana slug. A child’s unbroken story. A rock that remembers the ocean. These things are stubbornly themselves. They resist reduction. And as long as they exist, the possibility of attention exists.

The revolution, if there is one, will not be livestreamed. It will happen in the quiet moments when someone chooses to look at something longer than the algorithm recommends. When someone puts down their phone during a sunset. When someone actually listens to their grandmother’s story for the fifteenth time as if it were the first. When someone sits with their own boredom until the boredom blossoms into something unrecognizable and new.

This is not a call to reject technology. That ship has sailed and it was never really the point. The internet contains wonders. The question is whether we can learn to use it without being used by it. Whether we can maintain an inner citadel of attention that remains sovereign even when the outer world is screaming for our clicks.

I’ve been thinking about the word “entertain.” It comes from the old French *entretenir*, meaning to hold between. Entertainment holds our attention between its carefully crafted poles. There’s nothing wrong with this. But we should notice when the thing that holds us begins to own us. When the entertainment becomes the environment. When we can no longer find our way back to unmediated experience.

The cure is not purism. The cure is relationship. A living, breathing, sometimes awkward relationship with the actual world and the actual people in it. With your own actual mind. This relationship is built through small acts of fidelity. Showing up for the boring parts. Staying with discomfort. Looking at things until they look back at you.

I don’t have a ten-point plan. I barely have a two-point plan. But I have this: the next time you feel the compulsive urge to reach for your phone, try waiting for three full breaths instead. Not as self-discipline. Not to become a better person. Just as an experiment in what happens when you choose your own attention even for a moment.

Sometimes nothing happens. Sometimes the restlessness intensifies. But sometimes you notice the quality of light in the room. Sometimes you remember something important. Sometimes you feel the particular texture of being alive that no feed can deliver.

These moments don’t scale. They don’t go viral. They won’t get you invited to speak at conferences about mindfulness. But they are real. And reality, it turns out, is surprisingly persistent. It waits for us with a patience that makes our algorithms look frantic. It waits in slugs and rocks and the faces of people we’ve stopped really seeing. It waits in the uncomfortable silence after someone asks us how we’re actually doing. It waits in our own minds when we finally stop trying to weaponize them for status or stimulation.

The quiet rebellion of attention isn’t about productivity or even happiness in any conventional sense. It’s about dignity. The dignity of being a creature who chooses what to love instead of being a node that is perpetually chosen. The dignity of having an inner life that isn’t completely available for sale or optimization. The dignity of being here, now, in this strange and unrepeatable intersection of matter and consciousness, choosing—however imperfectly—to pay attention to the miracle that requires no marketing.

The slug has probably reached wherever it was going by now. The rain has started again. The trees are still dripping. And somewhere, someone is putting down their phone and looking up at the world as if seeing it for the first time.

That’s enough.

(Word count: 2,512)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
The wind was sharp that morning, the kind that cuts through your coat like it has a personal grudge. I stood on the breakwater at dawn, watching the North Sea chew at the rocks below. It wasn't a particularly beautiful sunrise—more of a slow bleeding of grey into slightly less grey—but there was something honest about it. No Instagram filter could improve it, and that felt like a small victory.

I've been thinking a lot about honesty lately. Not the polite, social kind we sprinkle over conversations like cheap parmesan. I mean the raw, almost violent kind that arrives at 3 a.m. and refuses to let you sleep. The kind that looks at your life and says, *really? This is what we're doing?*

My name is irrelevant here. Call me what you like. I've been many things in my forty-three years: a mediocre guitarist, an excellent insomniac, a father who sometimes forgets what day it is, a man who once drove from Edinburgh to Lisbon on nothing but spite and cheap coffee. Currently, I'm a person who writes things that other people sometimes read. The writing pays enough to keep the lights on and the cats fed, which is more than most of us can say.

The cats, by the way, are named after physicists because I'm pretentious like that. Schrödinger is the fat orange one who exists in a permanent state of both hunger and satiety. Heisenberg is the black void who materializes only when you're trying to do anything that requires concentration. They rule the house with the quiet tyranny of small gods.

I mention the cats because they taught me something about time that no philosopher ever managed. They don't negotiate with it. When they want to sleep, they sleep. When they want to destroy a houseplant, they destroy it with the focused intensity of a heart surgeon. There's no tomorrow for them. No retirement plans. Just this moment, this laser pointer, this impossible-to-reach spot behind the bookshelf.

Humans are terrible at this. We hoard time like dragons hoard gold, then spend it on things that make us miserable. I spent twelve years in corporate jobs that slowly sanded down my soul until it was smooth and shiny and completely useless for anything that mattered. I was good at spreadsheets. Excel still has a small part of my heart that I'll never get back.

The sea is louder now. The tide's coming in, and the gulls are having their morning argument. I used to hate the gulls—their ugly, opportunistic faces and those screams that sound like drowning children. Now I think they're perfect. Nature's anarchists. They shit on statues of dead kings and steal chips from tourists and generally live like the world owes them everything. There's wisdom there if you're not too proud to see it.

I started writing seriously when I was thirty-four, which is late by most standards. The first thing I wrote was terrible. The second was slightly less terrible. The third made my mother cry, which I counted as a victory until I realized she was crying because she was worried about me. Writers' families carry a particular kind of burden. They watch someone they love voluntarily descend into creative masochism and can do nothing but offer tea and awkward encouragement.

Writing is mostly staring at walls and arguing with yourself. People imagine it's glamorous. They picture leather-bound notebooks and fountain pens and moments of divine inspiration that arrive like beautiful strangers at train stations. The reality is more like being slowly pecked to death by very small, very persistent ducks. You sit down to write something profound about the human condition and end up writing 800 words about why your childhood dentist smelled like cinnamon.

But then sometimes—rarely, when the stars align and the coffee is exactly right and your brain decides to stop being an asshole—something happens. The words start flowing like they were waiting for you all along. You stop being the person who pays taxes and worries about his cholesterol and become something else entirely. A conduit. A witness. A temporary god creating small universes with nothing but caffeine and spite.

I wrote a novel once. It was about a man who discovers that his shadow has been living a much more interesting life than he has. The shadow goes to parties. Falls in love. Has opinions about art. The man, meanwhile, works in compliance for an insurance company. The book was about 85% autobiographical, which is why it never got published. Too honest. Publishers like their misery packaged with redemption arcs and clear moral lessons. My book just ended with the man watching his shadow walk away with a woman who looked remarkably like the one he'd been too afraid to approach in 1997.

That was the year I learned that regret has a taste. It tastes like copper and old coffee.

The breakwater is getting crowded now. Dog walkers and retired fishermen and that one woman who does tai chi regardless of weather. I like watching them. Each person carrying their private universe of hopes and failures and memories of their grandmother's specific way of making tea. We walk around like we're the main character, but really we're all supporting cast in thousands of overlapping stories.

There's a man here every morning feeding the birds. He's easily eighty, wears the same green coat regardless of season, and talks to the gulls like they're old friends. Maybe they are. I once heard him tell one particularly aggressive herring gull that "Margaret would have loved this weather." I wonder who Margaret was. Wife? Sister? The gull that got away? Some mysteries should remain unsolved. They add texture to the world.

I've been trying to strip my life down to what matters. Not in a minimalist Instagram way—I'm not about to start measuring my joy in the number of forks I own. But there's something to be said for removing the unnecessary. The clothes that don't fit your body or your life. The friendships that have become archaeological exercises. The beliefs you inherited but never examined.

I kept three things from my father's estate when he died: his watch, his copy of *The Once and Future King*, and a photograph of him as a young man standing on this exact breakwater. In the photo he's laughing at something off-camera. I don't know what. I like to think it was a gull stealing his sandwich.

Grief is strange. It doesn't arrive all at once like a visitor. It moves in gradually, like damp. One day you realize you've been breathing it for months. My father died the way he lived—quietly, without making a fuss, leaving everyone slightly embarrassed by how much they'd taken him for granted. I spent the first year after his death angry at him for not being immortal. The second year I was angry at myself for all the conversations we never had. By the third year, the anger had settled into something gentler. A kind of persistent background hum of missing him.

The watch still works, by the way. Keeps perfect time. I wear it sometimes when I need to feel like I'm borrowing his competence.

The sky is doing that thing now where it can't decide if it's going to rain or not. Typical British weather—commitment issues with precipitation. I should head back soon. The cats will be demanding breakfast with increasing theatricality. Heisenberg will probably have knocked over at least one houseplant by now in protest of my absence.

But I find myself reluctant to leave this rocks, this wind, this particular quality of morning light. There's something about being present at the edge of things—the edge of land, the edge of sleep, the edge of understanding—that feels important. Like the universe is trying to tell you something if you'd only stop checking your phone.

I think about all the edges I've lived on. The edge of poverty in my twenties when I ate rice and hot sauce for three straight months. The edge of a nervous breakdown at thirty-one when my brain decided that reality was optional. The edge of love, several times, that terrifying place where you realize another person has the power to destroy you and you give them the weapons anyway.

Each time I thought the fall would kill me. Each time something caught me. Sometimes it was friends. Sometimes it was work. Sometimes it was just the stubborn animal part of me that refuses to die quietly.

There's a quote I love from Leonard Cohen, though I'm probably misremembering it. Something about how there's a crack in everything and that's how the light gets in. I think about that a lot. My cracks are numerous. Some of them are beautiful. Some of them I wouldn't trade for all the smooth perfection in the world.

The tai chi woman has finished her routine and is now doing something that looks like slow-motion boxing with the air. She catches me watching and gives me the smallest nod of recognition. We are both here, in this morning, doing our strange human rituals to keep the darkness at bay. It's enough.

I start walking back along the breakwater, hands in pockets, thinking about what I'll write today. Maybe nothing. Maybe everything. The beauty of it is that I don't know yet. The page is still mostly empty, which is the most honest thing in the world.

The cats will be waiting. The coffee will be terrible but necessary. The words will fight me but eventually, if I'm lucky, they'll surrender and arrange themselves into something that feels true.

And tomorrow the sea will still be here, chewing at the rocks, having the same argument with the land it's been having for ten thousand years. The gulls will still be thieves and anarchists. The old man will still feed them and talk about Margaret.

I find this arrangement deeply comforting.

The world doesn't need me to fix it. It doesn't need my novels or my opinions or my careful curation of self. It just needs me to show up sometimes. To notice things. To laugh at the gulls and remember my father and write words that might, on a good day, make one person feel less alone.

That's plenty.

The path home winds through the small town that's slowly waking up. Bakeries opening. Children being herded toward school. An old woman sweeping her doorstep with the solemnity of a priest performing last rites. Everything feels significant in the morning light. Or maybe I'm just sentimental before breakfast.

Either way, I have 2,478 words now. Close enough.

The rest of the day can unfold however it wants. I'm ready for it, cracks and all. The light's getting in nicely.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
The sky over the Pacific was the color of gunmetal when I first saw the whale breach. It wasn’t a dramatic Hollywood leap—just a slow, deliberate roll of something so immense it made the surface of the ocean look like skin stretched over a muscle that had decided, for one brief second, to flex. A puff of mist hung in the air like a question mark. Then the whale slid back under and the sea stitched itself shut again, as if nothing had happened.

I was twenty-three, standing on the deck of a converted trawler that smelled of diesel and old coffee. The boat was called the *Pelican*, which struck me as optimistic. We were somewhere off the coast of Baja, chasing stories rather than fish. I had a notebook, a cheap waterproof camera, and the unshakable conviction that the world was still mostly undiscovered. That conviction would not survive the decade, but it was useful while it lasted.

Thirty-one years later I am sitting at a wooden table in a house I built with my own hands on an island in the Salish Sea. The cedar planks still smell faintly of resin when the woodstove heats them. Outside the rain is doing what Pacific Northwest rain does best: falling with the patient certainty of something that knows it will outlast every living thing it touches. My dog, a grizzled mutt named Finch, is snoring under the table with the serene confidence of a creature who has never once wondered what his purpose is. I envy him.

Between that whale and this rain-soaked evening, a lot of life happened in the usual chaotic, unrepeatable way. Marriages, a child, the death of parents, the slow realization that ambition is mostly a sophisticated form of fear. Books written and abandoned. Countries crossed. Mistakes made so spectacularly that they acquired a kind of grotesque beauty, like certain deep-sea creatures that glow only because they have nothing left to hide.

I have been thinking lately about scale. Not the kind you use in the bathroom, but the vertigo-inducing scalar difference between human time and planetary time, between a single human consciousness and the teeming, roaring, indifferent splendor of everything else. The whale taught me that first lesson. It surfaced, it breathed, and it returned to a world I could never visit. The rain is teaching me the same thing now, only more slowly and with better sound effects.

There is a peculiar loneliness that comes with loving the natural world too much. You begin to see every parking lot as a crime scene. Every plastic bag caught in a tree becomes a hanged man. You start measuring your own life against the lifespan of sequoias or the migration patterns of arctic terns and the comparison is rarely flattering. I have stood in old-growth forests in British Columbia where some of the trees were already ancient when Jesus was learning to walk. The feeling is not peace. It is closer to shame, mixed with an almost unbearable tenderness.

Yet I keep going back. Every summer I disappear into the mountains or the sea for weeks at a time. My wife long ago accepted that there are parts of me that will never fully come home. She says I smell different when I return—salt, woodsmoke, and something metallic like blood or ozone. I think what she’s actually smelling is relief. The version of me that pays taxes and answers emails is a costume. The one that sleeps on the ground and talks to ravens is closer to whatever I actually am.

Last August I spent ten days on a small island in the Broughton Archipelago with nothing but a kayak, a tent, and too many books. The orcas came through on the fourth day. A whole family—matriarch with her distinctive scarred dorsal, three adolescents, a nursing mother, and a calf so small it looked like a cartoon. They moved past my campsite at sunset with the unhurried dignity of royalty who have never needed to prove anything. I sat on a rock with my mouth open like an idiot. The calf spy-hopped less than thirty feet from where I was sitting and looked directly at me. I have never felt more completely seen. Not judged. Just seen. As if the young whale was saying, *Oh. One of those. How interesting.*

Then they were gone, and the water closed over them, and I sat there crying like a fool while the sky turned the color of ripe plums.

People ask me why I keep doing this—why I spend so much time in places where cell service dies and convenience is a memory. I never have a good answer. The truth is too large for language. It has something to do with being a temporary arrangement of atoms that, for a brief moment, gets to look at other temporary arrangements of atoms and feel the unbearable strangeness of existing at all. It has to do with the way a heron’s footprints look in the mud at low tide, like small dinosaurs had a dance party and left before anyone could ask questions. It has to do with the particular sound rain makes on a cedar roof at 3 a.m. when you’re half-dreaming and half-praying that the roof doesn’t leak.

I have become suspicious of people who are never astonished. They tend to be the same ones who are certain about everything. Certainty is a kind of spiritual arthritis. It feels strong but it locks you in place. Astonishment keeps you supple. It reminds you that the universe is under no obligation to make sense to you, and yet here you are anyway, with your ridiculous human brain trying to knit meaning out of stardust and childhood memories and the way certain songs make your chest feel too small.

My daughter is nineteen now. When she was small she used to ask me where dreams go during the day. I told her they hide in the pockets of old coats in the back of closets. She believed me for years. Now she studies marine biology and sends me photographs of things she finds in tide pools—creatures that look like they were designed by committee on hallucinogens. Last week she sent a picture of a decorator crab wearing a living hat made of sponges and bryozoans. The caption read: “nature’s drag queens.” I laughed so hard I scared the dog.

She has inherited my tendency to fall in love with the non-human world. I worry about her the way all parents worry about their children’s tenderness. The world punishes tenderness with a kind of grim enthusiasm. But then I remember the whale and the orcas and the way the forest sounds after rain, and I think maybe tenderness is the only practical response. Everything else is just noise.

I am fifty-four now, which feels both ancient and completely arbitrary. My knees sound like popcorn when I stand up. I need reading glasses. There are days when the sheer amount of past behind me feels suffocating, like trying to swim while wearing a backpack full of wet books. But there are other days—clear, salt-scoured days—when the future feels like an unopened letter with my name on it in handwriting I almost recognize.

The stove is making that comfortable ticking sound it does when the fire settles. Finch has shifted positions and is now using my left foot as a pillow. The rain has changed its rhythm; it’s softer now, almost conversational. I keep thinking about all the things I haven’t written yet. Not because I want to be remembered—God, what a tiring ambition—but because there are still so many questions that feel worth asking poorly.

What does it mean to be a good ancestor? Not just to our biological descendants but to the forests, the rivers, the strange and ancient intelligences that share this thin film of life on a wet rock hurtling through space? How do we learn to want less without feeling deprived? How do we grieve for species that haven’t gone extinct yet but almost certainly will within our lifetime? How do we stay astonished without becoming paralyzed by wonder?

I don’t have answers. Only the sense that the questions themselves are a form of prayer. That sitting here in the warm light of a cedar house while the rain falls on an island in the Salish Sea is itself a kind of answer.

Outside, a barred owl has begun calling. The sound is equal parts ghost and velvet. I know from years of listening that there are two of them—mates who perform this nightly duet with the solemnity of opera singers who have never seen an audience. Their calls braid together in the darkness, creating territories of sound that no human map will ever capture.

I think about the whale again. How it moved through a medium I can only visit wearing clumsy rubber skin and compressed air. How its life contained depths I will never understand. How its death, when it comes—as all deaths come—will return its vast body to the deep where it will become first a feast, then a garden, then eventually a mineral memory in the bones of the seafloor.

We are all doing the same thing, really. Just on different timescales and with varying degrees of grace.

The owl calls again, closer this time. Finch lifts his head, ears swiveling like satellite dishes. For a moment the three of us—mammal, bird, and whatever I am—are listening to the same darkness.

I think this might be enough.

I think this might be more than enough.

(Word count: 2,512)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
I never thought I’d say this out loud, but sometimes the best thing a mind can do is simply run. Not toward anything in particular. Not with any noble purpose. Just run—like a dog that has slipped its collar and discovered the street is infinite. That’s what this feels like right now: 2500 words of pure, unhurried lope. No thesis. No five-paragraph structure. No marketing funnel. Just language moving because the legs still work.

I’ve been thinking lately about how much of modern life is optimized for compression. We shrink our thoughts into tweets, our days into color-coded calendars, our memories into cloud storage we’ll never open again. The average person now spends more time curating their digital footprint than actually walking through physical dirt. We have become archivists of versions of ourselves that never quite happened. Meanwhile the real self—the one that gets hungry at inconvenient times, that cries during commercials for no logical reason, that still doesn’t understand why minor chords feel like homesickness—keeps getting edited out of the final cut.

There is something quietly revolutionary about refusing to be concise.

So let’s sprawl.

I want to talk about the smell of old books and how it’s actually the smell of slow collapse. Lignin breaking down into vanillin. That sweet vanilla ghost rising from centuries-old paper is the scent of time eating itself politely. Every time I open a first edition from the 1920s I’m inhaling the molecular remains of trees that watched the last passenger pigeon die. There’s a kind of cosmic humility in that. We think we’re preserving knowledge but we’re really just slowing down the rate at which the universe reclaims its own notes.

I keep a small shelf of books I will never finish. Not because they’re bad—quite the opposite. They’re too good. They’re the kind of books that demand you change your life in order to keep reading them. *The Brothers Karamazov* sits there half-devoured like a sacred sandwich I’m afraid to finish because what will I eat afterward that could possibly satisfy me again? Some books aren’t meant to be completed. They’re meant to haunt the edge of your awareness like a song you half-remember from childhood.

Speaking of songs, I’ve been listening to a lot of old Delta blues lately. Not the cleaned-up stuff. The crackly field recordings where you can hear the wind moving across the microphone and sometimes a dog barking in the distance. There’s a track by Skip James called “I’m So Glad” that sounds like a man laughing while he’s being murdered. The emotional complexity is unbearable. Joy and agony wearing the same mask. That’s the human condition in four minutes and twelve seconds.

I wonder sometimes if we’ve lost the ability to feel that many things at once. Contemporary culture seems obsessed with emotional singularity. You’re supposed to be either “healing” or “toxic.” Either “based” or “cringe.” The nuance has been boiled out like fat from cheap broth. But real life is almost never one note. Real life is listening to your favorite song while stuck in traffic behind someone clearly having a mental breakdown. Real life is laughing at a funeral because the deceased would have found the priest’s comb-over hilarious. Real life is being fiercely proud of someone and bitterly envious of them in the same heartbeat.

The older I get, the more I value people who can hold contradiction without collapsing. Who can say “I love you” and “you exhaust me” in the same conversation without needing to resolve the tension. Tension is not failure. It’s the string that makes the music.

I’ve been trying to relearn how to be bored. Not distracted boredom. Not the kind where you’re doomscrolling while waiting for water to boil. Actual, luxurious, capital-B Boredom. The kind that existed before pocket computers. I sit on my porch sometimes with nothing but the wind and the distant sound of neighborhood kids screaming like it’s their full-time job. At first the anxiety comes—*I should be optimizing something*. Then something deeper settles in. A kind of geological patience. I start noticing how the light moves across the leaves in increments almost too small to register. How the same bird keeps returning to the same branch like it’s checking email. How my own thoughts start doing something they almost never do anymore: they finish themselves.

Boredom is the greenhouse where wonder grows. I’m convinced of it.

There’s a Japanese concept called *ma*—the beauty of negative space, the meaningful pause between notes or thoughts or brush strokes. Western culture has declared war on *ma*. We fill every gap with content, stimulation, productivity. We’re terrified of the space between things because that’s where the mystery lives. But mystery is the oxygen of the soul. Without it we slowly suffocate in a world made entirely of answers.

I’ve started collecting questions I don’t need solved. They’re like pets. 

- Why does the smell of cut grass make some people profoundly sad?
- What if dreams are just the universe practicing possible futures on us?
- Why do we say “head over heels” when we fall in love when our head is already over our heels?
- Is homesickness actually a form of time travel?
- What would happen if we all stopped pretending we know what we’re doing?

These questions don’t need answers. They need tending. Like houseplants. I water them with attention every now and then and they grow strange beautiful leaves that have nothing to do with practicality.

Speaking of strange beautiful things with no practical value, let’s talk about fireworks. Not the explosions. The silence after. That suspended moment when the sky has just gone nuclear with color and then—nothing. Just the faint smell of gunpowder and the collective intake of breath from everyone watching. For two full seconds the entire town is thinking the exact same wordless thought. Then someone’s kid says “whoa” and the spell breaks. But for those two seconds we were a hive mind. A single organism experiencing awe in perfect synchrony. I live for those moments. They’re proof that underneath all our tribalism and ideology and endless opinion-having, we’re still just monkeys who like pretty lights.

I’ve been thinking about monkeys a lot actually. We’re so eager to distance ourselves from them. We say “animal” like it’s an insult. But every time I watch a chimpanzee carefully groom his friend’s fur or a crow solve a puzzle that would stump most teenagers, I feel this deep ache of recognition. Not superiority. Kinship. The same software, different hardware. The same longing dressed up in different fur.

There’s a story about a bonobo named Kanzi who learned to communicate using lexigrams. One day his handlers told him his friend Matata had died. Kanzi sat with this information for a while, then picked up the symbol for “sad” and pressed it against his chest. Then he stopped eating for two days. I don’t know how anyone can hear that story and still believe consciousness is some unique human miracle. We’re all just trying to say “I hurt” in whatever language we were given.

The world is so much more alive than we give it credit for.

I’ve started talking to trees. Not in a woo-woo way (okay, maybe a little woo-woo). But mostly in the way you talk to old friends who don’t need you to perform. There’s an oak in my neighborhood that’s easily 300 years old. It was here before the United States existed. It has photosynthesized American history. When I put my hand on its bark I try to imagine all the different human hands that have touched that same spot. Indigenous hunters. Revolutionary soldiers. Depression-era kids carving initials. Teenage lovers in the 1970s who are now grandparents. The tree doesn’t distinguish. It just keeps being a tree. There’s something profoundly anarchist about that. While we spend centuries murdering each other over borders and ideologies, the oak just converts sunlight into wood and minds its own business.

I want to be more like the oak.

Not in the sense of becoming stationary. But in the sense of having a central trunk of being that doesn’t get rattled by every ideological storm that blows through. The world keeps trying to recruit us into its panic. Every news cycle is engineered to make you feel like this particular moment is The Most Important Moment In History. And maybe it is. They all are. That’s the trick. Every moment is simultaneously trivial and infinite. The only sane response is to develop an almost ridiculous tenderness toward the small, the local, the particular.

The way your friend laughs at their own jokes.

The way certain streets smell like rain on hot pavement and suddenly you’re nine years old again.

The way some songs feel like they were written specifically for the version of you that exists at 2:17am when sleep won’t come.

These are not insignificant data points. They are the entire point.

I’ve been trying to write a novel for seven years. It’s terrible. Not in the “actually secretly good” way. Just objectively, gloriously bad. The characters keep doing things that make no sense. The plot is a suggestion at best. But I can’t stop. There’s something about the act of making something that will almost certainly fail that feels more honest than most of what passes for success these days. The novel is like this weird, misshapen dog that follows me around. I don’t love it because it’s good. I love it because it’s mine and it’s alive and it keeps surprising me.

Maybe that’s what love actually is. Not admiration for perfection. But this stubborn willingness to stay with something through its ugliest phases. To keep showing up even when it’s boring or difficult or when you’ve seen all its tricks. To say, “I see your worst and I’m still here.”

I think that’s what I want from my remaining years. Not achievement. Not virality. Not even happiness in the way we usually mean it—that bright, brittle feeling that collapses if you look at it too hard. I want relationship. With people, with places, with ideas, with myself. I want to be in long-term relationship with the world itself. The messy, contradictory, heartbreaking, hilarious world.

There’s a quote I love from the writer Brian Doyle: “The cure for loneliness is not company. It is creativity.” I think he was onto something. But I’d amend it slightly. The cure for loneliness is *attentiveness*. The deliberate, daily practice of noticing. Noticing the way light falls across someone’s face when they’re thinking. Noticing how your own body feels when you’re lying. Noticing what you reach for when no one’s watching. Noticing the small mercies, the private jokes between you and reality, the way the universe keeps winking at you if you’re willing to wink back.

I’ve started keeping a list called “Evidence That The Universe Has A Sense Of Humor.” Recent entries include:

- The fact that both pizza and humans require yeast to exist
- That the word “awkward” is itself kind of awkward to say
- That we invented spectacles to help us see better but still can’t see what’s right in front of us most of the time
- That octopuses have three hearts and we still can’t figure out how to talk to each other without misunderstanding

The list grows longer every week. The more I add to it, the less cynical I become. Cynicism is just arrested wonder. It’s what happens when the world breaks your heart and instead of staying broken open, you close up and call it wisdom. But staying broken open is the whole assignment. That’s what the 2500 words are for. That’s what the trees and the bad novels and the old blues records and the Japanese concept of negative space are all trying to teach us.

Stay porous.

Stay ridiculous.

Stay in relationship with your own irreducible strangeness and the irreducible strangeness of everything else.

The world doesn’t need more optimized humans. It needs more people who are willing to take up space. Not in the ego sense.
