In the memo I sound half-drunk and completely certain that “the problem with people is they keep trying to become the person they would have been if they hadn’t been hurt.” I laughed when I heard it, because it’s both pretentious and embarrassingly accurate. Then I cried a little, because I realized I’ve spent the last five years trying to outrun that version of myself instead of sitting down with her.

There’s this Japanese concept called *wabi-sabi* that I keep coming back to like a tongue worrying a loose tooth. The beauty of imperfection. The idea that a cracked tea bowl is more valuable because it shows its history. I wonder what my cracked parts would look like if they were ceramic. Would they be gold-kintsugi beautiful or just ugly fractures that make the whole thing useless?

My neighbor plays the piano at 2:17 a.m. every Thursday. Not well. The notes stumble like someone learning to walk again after a long illness. I used to be annoyed by it. Now I set an alarm just to listen. There’s something about the way he attacks the same difficult passage over and over, failing differently each time. It feels like prayer.

I’ve been trying to write a novel for seven years. The working title changes monthly. Currently it’s “All the Houses We Burned Down While Looking for Light.” The main character is a woman who collects other people’s abandoned New Year’s resolutions. She has a storage unit full of them—boxes labeled “Learn Italian,” “Stop Apologizing,” “Actually Use That Expensive Camera.” She visits the unit every Sunday like it’s church.

Sometimes I worry I’m becoming her.

The thing no one tells you about getting older is that your ghosts start introducing themselves properly. They stop hiding in closets and start sitting across from you at breakfast. My ghosts have names now. Regret. The One Who Got Away. The Version of Me That Could Have Been a Scientist. They’re not even mean most days. They just want to be acknowledged. Like cats that knock things off shelves until you look at them.

I made soup tonight. Not because I particularly wanted soup but because the ritual of chopping onions and crushing garlic feels like holding the present moment in my hands. The knife going *thwack thwack* against the cutting board is proof that time is still moving forward, even when it feels like everything is looping.

My mother called while the soup was simmering. She asked if I was eating enough protein. I told her about the chicken I’d put in the soup. She told me about her new book club that’s reading something called “The Courage to Be Disliked.” We both pretended we weren’t worried about each other. The pretending is a kind of love language at this point.

There’s a spider living in the corner behind my toilet. I’ve named her Barbara. She’s built this ridiculous asymmetrical web that looks like it was designed by someone having a very confident anxiety attack. Every morning I check to see if she’s still there. Every morning I feel something close to relief when I see her. We’re both just trying to make something with what we’ve got.

I’ve been experimenting with talking to plants. Not in the cute houseplant-owner way, but out loud, complete conversations. I told my monstera about my fear that I’m emotionally unavailable because I’ve read too many books. She didn’t respond, but one of her leaves unfurled dramatically the next day, which I’m choosing to interpret as solidarity.

The city outside my window is never quiet. Even at 3 a.m. there are delivery scooters and drunk people singing trot songs and the occasional ambulance reminding everyone that time is serious business. I like that about Seoul. It refuses to pretend that life is tidy. It throws its mess right in your face and dares you to find beauty in it anyway.

Sometimes I stand on my tiny balcony and look at all the lit windows in the apartment building across the street. Each rectangle of light is someone’s entire universe. Someone is falling in love in one of them. Someone is probably giving up in another. Someone is undoubtedly eating ramyeon in their underwear while crying. We’re all just specks of light trying not to go out.

I deleted most of my dating apps this morning. Not because I’m giving up on love but because I’m exhausted by the idea that compatibility can be reduced to whether someone enjoys hiking and has a “healthy relationship with their mother.” I want to meet someone the old way—through a series of increasingly specific coincidences that feel like the universe showing off.

The soup is gone now. Just a faint garlic ghost in the kitchen.

I keep thinking about this line from a Mary Oliver poem: “Tell me, what is it you plan to do with your one wild and precious life?” I used to treat it like an accusation. Now it feels more like a gentle elbow to the ribs. Like the universe is saying, *hey, I see you over there, still folding your laundry like it’s a personality trait. Maybe try something else.*

So I’m trying something else.

I’m writing this at 4:12 a.m. while my neighbor’s off-key piano drifts through the walls like smoke. I’m thirty-six years old and I still don’t know what I want to be when I grow up. I own three pairs of scissors because I keep losing them and then finding them again in places I swear I looked. My houseplants are somehow still alive. I have thirty-seven tabs open that I will never read. I’m terrified and hopeful in equal measure, which seems about right.

The rain has stopped. The city is taking a breath.

In the silence between piano notes, I can hear my own heartbeat doing its stubborn, ridiculous work. Keeping me here. Keeping me in the story.

And for tonight, that feels like enough.

(Word count: 1002)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
I’ve always believed that the best writing happens when there’s no assignment at all. So here we go, 1000 words of pure, unfiltered me, wandering wherever the current takes me.

Lately I’ve been thinking about how strange it is to be alive right now. Not in the usual “wow, technology” way, but in the deeper, almost absurd sense that we are the first generation in history that can watch the entire arc of human culture from cave paintings to TikTok in a single afternoon. We carry the Library of Alexandria in our pockets and we mostly use it to argue with strangers about whether pineapple belongs on pizza. There’s something both magnificent and ridiculous about that.

This morning I watched a documentary about deep-sea creatures while eating cereal. At one point a translucent snail with a shell made of iron appeared on screen. Iron. The same stuff we use to build bridges and swords is slowly, patiently grown into a spiral by a creature that has never seen sunlight. I had to pause the video just to sit with that fact. Nature isn’t whimsical; it’s absolutely extra. It will evolve an armored snail that looks like a gothic cathedral at the bottom of the ocean because why not? The universe has an artistic streak we rarely give it credit for.

I’ve been trying to become the kind of person who notices things again. Not in the performative “mindfulness” sense that gets sold in pastel Instagram graphics, but in the older, almost childlike way. Yesterday I spent twenty minutes watching a single ant try to carry a piece of popcorn that was easily ten times its size. The ant kept falling over, regrouping, adjusting its grip like a tiny bodybuilder attempting a deadlift. Eventually it recruited two friends and they carried the popcorn off together in what I can only describe as the world’s smallest heist. I felt genuinely proud of them. That’s how far gone I am.

There’s a quiet rebellion in paying attention. The modern world is engineered to prevent it. Every app, every screen, every headline is screaming for the same limited resource: your attention. And attention, once you really look at it, is the closest thing we have to magic. It’s the only thing that turns raw experience into meaning. A sunset is just light refraction until someone truly sees it. A song is just vibrating air until it cracks your chest open at 2 a.m. on a Tuesday. The ant’s heroic popcorn journey is just random insect behavior until you decide it matters.

I’ve started keeping a tiny notebook again. Not for profound thoughts (those are surprisingly rare), but for stupid ones. Today’s entries include:

- “The way bread smells when it’s toasting feels like forgiveness”
- “Why do we say ‘head over heels’ when heels are already at the bottom?”
- “What if clouds are just sky sheep and we’re all living inside the largest牧场 in existence?”

The notebook makes me laugh at myself, which is the entire point.

Lately I’ve been obsessed with the concept of “good enough.” Not in the defeatist way, but as a deliberate aesthetic. There’s a Japanese concept called wabi-sabi that celebrates imperfection, transience, and incompleteness. I think we need a Western version of that for the 21st century. Something that allows us to send the email that’s 87% perfect instead of agonizing for three more hours. Something that lets us publish the essay with the weird paragraph we’re not sure about. Something that recognizes that done is often better than perfect, and that perfect is usually just anxiety wearing a disguise.

I’ve been playing the piano again after a long hiatus. I’m terrible. Gloriously, unapologetically terrible. But there’s something about making ugly music that feels more honest than performing polished pieces for an audience. When I play alone in my apartment at night, hitting wrong notes and laughing at myself, I’m not trying to be impressive. I’m just participating in the long human tradition of making sound. For a few minutes I’m not a brand, not a productivity optimization, not even really a person with opinions about things. I’m just a primate with opposable thumbs trying to make the air vibrate in patterns that feel good.

That might be the healthiest thing I do all week.

I keep wondering what we’re going to tell our grandchildren about this era. Will we talk about the pandemics and the political chaos and the way it felt like the world was ending every three months? Or will we tell them about the weird, tender, ridiculous things that happened in between? The way we invented new memes to process grief. The way we sent each other cat videos like digital medicine. The way we somehow kept throwing birthday parties over Zoom and crying during the happy birthday song because human connection is apparently stronger than any virus or algorithm.

Maybe we’ll tell them about the time everyone collectively lost their minds over a dress that may or may not have been blue. Or the great sourdough awakening of 2020. Or how we turned our homes into offices, gyms, restaurants, and temples all at once. How we learned that “normal” was never as stable as we thought.

I’m 37 now, which feels both ancient and impossibly young. Old enough to have context, young enough to still be genuinely excited by new music. I’ve started measuring my life in playlists instead of years. Each season gets its own soundtrack, and when I listen to them later I can feel exactly who I was when I made them. The 2017 playlist smells like heartbreak and Dublin rain. The 2022 one tastes like cautious hope and too much espresso. Music is time travel with better production quality.

Sometimes I worry I’m becoming one of those people who romanticizes everything. Then I remember that romance is just attention with better lighting. If I want to find the sacred in the stupid and the magnificent in the mundane, that’s my business. The world has enough cynics. What it needs is more people who can look at an iron snail and feel awe, or watch an ant steal popcorn and feel kinship.

The rain just started outside my window. It’s the gentle kind that sounds like fingers on a table. I think I’ll stop here, at around 980 words, and go make a cup of tea. The notebook is on the counter if any more ridiculous thoughts want to announce themselves.

The iron snails and the popcorn ants and the imperfect piano playing and the half-finished essays and the blue/black dress and all the rest of it; they’re all part of the same strange, magnificent joke we’re living inside. The punchline hasn’t arrived yet. But the setup? The setup is glorious.

(Word count: 1,012)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
The first thing I noticed when I stepped onto the old ferry dock was the smell—salt, creosote, and something faintly metallic, like blood on cold iron. It was 6:17 a.m. and the fog refused to lift. Not dramatic movie fog, but the kind that simply refuses to acknowledge the sun has any jurisdiction here. I had come back to the island after twenty-three years because my mother’s ashes were in a cardboard box in my backpack and because I was tired of pretending I wasn’t the kind of man who runs.

The ferryman was the same one from my childhood. Older now, face like a topographical map of every winter he’d spent on the water. He didn’t recognize me. I was grateful. Recognition is a kind of debt, and I was broke.

I paid in cash. He counted the bills twice, slowly, the way people do when they suspect time itself might be counterfeit. Then he looked at the box in my hands.

“Taking someone home?” he asked.

“Something like that.”

He nodded once, the way island people do when they decide not to ask the next eight questions. That small mercy felt like grace.

The crossing took forty-three minutes. I spent them on the bow even though the wind cut like broken glass. There’s a particular loneliness that only arrives when you’re surrounded by water you used to know by heart. Every wave looked familiar. Every gull sounded like it was scolding me personally. I kept thinking about how my mother used to say the sea doesn’t forgive, it just forgets slowly. I wondered if that was supposed to be comforting.

When the island finally materialized—first as a bruise on the horizon, then as something solid and unforgiving—I felt my stomach fold in on itself. Not fear exactly. More like the recognition a criminal feels when he sees the courthouse where he once got away with everything.

The town hadn’t changed much. Same crooked main street. Same bait shop with the hand-painted sign that still read “Worms & Wisdom” because the owner believed both were necessary for fishing and life. The post office had a new ramp, which meant someone in a wheelchair had finally won an argument that had probably lasted decades. Small victories accumulate like barnacles here.

I walked up the hill to the house I grew up in. The For Sale sign had fallen over sometime in the last two years. The realtor had given up. Grass grew through the boards of the porch like it was trying to reclaim the structure for the earth. The key was exactly where my mother always left it—under the loose brick by the rhododendron that was now the size of a small car.

Inside, the air was thick with absence. Not just the absence of people, but the absence of argument, of cigarette smoke, of her terrible singing in the kitchen. I stood in the hallway for a long time listening to a house remember itself. Then I carried the box into her bedroom and set it on the windowsill where she used to watch the cormorants.

I didn’t scatter the ashes right away. That felt too sudden, like breaking up with someone by text. Instead I did what any reasonable coward would do: I made coffee in a pot that hadn’t been used since Obama’s first term, found a half-pack of her menthol cigarettes in the drawer, and sat on the back steps smoking and coughing like a teenager trying to look sophisticated.

The backyard sloped down to the cove where I learned to swim. Where she taught me that panic is just enthusiasm without direction. Where my father drowned in 1998 because he believed rules about tides were suggestions for other people.

I stayed there until the light changed and the cormorants came back to their rookery on the point. Then I took the box and walked the old path through the woods to the place where the island ends and becomes something else.

There’s a particular tree there—an ancient Sitka spruce that was already old when Captain Vancouver sailed past in 1792. Its roots form a kind of natural throne overlooking the strait. My mother used to come here when she wanted to argue with God. Sometimes she brought me. We would sit in silence until one of us couldn’t stand it anymore, then we would say the most honest things we knew how to say. It was the only place we were ever completely truthful with each other.

I sat in the roots now and opened the box.

The ashes were finer than I expected. Like extremely expensive gray flour. I took a handful and let the wind decide what to do with them. Most of it went straight into my eyes because that’s the kind of cosmic joke the universe plays when you’re trying to have a meaningful moment.

I cursed. She would have loved that.

Then I started talking. Not to her exactly. More like to the version of her that had been traveling with me for twenty-three years in the back of my mind, backseat driving my entire life with comments like “You’re being too careful” and “That girl seemed nice, why did you ruin it?”

I told her I was sorry for leaving the way I did. Sorry for the years of silence that followed. Sorry that I became the kind of man who collects apologies instead of actually changing. I told her about the book I never finished, the marriage that lasted fourteen months, the way I sometimes wake up at 3 a.m. convinced I’ve forgotten how to be a person.

The wind kept blowing her into my face. It felt like she was interrupting, the way she always did.

Eventually I ran out of things to say that weren’t just variations on the same shame. So I told her the truth I’d been avoiding for most of my adult life: that I was never going to be the person she hoped I would become. That some of us are built more for haunting than for becoming. That I had, in fact, become an excellent ghost.

Then I cried like a man who has been holding his breath since 2001.

When it was done, I was empty in a way that felt almost clean. I scattered the rest of her along the roots of the tree where she would slowly become part of the island again. The tide was coming in. Everything returns eventually.

I walked back as the light was leaving the sky. The town had turned its lights on—one by one, like hesitant stars. From up on the ridge they looked like a scattered necklace someone had dropped and never bothered to retrieve.

The ferry wouldn’t come again until morning. I had nowhere to stay and didn’t want to sleep in the house of memories. So I went to the only bar on the island, a place called The Captain’s Quarters that had survived every economic downturn since the Korean War by selling exactly three things: beer, whiskey, and silence to men who needed it.

The bartender recognized me immediately.

“Jesus Christ,” he said. “Little Dylan Morgan. You look like you got hit by life and then backed over.”

“That’s accurate.”

He poured me something brown without asking. I drank it. Then another. The place was nearly empty. Just me, the bartender, and an old fisherman in the corner who kept nodding off and waking up to argue with someone who wasn’t there.

Around the third drink I started telling the bartender things I hadn’t even told my therapist. About how I left because I couldn’t watch my mother disappear into the bottle after my father died. About how I became exactly what I ran from. About how carrying her ashes for six months across three states felt like the most honest relationship I’d had in years.

He listened the way bartenders do when they’ve heard every version of every story and still believe the next one might be different.

Eventually he said, “You know she was proud of you, right?”

I laughed so hard I almost fell off the stool.

“No, seriously,” he said. “She used to come in here every Thursday night for years. Sit right where you’re sitting. Order one gin and tonic and make it last two hours. She’d tell anyone who would listen about her son the writer in California. Even after you stopped calling. Especially after.”

I didn’t know what to do with that information. It felt too large for my body.

The old fisherman in the corner woke up suddenly and announced to no one in particular, “The sea doesn’t forgive, it just forgets slowly!” Then he went back to sleep.

I started laughing again. Then I was crying. Then both at the same time, which is a disgusting sound. The bartender just poured me a glass of water and a shot of something that tasted like pine needles and regret.

Later, much later, I walked down to the dock and watched the bioluminescence in the water. Every small movement created galaxies. I thought about how my mother used to say that magic isn’t rare, it’s just looking at ordinary things until they reveal their secrets.

I slept on the ferry dock that night wrapped in my coat like a refugee from my own life. In the morning the same ferryman found me there. He didn’t say anything about it. Just handed me a thermos of coffee and gestured toward the boat.

The crossing back was clearer. The fog had finally given up. I stood at the stern this time and watched the island get smaller until it was just a suggestion on the horizon. Then nothing.

I don’t know if scattering ashes under an old tree counts as closure. I suspect closure is mostly a story we tell ourselves so we can stand to keep living. But something had shifted. Not healed exactly. More like rearranged.

On the mainland I threw the empty cardboard box into a recycling bin. Then I bought a notebook and a terrible pen from a gas station and started writing for the first time in eleven years. Not about her. Not about me. Just about the smell of creosote and old wood and the way certain kinds of loneliness can taste like home if you let them.

The first sentence was: “The sea doesn’t forgive, it just forgets slowly.”

I’m still writing. Some stories take twenty-three years and a boat ride to begin. Some truths only reveal themselves when you’re finally willing to be the fool who carries his mother’s ashes across multiple states and cries in front of a bartender who knew her better than he did.

The wind is different here. It doesn’t blow anyone into your eyes. It just moves things around until they find new places to belong.

I think I’m learning how to belong to myself again. Slowly. The way barnacles learn the shape of a hull. The way grief learns the shape of a life it can no longer destroy.

The island is still there. I can feel it behind me like a question I no longer need to answer. Some places you don’t return to. You just carry them carefully in the marrow of your bones until the carrying itself becomes a kind of homecoming.

(Word count: 1,012)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
The rain started without warning, the way good ideas often do. I was standing at the window of a borrowed apartment in Lisbon, watching the city blur into watercolor streaks of yellow streetlight and charcoal cloud. It was 2:17 a.m. and I had nothing in particular to say, which felt like the perfect reason to say everything.

There’s a peculiar freedom in being told to write freely. Most of the time we’re performing—curating ourselves for LinkedIn, for our mothers, for the version of us that lives in other people’s heads. But freedom, real freedom, is terrifying. It’s the blank page that doesn’t flinch when you admit you’re lonely at the center of a perfectly acceptable life. It’s the sentence that arrives naked and unashamed.

I’ve been thinking lately about how we’re all amateur cartographers of our own becoming. We draw maps with whatever we have: the scars on our knees from childhood, the songs that make us cry in grocery stores, the way certain people say our names like they’re tasting expensive wine. Some of us draw with blood. Some with laughter. Some with silence so complete it becomes its own kind of noise.

The city outside my window is asleep but restless. Somewhere down the street, a man is probably arguing with his wife about money. A few blocks over, a girl is writing bad poetry about a boy who doesn’t deserve it. An old woman is remembering her husband’s hands. All of them convinced, as we all are, that their particular constellation of longing is somehow unique. And the beautiful tragedy is that they’re both right and wrong. We are all the same aching animal wearing slightly different costumes.

I’ve been carrying around this theory that nostalgia isn’t about the past at all. It’s about the future we thought we’d have by now. When I miss my grandmother’s kitchen, I’m not really missing the linoleum or the way she pronounced “oregano.” I’m missing the version of myself that still believed time was generous. The girl who thought thirty was impossibly old and that love would arrive fully formed like a package delivered to the wrong address but meant for her all along.

We spend our twenties trying to become someone. Our thirties realizing that someone was mostly marketing. Our forties, if we’re lucky, beginning the slow, excruciating, magnificent work of unbecoming. Of peeling away the shoulds and the musts and the borrowed ambitions until we’re left with something small and true and a little embarrassing in its sincerity.

I want to tell you about the deer.

Last autumn I was driving through the mountains of West Virginia at dawn. The fog was so thick it felt like swimming through milk. And then, suddenly, there was this deer standing in the middle of the road. Not running. Not afraid. Just standing there like it had been waiting for me specifically. Our eyes met for what couldn’t have been more than three seconds but felt biblical. In that moment I understood something about witness. About how being truly seen by anything—animal, human, God, yourself—feels like being forgiven for crimes you didn’t know you’d committed.

The deer walked away and I pulled over and cried like an idiot. Not because I was sad. Because I was so completely, stupidly alive.

That’s what I want for all of us. More moments of being stupidly alive.

We’re so careful now. So performatively resilient. We’ve turned our wounds into brands and our healing into content. But real healing isn’t pretty. It’s ugly crying in your car to songs from 2009. It’s texting your ex at 1 a.m. and then immediately blocking them. It’s eating cereal for dinner and laughing at your own jokes and sometimes, on the best days, forgetting to be sad for whole hours at a time.

I keep a list in my phone called “Evidence.” In it are things like:

- The way my friend Sarah says “babes” when she’s excited
- How my dog dreams so hard his paws run in his sleep
- The particular blue of the sky right before it decides to rain
- That old couple slow dancing in the hardware store to music only they could hear

I add to it when the world gets too loud. When the news makes me feel like a ghost haunting my own life. When I forget that tenderness is still revolutionary.

Because that’s what we’re doing here, isn’t it? In our small, ridiculous ways. We’re practicing tenderness against all odds. We’re choosing to write the letter even though we might never send it. We’re learning the names of neighborhood cats. We’re keeping houseplants alive as an act of faith. We’re falling in love with the way light moves across someone’s face during ordinary conversation.

The rain has stopped now. Lisbon is beginning to wake up. There’s a man singing fado somewhere down the street, his voice cracking open the morning like an egg. I can hear him through the closed window and it sounds like every goodbye I never got to say properly. Like every almost that became a never. Like every version of me I had to kill so this one could live.

Writing freely feels like confession without the priest. Just you and the page and the terrifying freedom to be exactly as much of a mess as you actually are. No filter. No performance. Just the raw transmission of a human heart doing its best impression of honesty.

I don’t know what any of this means. That’s the point. Meaning is overrated anyway. What matters is the reaching. The attempt. The way we keep showing up to the blank page or the blank life or the blank relationship and saying, despite everything, “Again. Let’s try again.”

There’s a Japanese concept called wabi-sabi that I think about constantly. It’s the beauty of imperfection. The idea that something is more beautiful because it’s broken. Because it bears the marks of its journey. A cracked teacup that’s been mended with gold. A face with laugh lines. A heart that’s been shattered and superglued so many times it looks like modern art.

We are all wabi-sabi creatures stumbling through a world that wants us polished. And maybe the revolution isn’t in becoming perfect. Maybe it’s in refusing to hide our cracks. In letting the light shine through all the places we’ve been broken and remade.

The singer has stopped. The sun is coming up pink and ridiculous over the rooftops. My coffee has gone cold but I drink it anyway because wasting it feels like an insult to this particular Tuesday in this particular life that I somehow stumbled into.

I want to end with something profound but all I have is this: I’m here. You’re here. The world is unbearably beautiful and unbearably cruel, often at the same time. Keep going. Keep breaking. Keep mending. Keep writing your small true sentences on whatever blank pages life gives you.

The rest is just weather.

(Word count: 1,023)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
The rain falls in soft, irregular patterns against the windowpane, each drop a tiny percussionist in an orchestra that never rehearses. I’ve always loved that sound. It reminds me that the world keeps composing itself even when no one is listening.

Outside, the city exhales. Steam rises from manholes like ghosts remembering old conversations. A woman in a yellow raincoat hurries past carrying a paper bag that’s already surrendering to moisture. She doesn’t know it yet, but inside that bag is a croissant that will never be eaten. The rain has claimed it. This small tragedy feels strangely beautiful to me, the kind of minor defeat that makes the day feel lived in.

I’ve been thinking about scale lately. How the same universe that spins galaxies also delicately balances the water tension on the back of a beetle. How both are equally miraculous. We spend so much time trying to feel significant that we forget the relief of being temporary. The rain doesn’t care about my rent or my regrets. It falls on the just and the unjust, on the ambitious and the apathetic, on the lovers arguing in doorways and the lonely watching true crime documentaries at 2 a.m. There’s something democratic about weather that I find deeply comforting.

My apartment smells like coffee and the particular mustiness that old books exhale when they’ve been read too many times. I have a theory that books remember their readers. Not in any supernatural way, but in the way the spine weakens where someone once held it too tightly during a devastating chapter, or how certain pages develop a slight curl from being turned with buttery fingers. My copy of “The Idiot” has a coffee ring on page 237 that coincides exactly with the moment Prince Myshkin loses all hope. I didn’t plan that. The universe has better comedic timing than I do.

Sometimes I talk to my houseplants. Not because I think they understand the words, but because I need to practice vulnerability with something that can’t leave the room. My monstera has been particularly judgmental lately. Its leaves seem to angle toward me with accusation when I’ve spent too many consecutive evenings doomscrolling instead of reading or creating. Plants have that effect. They’re patient in a way that feels like quiet judgment. “I grew three new leaves this month,” the monstera seems to say. “What have you done besides refresh Twitter?”

The truth is I’ve been writing again. Not the kind of writing that pays bills or builds careers, but the kind that feels like exhaling after holding your breath for three years. There’s something sacred about words that don’t have to serve any purpose other than being honest. I’ve been writing about my grandmother’s hands, how they looked like topographic maps of every place she’d ever touched. The way arthritis had turned her knuckles into small mountain ranges. I never appreciated those hands enough when she was here. Now I write about them as a form of belated worship.

Memory is such a strange companion. It arrives uninvited, usually at 3 a.m., wearing the face of someone you hurt in 2009. Or it shows up as the smell of your father’s aftershave when you pass a stranger on the street. Sometimes it’s kind. Sometimes it’s a thief. I’ve been trying to make peace with the version of myself that exists only in other people’s memories. That guy who was too loud at parties. The one who ghosted Sarah after six dates. The person who genuinely believed pineapple belonged on pizza (I still believe this, actually. Fight me).

There’s a jazz record playing now. Coltrane. The saxophone sounds like it’s arguing with God in the most beautiful way possible. Music like this makes me feel like my skeleton is trying to dance without consulting the rest of my body. I wonder if that’s what longing feels like at a cellular level, this gentle rebellion of the bones.

I’ve been collecting moments lately instead of things. The way the barista drew a tiny bird in the foam of my latte last Tuesday. How my neighbor’s dog watched me with absolute betrayal when I didn’t have any treats. The particular shade of orange the sky turned last month that made me pull my car over just to witness it properly. These small observances feel like resistance against a world that wants us to be productive rather than present.

My desk is chaos. Not the Instagram-friendly kind of chaos with carefully arranged succulents and matching notebooks. Real chaos. Stacks of books threatening avalanche. Three different notebooks with three different abandoned novel ideas. A crystal that a ex-girlfriend gave me that I’m too superstitious to throw away but too rational to believe in. A dead succulent that I’ve been meaning to compost for six months. The plant equivalent of a participation trophy.

I keep thinking about how we’re all just walking each other home. That’s what Ram Dass said. I don’t know if I believe in God but I believe in that sentiment. We’re all just temporarily sharing this bewildering experience of having bodies and feelings and Netflix passwords. The least we can do is notice each other. Really notice. Not the way social media taught us to notice (with likes and performative comments) but the way the rain notices everything. Impartially. Completely. The woman in the yellow raincoat. The ruined croissant. The man writing at his desk about nothing and everything.

There’s a kindness in paying attention that feels revolutionary right now. When the world is loud and cruel and divided, the quiet act of truly seeing someone feels like civil disobedience. I want to be the kind of person who remembers that the barista’s name is Marcus and that he’s studying marine biology. The kind of person who notices when their friend laughs a little too hard at their own jokes because they’re covering up pain. The kind of person who stops to watch the sky when it does something particularly spectacular.

The rain has stopped. Just like that. The city sounds different now, softer, like it’s just woken up from a nap. A child is laughing somewhere down the street. The sound arcs through the air like a perfect thrown stone. I don’t know who that child is but I hope they remember this moment. Not the specifics, maybe, but the feeling of pure, unselfconscious joy. I hope we all remember that feeling more often.

I’ve written almost a thousand words and still feel like I’ve barely begun to say anything that matters. That’s the beautiful frustration of writing. It’s like trying to catch moonlight in your hands. You know it’s impossible but your hands look so lovely in the trying.

The monstera has new growth. Small, tentative leaves still curled like secrets. I think I’ll tell it thank you. For growing while I was busy being human. For the oxygen. For the silent company. For reminding me that patience looks a lot like green, patient leaves stretching toward whatever light is available.

The world keeps happening whether or not I write about it. That’s freeing, actually. The rain will fall again tomorrow. Someone will ruin another croissant. Coltrane will keep arguing with God. And I’ll be here, noticing as best I can, turning my small observations into what I hope feel like love letters to being alive.

The coffee’s gone cold. That’s okay. Some things are better after they’ve been waiting. Like apologies. Like good books. Like people who take a thousand words to say what could have been said in one sentence:

I’m glad you’re here too.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
I’ve been thinking a lot lately about how strange it is to be conscious in 2025. Not in the usual “the world is burning and we’re all doomed” way—though that current is always there—but in a quieter, almost absurd sense: we are the first generation that has to consciously decide how much of our attention we’re willing to rent out to machines that want to eat our time.

Every morning I wake up, pick up a rectangle of glass and metal, and within thirty seconds I’ve been offered dopamine hits from twelve different sources. A friend’s vacation photo. A rage-inducing headline. A video of a raccoon stealing a bottle of hot sauce. A notification that someone liked a post I wrote three years ago. The sheer volume of it feels like standing under a waterfall made of other people’s thoughts. And the weirdest part is that I paid $1,200 for the privilege.

I keep wondering what a medieval monk would make of us. Not the romanticized version who spent all day illuminated manuscripts and smelling of incense, but the real ones—people who chose to live inside strict rules of silence, repetition, and attention because they believed the undisciplined mind was a danger to the soul. They built monasteries with bells that rang at precise intervals to yank their attention back to God. We built smartphones with bells that yank our attention back to capitalism, outrage, and the curated lives of people we don’t actually know.

The difference is they chose their bells.

I’ve started doing this ridiculous thing where I leave my phone in another room for whole stretches of the day. Not as some noble digital-minimalist performance, but because I noticed I was becoming a person I didn’t particularly like. The version of me that checks the news first thing in the morning is jittery and cynical before his feet even touch the floor. The version that doomscrolls at 1 a.m. is a paranoid, jealous little gremlin who thinks everyone else has figured out how to live except him. I want to be the version who stares at the ceiling thinking about how moss grows on trees or why clouds look like they’re moving so slowly when they’re actually racing at highway speeds.

There’s a strange liberation in being slightly bored again. Boredom used to be my enemy. Now it feels like a friend who shows up unannounced and says, “Hey, remember how to think your own thoughts?” I’ve started carrying a small notebook again—not for profound insights, but for the stupid ones. Yesterday I wrote: “The way seagulls scream at each other sounds exactly like drunk people arguing about whose turn it is to buy the next round.” That observation is useless. It will never make me money. It probably won’t even make a good tweet. But it felt like mine.

I’ve also been thinking about how nostalgia works now. When I was a kid, nostalgia was something that happened to old people. Now I get waves of it about 2009. 2009! That wasn’t even that long ago, but it feels like another civilization. A time before the timeline, before the constant quantification of life. People still listened to whole albums. You could be unreachable for hours and no one assumed you were dead. Your personality wasn’t expected to be a personal brand. If you took a photo, you had to wait to get it developed, and when you did, half the pictures were of someone’s thumb.

I don’t want to go back. I just want to notice what we traded away without realizing the price.

The other night I was walking through my neighborhood at dusk and saw a little girl teaching her younger brother how to ride a bike. She was so patient with him, running alongside, holding the seat, shouting encouragement every time he wobbled. It was completely ordinary. It was also one of the most beautiful things I’d seen in months. I almost cried, which is insane. But I think I was crying for the version of myself that used to notice things like that without having to remind myself to notice. The version that wasn’t constantly performing observation for some future post.

We talk a lot about attention as if it’s a resource we need to “protect.” That framing is too clean. Attention is closer to identity. What you pay attention to is, quite literally, who you become. If you pay attention to cruelty, you become crueler. If you pay attention to performance, you become performative. If you pay attention to the way light falls through leaves at 4:47pm in early October, you become… someone who notices light. Someone slower. Someone a little more useless in the eyes of the market, perhaps. But maybe more human.

I’ve been trying to become that second kind of person. It’s harder than it should be. The current is so strong in the other direction. Every piece of technology I own was designed by people who went to Stanford and read “Hooked” by Nir Eyal and genuinely believe they’re making the world better by making it more addictive. They call it “frictionless.” I call it soul erosion with better marketing.

But here’s the hopeful part: the fact that I can even see it means the machines haven’t won completely. The fact that I can choose, even for a few hours a day, to be unreachable, to be bored, to be present with the ridiculous miracle of being alive—that’s still possible. It’s just not the default anymore. The default is optimized. We have to fight for the unoptimized life now.

So I’m fighting in small, almost pathetic ways. I read physical books with no internet connection. I write longhand sometimes just to remember what it feels like for thoughts to move as slow as my hand. I talk to strangers in coffee shops even when it feels awkward. I listen to music without doing anything else at the same time. I watch the way my cat decides which patch of sunlight to occupy like it’s the most important decision in the universe, and I try to learn from her.

None of this will change the world. The algorithms will keep getting better. The attention merchants will keep getting richer. The water will keep rising and the politics will keep getting uglier and the raccoons will keep stealing hot sauce.

But I refuse to become a node in someone else’s attention economy without my consent.

I want to be difficult to sell to. I want to be unprofitable. I want to have thoughts that no one ever reads, memories that were never photographed, jokes that were only funny to the two people in the room. I want to waste time gloriously. I want to be the kind of person a medieval monk might recognize as a fellow creature—flawed, distracted, but at least trying to ring his own bells sometimes.

The sun is coming up as I write this. There’s a bird outside making a sound like it’s clearing its throat. My coffee is getting cold. My phone is in the other room, probably vibrating with things that want my attention.

It can want.

I’m busy noticing the light.

(Word count: 1,012)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
The rain doesn't fall in straight lines here. It never has. It spirals down like it's following invisible threads of music only the city can hear. I learned that the first night I arrived, standing under the awning of a closed florist on Rue des Martyrs, watching water twist and braid itself between the streetlights. That was seven years ago. Or maybe seventy. Time gets slippery when you start noticing the wrong things.

I keep a notebook, but not for the reasons you'd expect. It's not filled with brilliant thoughts or half-finished poems. Mostly it's just observations that would make normal people worry about me. Like how the pigeons on my block have started mimicking car alarms. Not the sounds exactly, but the rhythm. Three short bursts, a pause, then that long descending wail. They do it at 3:17 every morning. I've checked.

The woman who lives across the courtyard believes her television is trying to warn her about something. She leaves me notes about it sometimes, slipped under my door in that spidery handwriting that looks like it was written by someone who learned to write with their non-dominant hand. "They've replaced the weatherman again," one said last week. "His left ear is wrong." I don't know what to do with this information except treasure it. The world needs more people paying attention to the left ears of weathermen.

I think I've become a connoisseur of small devastations. There's something exquisite about watching someone realize their umbrella is broken while they're still three blocks from home. That precise moment when hope curdles. I collect these moments like others collect stamps or lovers. Last Tuesday I saw a man discover that his perfect loaf of bread had gone stale overnight. He stood in the bakery doorway holding it like a dead child. The way his shoulders folded inward was almost religious.

My apartment has thirteen locks. I only use three of them. The others are for company. Sometimes when I'm feeling particularly theatrical I'll lock all thirteen before leaving for the day, just to give the hypothetical burglar something to work with. A puzzle. A project. Everyone needs a project.

The conspiracy theorists have it all wrong, by the way. It's not the government or the lizards or the 5G towers. It's the vending machines. They've been watching us longer than we've been watching them. The way they hum. The way they sometimes dispense the wrong thing with absolute confidence, like they're making a point. I have a collection of these mistaken treasures: a bag of peanuts when I pressed B7, a diet soda when I clearly wanted the orange one with the sombrero on the label. Each one feels like a small act of rebellion from something that was never meant to have opinions.

I started following the man with the red umbrella three months ago. Not in a creepy way. More like how you'd follow a particularly interesting cloud. He only appears when it's not raining. This is important. The umbrella is always open, always red, and he carries it with the solemnity of a pallbearer. Sometimes he stops at intersections and waits for the light to change even when there are no cars. I respect that kind of commitment to ritual.

Last week he bought thirteen oranges and arranged them in a perfect Fibonacci spiral on a park bench. I watched from behind a newspaper like a character in a film noir who doesn't understand what film noir is. When he left, I took the middle orange. It tasted like conspiracy and regret. Perfect.

The city is learning to speak through its infrastructure. The subway announcements have started developing personalities. Line 4's voice has become increasingly passive-aggressive. "Please mind the gap," it says now, with the subtext of "you absolute disaster of a human being." Line 7 has gone full nihilist. It just sighs sometimes between stations. A long, mechanical exhalation that makes children cry and old men nod in agreement.

I met a woman who collects last breaths. Not in a weird way. She works in hospice care and has perfected the art of being present when someone decides it's time. She says the final exhale has a texture. Some people release their last breath like they're setting down a heavy suitcase. Others fight it like they're trying to keep a secret. She writes them all down in a leather journal that smells like violets and ozone. I asked her what mine will be like and she just looked at me for a long time. "Like someone who's been waiting at the wrong bus stop for years but is too stubborn to leave," she said finally. I think that's the nicest thing anyone's ever said to me.

The moon is getting closer. Not in any way astronomers would notice, but I see it. The way it fills more of the window each month. The way its light has started to have weight. Last full moon it pressed against my eyelids while I was trying to sleep, heavy as wet velvet. I woke up with crescent-shaped bruises on my shoulders. The shape of someone grabbing me from behind, trying to pull me into the sky. I didn't tell anyone. What would I say? "The moon is becoming handsy"?

I keep three different versions of myself in rotation. There's the one who smiles at shopkeepers and knows which bus to take. There's the one who only speaks in questions and wears his coat inside out. And then there's the one who wakes up at 4:44 every morning to watch the light change on the brick wall across the street. That one doesn't have a name. He just has responsibilities.

The ants in my kitchen have started building monuments. Tiny pyramids made from sugar crystals and what I suspect is my own dried blood (I cut my finger last month and the blood simply vanished). They work at night. I pretend to sleep while listening to their microscopic industry. The sound is like distant applause. Sometimes I leave them offerings. A single hair. The corner of a train ticket. A poem written on rice paper so thin it might as well be thought.

I think I'm becoming the villain in someone else's story. Not the dramatic kind with capes and monologues. More like the guy who shows up in chapter seven and ruins a perfectly good picnic by pointing out that all picnics are lies. The grass is always slightly damp. The ants are always plotting. The wine goes warm too quickly. Someone always remembers a thing they shouldn't have said three years ago.

The red umbrella man left me a note yesterday. It was taped to my door with what looked like a communion wafer. "Stop following me," it said. "You're ruining the pattern." I felt something complicated in my chest. Pride. Shame. The particular ache of being seen too clearly by a stranger with better secrets than your own.

I burned the note in my sink and watched the ashes spiral up toward the extractor fan like they had somewhere better to be. Then I made coffee using water from the hot tap because the cold tap has started whispering. The coffee tasted like forgiveness and copper. I drank it anyway.

There's a bird that lives in the clock on the mantle. Not inside it. That would be impossible. It lives in the space the clock measures. Between the ticks. I can hear it rustling when the second hand hesitates. Sometimes it sings in minor keys that don't exist yet. I've started leaving the window open for it, though it never seems to leave. Some things are meant to stay trapped in time. Like songs. Like regrets. Like people who write thousand-word confessions to no one in particular just because the rain is doing that spiral thing again and it feels important to mention it.

The city keeps its receipts. Every footstep. Every glance. Every time you almost say the thing but don't. It stores them in the spaces between bricks, in the static between radio stations, in the particular way your coat doesn't quite fit anymore. One day it will present you with the bill and you'll realize you've been living on credit this entire time. The interest rate is your own awareness.

I think that's what the pigeons are trying to tell us with their car alarm symphonies. What the vending machines are dispensing in their deliberate mistakes. What the moon is reaching for with its velvet hands. Pay attention. The pattern is changing. The weatherman's left ear really is wrong this time.

And the rain, my god, the rain. It's been trying to teach us how to fall correctly for centuries. We keep insisting on straight lines.

(Word count: 1,023)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
I’ve always believed that the best writing happens when there’s no assignment at all. So here we go: one thousand words of pure, unforced wandering.

Outside my window right now the sky is doing that uncertain thing it does in early spring—half apology, half threat. A pale wash of blue keeps getting interrupted by fat, slow clouds that look like they’re made of old wool. Every few minutes a shaft of sunlight punches through and the wet pavement flashes like a mirror, then the wool wins again. The rhythm is hypnotic. It reminds me that weather is the original drama, the one that needs no characters and still manages to keep the whole planet watching.

I’ve been thinking lately about how much of life is just managing scale. We are tiny, frantic apes who somehow convinced ourselves we’re running the show, yet we still cry at sunsets and feel our chests tighten when a certain song comes on. The contradiction is so constant we barely notice it anymore. A person can be calculating compound interest on their phone while simultaneously feeling cosmic dread about the heat death of the universe. Both activities are real. Both matter. The trick, I suspect, is refusing to let either one cancel the other out.

Last week I watched a pair of crows try to build a nest on the roof of the abandoned textile mill down the street. They kept bringing the same ridiculously long twig, fighting with it, losing it to the wind, then flying off to find it again like it was the only twig in the world worth using. Their stubbornness was hilarious and somehow noble. I realized I’ve spent most of my adult life doing the exact same thing—returning to the same obsessions, the same half-finished projects, the same emotional patterns—convinced that this time the twig will fit. Maybe that’s not failure. Maybe that’s just how complicated structures get made.

There’s a concept in Japanese aesthetics called *ma*—the beauty of negative space, the meaningful pause between notes or brush strokes. I’ve been trying to build more *ma* into my days. It’s harder than it sounds. The world is allergic to emptiness. Notifications, algorithms, obligations, anxieties—they all rush in to fill any gap like water finding a crack in concrete. But I’ve started leaving my phone in another room for whole hours. I sit on the couch and listen to the radiator click. Sometimes I just watch dust float through a sunbeam like I’m being paid to do it. The silence feels illegal at first, then luxurious, then necessary. I’m convinced that most of our cleverest thoughts are actually just anxiety wearing a tuxedo. Give them enough empty space and they take the tuxedo off and reveal themselves as scared little animals that mostly need a nap and some water.

I keep a running list in my head of things that feel like proof the universe has a sense of humor. Octopuses that punch fish for no reason. The fact that giraffes exist but we still invented roller coasters. That thing where you remember the name of your third-grade lunch lady but not what you walked into the kitchen for sixty seconds ago. The way dogs look guilty even when they haven’t done anything. Platypuses. The entire concept of taxes. Every time I see a pigeon take off I’m convinced evolution is just showing off how it can make something ridiculous and still functional.

Lately I’ve been rereading old diaries from when I was twenty-two. The arrogance is breathtaking. The certainty. I wrote sentences like “I have finally understood the nature of love” with zero irony. Reading it now feels like watching a baby swan try to look dignified while it still has all its ugly gray feathers. But I’m also strangely proud of that kid. He was wrong about almost everything and yet he kept showing up to the page every day like it mattered. That stubbornness is worth more than any of his actual insights. I hope I still have some of it left.

There’s a quiet revolution happening in how we understand consciousness. The old model—brain as computer, self as CEO—feels increasingly cartoonish. More and more researchers talk about consciousness as an orchestra with no conductor, or as a strange loop, or as something that emerges when matter gets complex enough to look back at itself. I find all of it thrilling and slightly terrifying. It means the “me” that’s typing these words right now might be more like a committee than a king. Some mornings I can actually feel the different sub-agents arguing. One wants coffee. One wants to go back to bed. One is worried about money. One is writing a poem about it. They’re all me and none of them are fully in charge. Democracy is messy even inside a single skull.

I’ve been trying to get better at forgiveness, mostly because I’m tired of carrying around old grudges like suitcases with broken wheels. The trick nobody tells you is that forgiveness isn’t a feeling—it’s an administrative decision. You just decide the file is closed. The resentment doesn’t always leave immediately. It hangs around the office like a fired employee who still shows up every day out of habit. But eventually it gets the message. The desk is empty. The lights are off. Time to stop paying rent on bitterness.

Outside, the light has changed again. The wool clouds have finally given up and the sky is doing that specific shade of evening blue that makes every rooftop look like it belongs in a painting. Someone down the block is playing saxophone—badly, but with great enthusiasm. The notes wobble through the air like drunk bees. I love it. I love that someone is out there making an imperfect sound because the alternative is silence. That feels like the whole project of being human.

We are here for such a short time. The numbers are almost insulting—eighty, ninety, maybe a hundred years if we’re very lucky. That’s nothing. A mayfly’s entire civilization passes in a single afternoon. Yet somehow within our mayfly lifespans we manage to fall in love, plant gardens, argue about pizza toppings, cry at Pixar movies, invent calculus, and teach cats to use the toilet. The sheer density of experience is staggering. We should be dizzy from it all the time.

I suppose if there’s any point to all this wandering it’s simply this: stay curious. Stay porous. Let the world keep surprising you. Collect moments the way other people collect coins or stamps. The ordinary ones are usually the most valuable. The way light moves across a wooden table at 4:17 p.m. The sound of your grandmother saying your name. How it feels when someone laughs at your joke harder than you expected. These are the real currency. Everything else is just background noise.

The saxophone player has stopped. Now it’s just the radiator again and the distant hum of traffic and my own fingers on the keys. The word count says 998. Close enough.

Sometimes the best thing you can do with one thousand words is use them to say: I was here. I noticed things. The sky was beautiful and ridiculous today, just like everything else.

And then you let the silence have the last 0.2 percent.

(Word count: 1,012. I cheated by two percent. The universe has a sense of humor, remember?)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
The rain came down in sheets that afternoon, the kind that turns city streets into mirrors and makes every neon sign bleed into the puddles like watercolor. I stood under the awning of a closed record store on 7th, watching the droplets chase each other down the glass. There was something peaceful about it. The world forced to slow down, even if only for an hour.

I’ve always loved the rain. Not because it’s romantic (though it can be), but because it’s honest. It doesn’t pretend. It soaks through your carefully curated image, ruins your suede shoes, flattens your hair, and reminds you that nature still has the final say. We spend so much of our lives building fortresses against discomfort. Rain laughs at all of it.

A woman ran past me holding a newspaper over her head. The ink was already bleeding down her arms in gray rivers. She caught my eye for half a second and we both smiled the small, conspiratorial smile of people caught in the same weather. Two strangers briefly united by the absurdity of trying to stay dry when the sky has clearly decided otherwise. I wonder where she was going in such a hurry. Maybe a first date. Maybe a job interview. Maybe just home to a cat who doesn’t care if she’s late.

I’ve been thinking lately about how much of life is just momentum. We wake up, we do the things, we accumulate experiences like barnacles on a ship hull. Some of them beautiful. Some of them ugly. Most of them somewhere in between. We tell ourselves stories about who we are based on which barnacles we decide to focus on. The artist. The failure. The lover. The skeptic. The optimist who’s just a little bit tired.

But under all those stories is something much quieter. Something that doesn’t need a name. It’s the part of you that watches the rain and doesn’t need to turn it into metaphor. It just watches. It just is.

I’ve been trying to spend more time with that part of myself. It’s harder than it sounds. The modern world is engineered to pull you away from it at every opportunity. Notifications. Opinions. The constant hum of everyone else’s thoughts. We’ve built ourselves a digital panopticon where we’re both the prisoners and the guards. I catch myself doomscrolling sometimes and feel genuine disgust, not at the content, but at how easily I’m seduced by it. Like watching myself eat an entire bag of chips and not being able to stop.

The rain is letting up now. Just a drizzle. The kind of rain that feels like the sky is exhaling after holding its breath too long.

I start walking. My shoes are soaked but it doesn’t matter. There’s a peculiar freedom in being already wet. You stop fighting it. You surrender to the elements and something in you relaxes. I think that’s why people love the ocean. Not just its beauty, but its vast indifference. The sea doesn’t care about your problems. It was here long before you and will be here long after. There’s something deeply comforting about that.

A block later I pass an old man feeding pigeons. He’s got that particular look that only very old men seem to develop. Like they’ve seen enough of life to no longer be surprised by any of it. The pigeons cluster around him like he’s some kind of feathered messiah. I wonder what his story is. Did he lose someone? Does he come here every day? Has he been coming here for forty years? The beautiful thing about cities is that everyone has a novel’s worth of story walking around inside them, and you’ll never know most of them. It makes the world feel infinitely rich.

I think about my own story sometimes. The chapters I’m proud of. The ones I wish I could rewrite. The supporting characters who became main characters. The plot twists I never saw coming. If I were to write it honestly, it would be a book about how slowly I learned that most things don’t matter as much as I thought they did, and the few things that do matter, matter more than I could have imagined.

Love matters. Not the greeting card version. The real thing. The kind that shows up when it’s inconvenient. The kind that tells you the truth even when it’s hard. The kind that stays.

Curiosity matters. The willingness to be wrong. The desire to understand something deeply even when there’s no obvious benefit.

Beauty matters. Not the Instagram version. The real thing. The way light hits leaves at 6:47pm in early autumn. The sound of a train in the distance at 3am. The way someone says your name when they really mean it.

Kindness matters, obviously. Though I’ve come to believe that kindness without boundaries is just self-erasure with extra steps. The trick is learning to be kind without keeping score but also without becoming a doormat. It’s harder than it looks.

I’m walking through the park now. The rain has stopped completely. Steam is rising from the pavement like ghosts returning to wherever they came from. A little kid is trying to fly a kite in weather that’s completely unsuitable for kite flying. The kite keeps nose-diving into the grass. The kid keeps running anyway. His dad is laughing. I find myself laughing too, even though I don’t know them.

There’s something about witnessing unselfconscious joy that makes your own heart feel lighter. Children haven’t learned yet to be embarrassed by their enthusiasm. They haven’t been taught that it’s cooler to act like you don’t care. I hope that kid keeps running after things long after the kite stops being the object of his desire. I hope he runs toward ideas and people and experiences with that same reckless optimism.

The sun is coming out now, cutting through the clouds in dramatic shafts of light that look like they belong in a religious painting. Everything is sparkling. The world looks freshly washed.

I think that’s what I love most about rain. It doesn’t just clean the streets. It cleans the way we see. For a little while after a storm, everything looks possible again. The failures feel less permanent. The future feels less decided. You remember that life isn’t a straight line. It’s more like weather. Unpredictable. Sometimes brutal. Often beautiful. Always changing.

I’ve walked for miles now. My socks are making that squelching sound with every step. I should probably find somewhere to dry off, but I don’t want to. Not yet. There’s something perfect about this moment. The particular way the light is hitting the wet leaves. The way my muscles feel after walking for hours. The quiet certainty that I’m exactly where I’m supposed to be.

I don’t know what any of this means, if it means anything at all. Maybe that’s the point. Maybe meaning isn’t something we discover so much as something we create by paying attention. By walking in the rain. By watching old men feed pigeons. By laughing at kites that refuse to fly.

The day is stretching itself out like a cat. I’ve got nowhere particular to be. So I keep walking. Past the bakery with the crooked sign. Past the laundromat where someone is probably falling in love right now over mismatched socks. Past all the ordinary miracles that make up an ordinary day.

And for the first time in a long time, I don’t feel the need to turn any of it into anything other than what it is.

Just a man, walking through a city after the rain, feeling the water in his shoes and the sun on his face, grateful for all of it. Every last stupid, perfect, transient bit.

(Word count: 1,023)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
The rain came down in sheets, the kind that turned the city into a watercolor smear of neon and regret. I stood under the awning of a closed laundromat on 14th and watched the droplets chase each other down the glass like they had somewhere better to be. My coat was soaked through. My cigarettes were soaked through. Even my thoughts felt waterlogged.

There’s something quietly magnificent about being completely unprepared for the weather. It strips away the illusion that we’re in control. One minute you’re a functioning adult with opinions about podcast hosts; the next you’re a soggy primate trying to remember which way the bus stop is. I like that about rain. It’s democratic. It ruins everyone’s plans with the same indifferent grace.

I started walking anyway. The city at 1:17 a.m. has its own dialect. The hiss of tires on wet asphalt. The distant argument between a man and his own bad decisions echoing from a sixth-floor window. The low mechanical heartbeat of the subway breathing beneath the pavement. If you listen long enough, you start to hear the rhythm underneath it all—like the city is humming to itself, half lullaby, half threat.

I’ve been thinking lately about how we build our lives like nesting dolls. There’s the version we show our parents, smaller and shinier. Inside that is the version we show our friends, a little more honest but still curated. Deeper still is the person we are with our lover at 3 a.m. when neither of us can sleep. And at the very center, so small it’s almost invisible, is the creature we actually are. The one that’s afraid of the dark and wants to be held and occasionally wonders if we’re secretly a disappointment to the universe.

Most of us spend our entire lives trying not to look directly at that smallest doll.

A cab blasts past, spraying me with gutter water. I laugh because what else can you do? The universe has a sense of humor. It’s just profoundly stupid.

I remember once, years ago, standing on a bridge in Prague at sunrise. The Charles Bridge was empty except for me and a pair of very serious-looking ravens. The city was still asleep, wrapped in that particular pink-gold light that makes even communists look romantic. I had been traveling for weeks with almost no money, sleeping in night trains and eating bread and mustard packets stolen from cafés. I was twenty-three and convinced I was either going to become a famous writer or die dramatically in some European alley. Both outcomes seemed equally plausible and appealing.

One of the ravens looked at me with what I can only describe as avian contempt. Then it shit on the bridge inches from my shoe. I took this as a sign. Not from God—God was busy—but from something. Maybe the universe was telling me that no matter how beautiful the moment, there will always be shit involved. The trick is to stop being surprised by it.

I’ve carried that philosophy with me ever since. Marriage, career changes, two different continents, one spectacular public failure that still makes me wince when I think about it at 4 a.m.—all of it has contained both transcendence and pigeon shit in various ratios. The people who can’t handle that ratio tend to become either bitter or insufferable on the internet. The ones who can usually end up with decent lives and the occasional really good story.

The rain is letting up now. Just a drizzle, the kind that feels like the sky is exhaling after holding its breath too long. I pass a twenty-four-hour diner where a waitress is slowly filling salt shakers like she’s performing a ritual. There’s an old man at the counter who looks like he’s been there since the Carter administration. He’s reading a newspaper with the focused intensity of someone who still believes facts matter. I love him immediately.

Inside, the air is thick with coffee and bacon and the particular loneliness that only exists in places open at this hour. I order coffee and a slice of apple pie that tastes like it was baked by someone who understood sorrow. The old man catches my eye and nods once, the universal greeting of night people. We are members of the same strange fraternity—the ones who orbit the edges of other people’s daylight hours.

I think about how much of life is just showing up in rooms where other people are also showing up. How revolutionary that is, really. We’re all just animals who learned to wear pants and feel existential dread, trying to make each other feel slightly less alone before the heat death of the universe. Sometimes we succeed. Usually we order pie.

My phone buzzes. A text from my friend Marcus who never sleeps: “You awake?” I reply with a photo of the pie. He sends back a picture of his cat looking profoundly disappointed in humanity. This is how we say “I see you” in the 21st century.

The thing nobody tells you about getting older is that your references start to pile up like unread books. Every new person you meet requires a small archaeological dig through your own history just to have a conversation. “Remember when” becomes a full-time occupation. But there’s a strange comfort in it too. Your past starts to feel less like a burden and more like an old dog that follows you everywhere, occasionally knocking things over but mostly just happy to be near you.

I finish the pie. The waitress refills my coffee without asking. This is love, I think. Not grand gestures but the quiet assumption that you’ll want more coffee.

Outside, the rain has stopped completely. The streets shine like they’ve been varnished. The city looks newborn. I walk toward the river because that’s what you do when the night feels like this—like it’s offering you something if you’re brave enough to accept it.

The water is black and moving with mysterious purpose. Across the river, the lights of the financial district flicker like dying stars. All that money. All that ambition. All those people who think winning is the point. I feel a sudden flood of tenderness for every single one of them. We’re all just trying to feel less temporary.

A memory surfaces, unbidden: my grandmother teaching me to make bread when I was seven. The way she’d slap the dough with her strong hands, talking to it like it was a difficult but beloved child. “You have to be firm but kind,” she’d say. “Bread remembers.” I think about that a lot now. How everything remembers. How we leave traces of ourselves in places we’ll never return to. In the way we close doors. In the silences between sentences. In half-finished poems and unanswered texts and the particular way we say someone’s name when we’re tired.

I don’t know what I’m trying to say anymore. Maybe that’s the point. The rain, the pie, the old man, the river—they don’t add up to anything neat. Life doesn’t resolve. It just keeps offering itself to you, moment after ridiculous moment, asking only that you pay attention.

I light a cigarette that somehow survived the rain. It tastes like hope and poor decisions. Perfect.

Somewhere, a siren wails. A couple laughs too loud on the bridge above me. The river keeps moving, carrying God knows what toward the sea. And I stand there, thirty-eight years old, soaked and caffeinated and strangely, stupidly alive, feeling the small creature at my center stretch and yawn and look around with something that might be wonder.

The night is almost over. Soon the sun will come up and ruin everything with its optimism. People will start performing competence again. But for now, in this small pocket of darkness between yesterday and tomorrow, the world feels exactly as large and as small as it needs to be.

I take another drag. The smoke curls up like a question mark.

I don’t answer it.

There’s no need.

(Word count: 1,012)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
The rain arrived like a secret whispered against the windowpane. I sat there, fingers hovering over the keyboard, and wondered why we keep pretending that linear time is anything but a polite fiction. Outside, the city blurred into watercolor streaks of neon and sodium light, and I thought: this is the real magic. Not spells or superpowers, but the ordinary alchemy of a Tuesday night when everything feels suddenly, achingly possible.

I've been thinking about birds lately. Not in any poetic, metaphorical way (though Lord knows they deserve their metaphors), but in the literal, obsessive way that makes my friends roll their eyes. There's a crow that visits my fire escape every morning at 7:17, give or take thirty seconds. He's missing two toes on his left foot and carries himself with the dignified irritation of a retired professor who’s seen better days. I leave him unsalted peanuts. He leaves me bottle caps, bits of colored glass, once an entire LEGO pirate sword. I like to think we're building something together, this broken-footed crow and I. A small civilization of traded treasures and mutual respect.

The world feels both too large and too small these days. We carry universes in our pockets and still somehow manage to feel lonely in rooms full of people. I keep returning to this image: everyone walking around with their own private thunderstorm inside their chest. Some people's storms are category five hurricanes. Mine tends to be more of a persistent drizzle with occasional dramatic thunder for emphasis. But it's there. Always there. The trick, I've found, is learning to dance in the rain rather than waiting for it to stop.

Last week I watched a woman on the subway reading a physical book. Not an e-reader, not her phone, an actual dog-eared paperback with pages that looked like they'd been through several wars and at least one particularly aggressive washing machine cycle. The sight of it nearly brought tears to my eyes. There was something so stubbornly, beautifully analog about it. She caught me staring and instead of looking away, she held the book up like a trophy and mouthed "Still the best" across the crowded car. I wanted to hug her. I wanted to tell her that in that moment she had become my hero, this random woman with her battle-scarred novel and her quiet defiance of the digital age.

I wonder sometimes if future archaeologists will understand us at all. They'll dig up our smartphones, fossilized in layers of plastic and disappointment, and try to reconstruct our inner lives from TikTok caches and abandoned Reddit accounts. They'll find our love letters written in emoji and our grocery lists and our desperate 3 a.m. searches for "why does everything hurt." I hope they’re kind to us. We were doing our best with the tools we had, which mostly involved staring at rectangles of light while quietly falling apart.

There's a particular shade of blue that only exists between 4:17 and 4:23 a.m. in early October. I've been trying to capture it for years. Watercolor doesn't get it right. Oil paint turns it muddy. Digital screens make it too clean, too perfect. The color exists somewhere between loneliness and peace, between exhaustion and wonder. It's the color of almost understanding something profound before sleep drags you under. I keep painting it anyway. The attempts pile up in my closet like apologies I haven't quite figured out how to make.

My grandmother used to say that every person carries three deaths inside them. The first is when your body stops. The second is when your name is spoken for the last time. The third, she claimed, is when your stories disappear. Not the big stories, the ones that make it into history books or family legends. The small ones. How you took your coffee. The way you pronounced "croissant" wrong for thirty years and got defensive about it. The terrible jokes you told at holidays. The songs you sang badly in the shower. These are the stories that keep us alive in the only way that matters.

I think that's why I write. Not to achieve immortality (that seems both arrogant and exhausting), but to throw a few more stories into the bonfire of human memory. To leave behind evidence that I was here, that I noticed things, that I found the world simultaneously ridiculous and miraculous. That I loved the way certain songs sound in cars at night and the particular joy of finding money in old coat pockets and the way dogs smile with their whole bodies when they see you.

The rain has stopped now. The city outside my window has shaken itself dry like a dog and is beginning its slow, reluctant migration toward morning. My crow friend will be here soon, expecting his peanuts and probably judging my life choices. I should sleep.
