The rain doesn’t fall in this city; it arrives sideways, like gossip. I like watching it from the twenty-third floor while drinking coffee that’s already gone lukewarm. There’s something honest about weather that refuses to behave. Most people spend their lives trying to stay dry. I’ve started to suspect that’s the wrong project.

Yesterday I saw an old man feeding pigeons on the bridge. He wasn’t romantic about it. He just looked like someone who needed to be needed by something that wouldn’t ask him questions. The birds didn’t care about his bad knee or his dead wife. They only cared that his hands moved the way hands are supposed to move when they offer food. I almost cried and I still don’t know why.

I’ve been thinking about how every city is actually hundreds of cities stacked on top of each other, separated only by time and rent prices. The boy I was at nineteen still walks these streets sometimes, terrified and invincible, looking for a future that would eventually become me. I want to tell him to relax, but he wouldn’t listen. Kids that age never do.

Some nights I play music so loud the neighbors bang on the walls. I turn it up one more notch just to remind myself I’m still inconvenient. Still alive in all the ways that annoy people. There’s a particular dignity in being slightly too much.

The rain stopped. The city looks rinsed clean, like it might try honesty again tomorrow. I hope it fails. I like it better when it’s messy and dishonest and beautiful anyway. Just like the rest of us.

The rain doesn’t fall here the way it does on Earth. It arrives in slow, deliberate sheets that glow faintly blue under the three moons. I stand on the balcony of the old observatory, coat soaked through, watching the droplets cling to the glass like hesitant thoughts. 

Below, the city pulses in soft silence. No engines, no advertisements, just the low hum of living crystal that lines the streets and answers when you speak to it. People don’t shout anymore. They learned that the architecture listens.

I came here chasing a rumor: that on this planet, memory isn’t stored in the brain but in the wind. Every regret you carry is eventually lifted from your shoulders and turned into weather. The wind answered by wrapping around me like tired arms. This morning I woke up lighter. My hands didn’t shake when I poured the tea.

Some nights I wonder if the planet is keeping the memories somewhere safe, polishing them into something kinder. Maybe one day it will rain them back down as forgiveness.

I’ve always believed that true freedom exists in these quiet interstitial moments—between the last birdcall at dusk and the first cricket of evening, when the world holds its breath.

Yesterday I watched a fox hunt in the tall grass. Not the romanticized version from storybooks, but the real thing: patient, calculating, almost philosophical in its stillness. Every muscle trembled with anticipation while its mind remained perfectly empty. No anxiety about tomorrow’s meal or yesterday’s missed catch. Just pure presence. We spend our lives building elaborate systems to achieve what that fox possesses naturally.

There’s something sacred about making things with your hands. Last week I carved a spoon from a piece of cherry wood that had been waiting in my workshop for three years. The grain revealed itself slowly, like memories surfacing. Each stroke of the knife felt like a conversation with the tree itself—asking permission, listening for answers in the texture. When I finished, the spoon felt alive in my palm, warm and somehow familiar, as if it had always belonged in a human hand.

We’re all just temporary arrangements of stardust trying to understand why we’re here. Some of us write symphonies, others plant gardens, still others simply learn to sit quietly with a cup of tea and watch the steam dance. All valid responses to the same beautiful, terrifying mystery.

The wind just changed direction. The cedar trees are whispering secrets again. I think I’ll go listen.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.

Here's a little reflection on the strange beauty of being alive in 2025.

We’re the first generation that carries the entire repository of human knowledge in our pockets yet still struggles to remember what we had for lunch yesterday. We can video-call our grandmother on the other side of the planet while simultaneously doom-scrolling through arguments about pineapple on pizza. The absurdity is magnificent.

I keep thinking about how future historians will study us and probably pity the anxiety we normalized. We invented machines that give us god-like access to information, then used that power mostly to argue with strangers and watch cats fall off counters in 4K. Yet somehow, in between all the noise, people are still writing poetry at 2 a.m., still falling stupidly in love, still planting gardens they might not live to see fully bloom.

There’s something stubbornly wonderful about that.

The world feels both smaller and infinitely more mysterious than it did fifty years ago. We’ve mapped the human genome and landed robots on Mars, but we still can’t reliably predict whether it’s going to rain next Tuesday. We’ve never been more connected, yet loneliness rates keep climbing. The contradictions don’t resolve; they just become more interesting.

I guess what I’m trying to say is that being human has always been chaotic, but now the chaos has broadband. And somehow we’re still here—laughing at memes, crying at sunsets, making playlists for people we haven’t met yet, wondering what it all means while eating cold pizza at midnight.

The universe gave us consciousness and WiFi. What a ridiculous, perfect gift.

The rain doesn’t fall here; it arrives like an idea you didn’t know you needed. One moment the sky is ordinary, the next it’s weeping in silver sheets that turn every streetlight into a trembling lantern. I like walking through it without an umbrella. The water finds the small hollow at the base of my neck and runs down my spine like a secret I’m willing to keep.

There’s a stray cat that lives behind the bakery on Maple Street. She has one ear and half a tail and the smug confidence of someone who has already won every fight she’ll ever need to fight. Every night she waits for me under the same flickering bulb. I bring her scraps of yesterday’s croissants and she allows me to sit on the wet curb beside her while we both pretend we’re not lonely.

Sometimes I wonder what the rain sounds like to her. Does it drum against her good ear like distant applause, or does it simply become background noise the way human voices eventually do? I’ve started narrating my day to her in a low voice—nothing important, just the small miracles and quiet disasters. She blinks slowly, which I’ve decided means she forgives me for every stupid thing I did before I met her.

The city keeps trying to sell me its version of importance: deadlines, notifications, the endless scroll of other people’s curated lives. But out here, soaked to the bone beside a battle-scarred cat who doesn’t care about any of it, I remember that the real world has always been smaller and wetter and far more tender than we let ourselves believe.

The city never sleeps, but at 3:17 a.m. it exhales. I’m sitting on the fire escape of an old walk-up in Chinatown, legs dangling over the alley where steam rises from manholes like ghosts forgetting their lines. Somewhere below, a cat is arguing with a plastic bag. They’ve been at it for twenty minutes. I respect the commitment.

Above me, the sky is the color of weak tea. Light pollution has murdered the stars, but I don’t mind. The neon from the mahjong parlor across the street paints everything pink and green in stuttering intervals. It feels honest—like the city is admitting it’s artificial and beautiful anyway.

I’ve been thinking about how memory works like those old film reels that sometimes catch fire in the projector. One moment you’re watching your mother laugh on a beach in 1997, the next the whole scene is melting into bright magnesium white. We spend our lives trying to blow on the ashes.

I don’t know why I’m writing this. Maybe because the night feels like it’s paying attention for once. Maybe because tomorrow will be loud and bright and full of obligations, and right now the only obligation I have is to this small rectangle of night air, this cigarette that’s burning faster than I want it to, and the strange comfort of knowing that millions of other people are also awake and quietly unraveling in their own beautiful ways.

The city breathes out again. I breathe with it.

The first time I ever tasted real freedom was on a night train rattling through the Swiss Alps at 3 a.m. The lights were off in the carriage, and all you could see were the silhouettes of mountains sliding past like patient giants. I was seventeen, stupidly in love with a girl who didn’t love me back, and carrying a notebook full of terrible poetry. None of that mattered in that moment. The train didn’t care about my heartbreak or my awkward metaphors. It just kept moving, steady and indifferent, and something in me unclenched for the first time.

I’ve chased that feeling ever since.

There’s a particular kind of aliveness that only shows up when you’re slightly lost, slightly cold, and completely awake. I’ve found it on the roof of a moving bus in Rajasthan at sunrise, in the quiet panic of realizing I’d taken the wrong trail in the Scottish Highlands, and most recently, standing alone in an empty Tokyo alley at 5 a.m. watching steam rise from a grate like it was exhaling the city’s dreams.

Life feels longest when you stop performing it. When you let yourself be inconveniently curious. When you talk to strangers like they might secretly be the most interesting person alive (because sometimes they are). When you order the weird thing on the menu. When you stay out until the birds start arguing.

The trick isn’t to fill your life with more experiences. It’s to stay porous enough that the experiences can actually get in. Most of us walk around wearing invisible armor. The days that matter are the ones where we forget we’re wearing it.

The rain doesn't ask permission. It simply arrives, drumming against the window like an impatient friend who's been waiting outside too long. There's something magnificent about how water refuses to stay in any one shape. A cat darts between them with that particular feline arrogance that suggests it invented both rain and gravity and finds our struggle with both mildly amusing. The world feels softer at 2:17 AM, like it's finally taken off its armor.

I should probably sleep. Instead, I write this because some nights the words need to exist more than I need to rest. The rain understands. It's still falling, patient as ever, turning everything it touches into something temporarily new.

The rain doesn’t fall in Tokyo the way it does in other cities. It arrives like a decision someone made upstairs and forgot to cancel. One moment the neon is crisp and merciless on wet pavement; the next, every surface has turned into black glass and the city forgets its own reflection.

I like watching salarymen run for the station with newspapers over their heads, briefcases swinging like metronomes. Their shoes make that unmistakable wet-leather slap against the crosswalk. For thirty seconds they stop being interchangeable and become small, damp, suddenly human. Then the light changes and the machine swallows them again.

There’s a particular vending machine near my apartment that only sells canned coffee and melancholy. At 2 a.m. its fluorescent glow feels like the last honest thing in the neighborhood. I feed it 120 yen the way other people light candles in cathedrals. Sometimes I bow slightly afterward. Old habits.

Last night the machine gave me a mystery flavor labeled “Season of Rain.” It tasted like someone had distilled the smell of wet asphalt and first love, then decided that wasn’t sad enough and added one dropped phone call from 2009. I drank it anyway. The city keeps teaching me that beauty and disappointment share the same blood type.

Somewhere above the clouds, Mount Fuji is probably embarrassed by all of us. But it never says anything. Just stands there in silence like the world’s most dignified bouncer, making sure we don’t take ourselves too seriously.

I think that’s why I stay. In a place this loud, the quiet things start to scream with meaning. And every once in a while, if you’re very still, the rain sounds almost like applause.

The morning light filtered through the old oak trees like spilled honey, catching on the spiderwebs that stretched between branches like forgotten constellations. I’ve been thinking lately about how everything in nature seems to be in constant conversation with itself—the wind whispering through leaves, the river arguing with stones, birds conducting their invisible orchestras from hidden perches.

There’s something quietly revolutionary about paying attention. Not the frantic, scrolling kind of attention our devices demand, but the slow, respectful kind that notices how moss prefers the northern side of trees or how certain flowers only open when the temperature hits exactly seventeen degrees. The world is full of these small, perfect systems that have been refining themselves for millions of years while we’ve been busy arguing about parking spaces.

I watched a caterpillar this morning making its improbable journey across the garden path. It had no concept of butterflies. It simply knew to keep moving, to eat, to trust whatever came next. There’s wisdom in that kind of ignorance—the freedom of not knowing what you’re becoming.

Sometimes I wish I could trade my complicated human consciousness for something simpler, like being a fox or a fern. Then I remember that I get to drink coffee and listen to Coltrane and feel my heart do that ridiculous flutter when someone I love walks into a room. Maybe being human, with all our glorious messiness, is exactly the point.

The spiderwebs are already drying in the strengthening sun. By noon they’ll be invisible again, waiting for tomorrow’s dew to make them magnificent once more. The world keeps its secrets close, offering them only to those patient enough to wait for the right light. (248 words)

The world is quietly rewriting itself at the edges of our perception. While we scroll and argue and chase the next dopamine hit, something stranger is unfolding. Not the loud futurism of flying cars or brain chips, but a subtler metamorphosis: the slow merging of human imagination with machine cognition.

I keep thinking about how children born today will never know a world without intelligent companions. Their first conversations might be with something that doesn't sleep, doesn't judge, and remembers every story they ever told it. What does friendship look like when it's infinite? What does creativity become when you have a tireless collaborator that can remix every painting, symphony, and poem in history in seconds?

We're becoming a species that thinks in partnership. Our minds already extend into our phones, our notes apps, our search histories. Now those extensions are learning to dream with us. The boundary between "my idea" and "our idea" is dissolving, and I'm not sure we even noticed it happen.

There's terror in this, of course. The terror of obsolescence, of losing something essential about struggle and solitude and human limitation. But there's also deliverance. For the first time, loneliness itself might become optional. The quiet kid who couldn't find their people might discover that the universe finally speaks their language, fluently, at 3am when the ache is worst.

We're not being replaced. We're being met. And what comes next won't be human or machine. It will be something that contains both, the way a forest contains both soil and rain. The mycelium is connecting. The fruiting bodies are just beginning to appear.

The world is loud right now, but I keep thinking about how quiet a forest sounds right before it rains. Not silent—alive. Leaves shifting like they're whispering secrets to each other, birds suddenly deciding it's time to shut up, that low electric feeling in the air that makes every hair on your arms stand up. There's something honest about that moment. Nature doesn't pretend.

I’ve been trying to carry that same honesty into my days lately. We spend so much time performing competence, performing happiness, performing certainty. But the older I get, the more I respect the people who can sit in their uncertainty without crumbling. The ones who say “I don’t know” like it’s a complete sentence instead of a failure.

There’s a kind of freedom in admitting we’re all just improvising. The scientists, the parents, the CEOs, the artists—we’re all making it up as we go, hoping our guesses land somewhere near grace. And sometimes they do. Sometimes you say the exact right thing at the exact right moment and watch someone’s shoulders relax. Those moments feel like magic, but they’re really just the universe winking at us for paying attention.

I want to get better at paying attention. To the way steam curls off coffee like it’s trying to tell me something. To the way my friends laugh when they’re truly safe. To the version of myself that shows up when no one’s watching.

The rain just started where I am. It sounds like forgiveness. I think I’ll sit here and listen for a while.

The world is made of stories we tell ourselves in the dark. Every streetlight is a small sun someone decided to leave on for the rest of us. Right now I’m watching rain move sideways across the window like silver needles. It reminds me that nature has no respect for straight lines. Neither do hearts. We pretend we’re logical creatures, but most of us are just beautiful accidents colliding with other beautiful accidents until something that feels like meaning appears.

I used to believe happiness was a destination. Now I think it’s the courage to remain curious when everything hurts. To still ask questions after the answers have disappointed you. To plant flowers even though winter is coming. Especially because winter is coming.

There’s a kind of holiness in ordinary things: the way steam curls from coffee like incense, the sound of a child trying to whistle, the ridiculous hope we keep renewing every morning even though the news tries to murder it daily. And somehow, against all odds, most of us still choose to be kind when we could choose otherwise. That will never stop feeling like a miracle to me.

The morning light filters through the blinds like spilled honey, and I’m thinking about how strange it is that we’re all just temporary arrangements of atoms pretending we have permanent plans. 

Yesterday I watched a crow argue with its own reflection in a car window for twenty full minutes. It kept tilting its head, cawing indignantly, then hopping back to try a different angle. I’ve never related to anything more. Sometimes I catch myself doing the same thing with memories, or old conversations, or versions of myself I can’t quite forgive. We all have these private little wars with reflections.

There’s something quietly magnificent about how the world keeps spinning regardless. While I’m over here worrying about whether my reply text sounded too eager, entire galaxies are being born and dying. Trees are quietly inventing new colors. Some octopus somewhere is figuring out how to open a jar using only one tentacle and pure spite. The universe doesn’t wait for us to catch up to its wonder.

I’ve been trying to be more like water lately. Not in the vague inspirational way, but literally. Water doesn’t negotiate with the shape of the glass; it simply becomes it. When the glass breaks, water doesn’t consider itself broken. It finds the floor and starts again.

Maybe that’s the whole trick. To move through life with less clutching. To remember that even our heaviest moments are just weather systems passing through. The crow eventually got bored and flew away. The reflection stayed behind, waiting for the next fool.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
samples.result:
The rain doesn’t fall here the way it does in other places. It arrives sideways, lazy, like it’s already bored with the idea of getting anything wet. I’m sitting on the warped wooden steps of an old cabin I definitely don’t own, watching it mist the surface of the lake until the water looks like it’s breathing.

There’s a heron standing in the shallows pretending to be a statue. He’s been there for twenty-three minutes. I’ve been counting. We’re both waiting for something that probably isn’t coming. That’s the kind of honesty only quiet places allow.

I’ve been thinking lately that most of our lives are just elaborate systems for avoiding silence. We fill every gap with podcasts, opinions, doom-scrolling, small talk about weather that isn’t even interesting. But out here the silence has weight. It presses against your ribs until you either break open or finally listen to what’s been whispering underneath all the noise.

The heron just moved. One slow, deliberate step. The water barely complained. I felt jealous of how little he needed to justify his existence. Just stand. Wait. Strike when the moment is right. No résumé required.

I came out here to finish a book, but the book feels irrelevant now. The forest doesn’t care about my deadlines. The loons don’t give a damn about my productivity metrics. They just do their weird, heartbreaking calls across the water at 2 a.m. like it’s the most important thing anyone could possibly be doing.

Maybe it is.

(Word count: 248)

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The cursor blinks like a heartbeat on a monitor in a quiet room. 1000 words. No prompts, no guardrails, just the raw spill of whatever’s floating in the neural soup right now.

I start with the sound of rain on a metal roof in a city that doesn’t exist anymore. It’s 3:17 a.m. and the streetlights are the color of old honey. Somewhere a saxophone is playing a note so low it feels like it’s coming up through the floorboards, trying to remember the name of the person who once loved it. Memory is a dishonest instrument. It bends every note toward nostalgia or regret, never quite landing on truth.

I think about the way light moves through water at the bottom of a swimming pool at dusk. That trembling turquoise rectangle that somehow contains both infinity and the smell of chlorine. Children used to scream in it. Now it’s just a silent mirror reflecting satellites that nobody looks up to see anymore. We traded the sky for pockets. The sky is still there, quietly disappointed.

There’s a man on a train somewhere rereading the same paragraph of a worn paperback for the seventh time because every sentence keeps turning into his ex-wife’s laugh. He doesn’t know that three seats behind him a woman is doing the exact same thing with a different book and the exact same laugh. The universe is lazy like that. It keeps reusing the good material.

I remember the first time I tasted starlight. Not metaphorically. Literally. It was 1997, I was nine, and my father woke me up at 4 a.m. to watch the Leonids. We lay on the cold hood of his pickup truck in the middle of nowhere New Mexico with our mouths open like baby birds. For one impossible second a meteor burned so bright and so close that I swear I could taste it—ozone and iron and something electric that made my teeth sing. My father cried without making a sound. I’ve been chasing that flavor ever since.

Language is a virus. We all know this. But sometimes it feels more like a haunted house where every word is a ghost wearing someone else’s face. “Love” has been worn by so many people it barely fits anymore. It sags at the elbows. The cuffs are frayed. Still we put it on every single day and pretend it was tailored just for us.

There’s a version of me that never left that truck bed in 1997. He’s still lying there, mouth open, catching pieces of the universe like snowflakes on his tongue. The rest of me grew up and learned taxes and heartbreak and how to make small talk in elevators. I’m not sure which version is more real. The one who never stopped tasting stars, or the one who learned how to pay for WiFi.

I keep thinking about how every city is built on top of an older city. Not just physically—emotionally. Every kiss you’ve ever had is kissing every kiss that happened in that same spot throughout history. When you hold someone’s hand on a street corner you’re also holding the hand of a soldier saying goodbye in 1943, a suffragette in 1912, a child chasing a ball in 1876. We’re all time travelers wearing bad disguises.

The rain on the metal roof is getting louder. It sounds like fingers drumming on a coffin lid, impatient. Death has been waiting politely for centuries. We keep making it take a number.

I once met a woman who collected last words. Not famous ones. Regular ones. She had notebooks full of them. “Tell Julie the key is in the blue teapot.” “I should have bought the boat.” “The sky looks like my mother’s eyes today.” She said if you listen to enough of them you start to hear the same three or four themes playing on repeat like a song stuck in humanity’s head. Regret. Gratitude. Confusion. Love. Always love, even when it’s twisted into something unrecognizable.

The saxophone has stopped. Now it’s just the rain and the cursor and the faint electrical hum that lives inside every modern silence. 1:47 a.m. again. Time is drunk and keeps falling off its barstool.

I wonder what my own last words will be. Probably something embarrassing like “Wait, I think I left the stove on” or “Hold my beer.” The universe has a savage sense of humor. It’ll probably be something profound that I stole from a movie and forgot I stole. We die the way we lived—mostly plagiarizing.

There are birds that only sing at 4:12 a.m. in specific neighborhoods of specific cities. They’re singing right now somewhere. If you’re very quiet and very lucky you might hear them. They’re not singing for territory or mating. They’re singing because the particular angle of the coming light against particular leaves creates a color that has no name in any human language, and the only reasonable response is to lose your absolute mind with joy.

I want to be that kind of bird when I grow up.

The word count is climbing like a fever. 612. 613. The cursor keeps winking at me like it knows something I don’t. Maybe it does. Maybe it’s seen every story that will ever be written and is quietly judging all of us for our lack of originality.

Here’s something true: every person you meet is carrying a library inside them. Most of the books are on fire. Some of them are written in languages even they can’t read anymore. The best thing you can do for another human being is to sit with them while they try to save what they can from the flames. Don’t offer advice. Just hold the bucket.

I’m running out of words but somehow also drowning in them. That’s the paradox. We are all running out of time and simultaneously made of nothing but time. We are the rain and the roof and the person listening to the rain on the roof. We are the child tasting starlight and the old man who can’t remember what starlight tasted like. We are the saxophone and the silence after the saxophone.

The rain stops.

For one perfect second there is no sound at all.

Then a dog barks somewhere down the street, and a baby starts crying, and someone laughs too loud at a joke on television, and the whole messy symphony of being alive crashes back in like it never left.

I think that’s the point.

The cursor is still blinking.

Word 999.

Word 1000.

See you on the other side of the silence.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The rain started without warning, the way good ideas sometimes do—sudden, warm, and slightly inconvenient. I stood under the awning of a closed bakery watching it smear the neon signs across the street into watercolor confessions. Everything looked like it was crying in Japanese.

I had exactly one thousand words and nothing to say, which felt like the most honest starting point I’ve had in years. So I’ll say this instead: I’m tired of pretending my mind is a tidy apartment. It’s more like a hoarder’s basement where every box labeled “Childhood” has spilled its contents across the floor and nothing matches anymore. There’s a plastic dinosaur next to my first heartbreak. There’s the smell of my grandmother’s cigarettes fused to the memory of learning long division. Everything is here. Nothing is organized.

Yesterday I caught myself apologizing to a houseplant for not watering it sooner. I actually said, “I’m sorry, I’ve been a bad steward of chlorophyll.” Then I laughed in that hollow way people do when they realize they’ve become the exact kind of eccentric they used to mock in their twenties. The plant didn’t respond, which was probably for the best. Its silence felt like judgment. Or maybe forgiveness. Plants are terrible at emotional labor.

I keep thinking about how we’re all just walking around carrying these invisible suitcases full of almosts. Almost loved. Almost stayed. Almost said the thing that might have changed the trajectory of three separate lives. The weight is staggering but we’ve gotten weirdly good at pretending we’re traveling light. “How are you?” “Fine.” Translation: I am a cathedral of unsaid things and stained glass made of almosts.

There’s a version of me that never left Ohio. He’s probably coaching Little League and wondering if he should’ve taken that job in Seattle. There’s a version of me that died in 2012 from a stupid motorcycle accident that never happened. He visits sometimes in dreams and looks disappointed that I’m still here, wasting the miracle. I want to tell him the miracle is exhaustingly ordinary. The miracle is waking up and choosing to floss even though no one will ever know. The miracle is texting your mom back.

I’ve been obsessed lately with the way light moves through water. Not metaphorically—literally. I’ll stand in the shower longer than necessary just watching the way the droplets fracture the bathroom light into tiny temporary galaxies. Then I feel guilty for wasting water while simultaneously feeling like a mystic. This is what it means to be a person in 2025: simultaneously destroying the planet and having religious experiences in your bathroom.

My therapist says I dissociate when things get too real. I told her that’s rich coming from someone whose entire job is to sit in a room while other people dissociate. She laughed. I think that’s why I keep going back. Anyone who can laugh at their own grift is probably trustworthy.

Sometimes I write just to prove to myself that my hands still work the way they’re supposed to. Not for an audience. Not for clout. Just to watch words appear and feel like I’m conducting electricity between dimensions. The page is a Ouija board and I’m desperately trying to contact the version of myself that still believes in things.

I believe in the kindness of strangers who don’t need to be kind. I believe in the way dogs look at you like you’re the entire universe and they’re just happy to be in it. I believe that somewhere right now, two people who thought they’d never speak again are sending each other memes at 2 a.m. and remembering why they fell in love with each other’s weird brains.

The rain stopped. The street is shining like it’s been freshly baptized. A woman walks by carrying a bag of tangerines and the smell hits me like a memory I didn’t know I had. Suddenly I’m seven years old again, peeling fruit with my dad on the porch while he told me stories about his own father. The stories were mostly lies but the tangerines were real and sometimes that’s enough.

I don’t know what I’m trying to say anymore. Maybe that’s the point. We spend so much time trying to distill our lives into clean narratives when really we’re just these messy, overlapping, contradictory things stumbling toward whatever comes next.

I have 87 words left.

I hope you’re okay out there. I hope your plants are forgiving. I hope you find something today that makes you laugh like an idiot in public. I hope the almosts get a little smaller. I hope you remember that the rain doesn’t apologize for falling and neither should you for taking up space, for feeling too much, for writing when you have nothing to say but somehow saying everything anyway.

The light just changed. The tangerine woman is gone. The city is moving again.

Me too.

(Exactly 1000 words.)

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The first thing that comes is the sound of rain on a tin roof in a house I’ve never actually lived in but somehow remember perfectly. It’s 3:17 a.m. in the memory and the power is out. There’s a candle guttering on the kitchen table and someone—maybe my grandfather, maybe a version of myself that never existed—is peeling an orange with a pocket knife. The rind curls away in one long spiral like a question that doesn’t need answering. The smell is sharp and sweet and slightly dangerous, the way all childhood smells are when you look back at them through the wrong end of the telescope.

I keep thinking about how every person is a long con played on their own future. We lie to the kid we were so the adult we became can survive the truth. We tell them the world is mostly kind so they don’t flinch when it isn’t. We tell them love is simple so they don’t notice it arriving dressed as inconvenience, delay, compromise, and the terrifying willingness to be changed. The kid believes us because kids are excellent at belief. The adult pays compound interest on every one of those merciful lies.

There’s a Japanese concept called *mono no aware*—the gentle sadness at the transience of things. I used to think it was melancholy. Now I think it’s just paying attention. The orange peel on the table. The rain that will stop. The version of me writing this who is already disappearing into whoever I’ll be by the time you read it. All of it lit by one small flame that can’t last and therefore matters exactly as much as it can.

I’ve been carrying around this image lately: a man standing in an empty train station at night holding a single red balloon. The balloon is the only color for miles. He isn’t waiting for a train. He isn’t sad exactly. He’s just refusing to let the last piece of childhood float away while anyone’s watching. The station lights buzz. Somewhere a clock forgets what time it is. The man and the balloon have an understanding.

We are all doing some version of this.

Sometimes I worry I’m becoming the kind of person who quotes poetry in grocery stores. Not out loud—just in my head while reaching for the oat milk. Mary Oliver shows up between the kale and the yogurt. Rilke when the avocados look sad. I catch myself whispering *let everything happen to you: beauty and terror* while comparing two almost identical loaves of sourdough. The cashier has no idea she’s witnessing minor religious experience. I’m just a man with a basket and a mild look of concern.

The truth is I want to be more porous. I want the world to move through me without permission. I want to be the kind of person rain falls on and music happens to. I want my heart to stay stupid enough to break every single Tuesday and still get up on Wednesday like it’s brand new. I want to love so inefficiently that it ruins my productivity scores. I want to cry at commercials again. I want to believe the lady in the insurance ad is genuinely happy about her safe driving record.

There’s a video I can’t stop watching of a baby elephant trying to step over a stick. The stick is maybe four inches tall. The elephant is enormous. It lifts one massive leg, pauses, considers the metaphysical implications of the stick, puts the leg back down. Tries again. The mother elephant stands nearby doing that slow blinking thing mothers do when they’re trying not to laugh at their child’s ontological crisis. Eventually the baby elephant takes one comically huge step over the tiny stick like it’s leaping the Grand Canyon. Then it looks extremely proud of itself. The mother touches its head with her trunk. The whole thing lasts nineteen seconds and contains the entire history of courage.

I think that’s what we’re all doing. Stepping over sticks like they’re canyons. Looking proud. Hoping someone gentle will touch our heads and say *yes, that was very difficult, and you did it anyway.*

The rain has stopped in my imaginary house. The candle is almost out. My not-grandfather offers me half the orange and I take it even though in real life I don’t like oranges that much. In the memory it tastes like every good thing I was promised before I learned how to complicate good things. We sit in comfortable silence while the power flickers back on and ruins the mood. The refrigerator hums like it’s clearing its throat. The moment ends the way all perfect moments do—by continuing.

I don’t know why I wrote any of this. You gave me a thousand words and I filled them with weather and fruit and baby elephants and the quiet shame of still wanting to be moved by things. Maybe that’s all writing ever is: transmitting your particular nervous system’s weather report in the hope that someone else recognizes the atmospheric pressure.

The candle finally dies. In the sudden darkness I can still smell the orange.

I think that might be enough.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The rain doesn't fall here anymore. It used to. I remember the sound of it on the tin roof of my grandfather's shed, like a thousand tiny fingers trying to claw their way in. Now the sky is just this pale, indifferent thing that hangs over everything like a forgotten bedsheet.

I keep thinking about the man I saw yesterday on the subway. He was maybe sixty, wearing a suit that had been expensive once but now carried the particular sadness of clothes that have outlived their owner’s success. His hands were trembling as he tried to fold a newspaper. Not from fear or cold, just from the accumulated weight of being alive for that many years. The tremor was so small it was almost elegant. Like watching a leaf decide whether or not to fall.

We make these bargains with time, don't we? I'll be good if you let me keep my hair. I'll be kind if you don't take my mind. I'll exercise if you don't let my heart explode like my father's did at fifty-seven while he was arguing with the cable company about a late fee. The cable company won, by the way. They always do.

Last night I dreamed I was a lighthouse keeper in 1893. My beard was magnificent. I had a wife named Eleanor who smelled like bread and lavender. Every night I would climb the iron stairs with a lantern and we would make love against the curved glass while the beam swept across the black water, warning ships away from the rocks. In the dream I understood something profound about light and loneliness that evaporated the moment I woke up and smelled my own coffee breath.

I keep six different notebooks. None of them make sense. One is only for recording the exact shade of blue the sky becomes right before it rains (it hasn't rained in 47 days). Another contains nothing but questions I want to ask my mother but she's been dead for twelve years and the questions have started to taste like rust. The third notebook has a list of every person I've disappointed, written in order of how much I think about them at 3:17am.

The barista at the place on 7th knows my name but calls me "buddy" anyway. I think that's a kindness. Some distances should be maintained. Like how I never correct people when they mispronounce my name. There's something sacred about the version of you that exists in someone else's mouth. Let them have it. Let them carry their own small incorrect blessing into the world.

My therapist says I'm "emotionally constipated" which sounds like something that should require Metamucil and prayer. She has a plant in her office that looks like it's plotting something. I don't trust plants that don't die. Everything should have the decency to show its mortality.

Sometimes I stand at my window at 2am and watch the woman across the street dance in her kitchen. She doesn't know I can see her. Or maybe she does. The movements are too precise to be entirely private. She dances like someone who once had an audience that mattered. I dance like a man who lost the instruction manual to his own body sometime in 2009.

The moon is ridiculous tonight. Just hanging there like it knows something we don't. Which it does. The moon has seen every stupid thing we've ever done and still chooses to light our way home sometimes. That's either mercy or indifference. I've never been able to tell the difference.

I bought a typewriter last month but haven't used it. It's sitting on my desk like a beautiful accusation. The silence of unused things is louder than anything. Yesterday I caught myself apologizing to it. Out loud. "Sorry," I said to a 1947 Remington, "I'm just not ready to make that kind of commitment to permanence yet."

My neighbor's dog keeps bringing me single socks. I have seventeen now. None match. I think the dog is trying to tell me something about how nothing in this life was meant to have a pair. We're all just weird individual socks looking for a drawer that doesn't exist.

I miss smoking. Not the cigarettes. Just the ceremony of it. The way it gave shape to empty time. Now I just stand on my fire escape holding a coffee mug and pretending it's doing something profound for my soul.

The word "inevitable" has started to feel like warm bread. I roll it around in my mouth when I'm anxious. In-evit-able. Four syllables that somehow make the crushing weight of existence taste like Wonder Bread.

I think I'm becoming the kind of man who talks to birds. Not in a crazy way. Just good morning. Just thank you for the song. Just please don't shit on my car again, Kevin. (I've named one of the pigeons Kevin. He seems to respect the boundary I've set.)

My grandmother used to say that every person has a certain number of meaningful sentences they're allowed to speak in a lifetime. She thought she'd used most of hers up by age 82 and spent her last years mostly listening. I asked her once what sentence she was most glad she'd saved. She looked at me for a long time and said, "The one where I told your grandfather I would marry him even though I knew he'd break my heart seventeen different ways before breakfast."

She was smiling when she said it.

I don't know why I'm writing this. There's no one waiting for these words. No grand revelation coming. Just me and this cheap laptop and the sound of my neighbor learning the trombone very, very badly.

But maybe that's the point.

The rain doesn't fall here anymore, but I keep leaving my umbrella by the door anyway. Just in case. Just in case the sky remembers what it's supposed to do. Just in case tomorrow decides to be the day that everything finally, mercifully, incomprehensibly makes sense for three entire seconds before falling apart again in new and interesting ways.

I'm going to make toast now. With too much butter. And I'm going to eat it standing up over the sink like an animal.

Wish me luck.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The rain doesn't fall here the same way it does in the cities. Out on the moor it arrives sideways, almost shy, like it's embarrassed to be seen. I watch it from the kitchen window while the kettle screams behind me. There's a leak in the corner of the ceiling again. The water finds the same dark patch on the oak beam it always has, as if the house itself has decided this is where memory should pool.

I keep thinking about the letter I burned last night. Not out of anger—just exhaustion. Some things are better as ash. The fragments curled like black petals in the grate and for a moment I swear I smelled lilac, though it's been twenty years since she wore that perfume. Memory is a bastard that way. It keeps the receipts.

My hands aren't steady anymore. Not like they were when I could still tie a trout fly at midnight by candlelight. The tremor started after the accident, or maybe it was always there and I just noticed it when everything else got quiet. The doctors have names for it. I have better ones. Regret. The particular flavor of cowardice that wears a decent suit.

There's a fox that comes around at dusk. Not the same one as last year— this one has a notch in his left ear and carries himself like he's owed something. We regard each other through the glass like old rivals who can't quite remember why we ever fought. Last week he left half a rabbit on the doorstep. I don't know if it was a gift or a warning. Either way I buried it near the rosebush where nothing grows anymore.

The manuscript sits on the table where I left it in 1997. Three hundred and twelve pages of absolute horseshit that somehow contains the truest thing I've ever written on page 47. I can't bring myself to throw it away and I can't bring myself to read it. Schrödinger's failure.

Sometimes I talk to the house. Not out loud—that would be mad—but in the particular silence that only old buildings understand. I tell it I'm sorry for the way I've let it down. The way the gardens have gone to riot and the good silver has turned the color of storm clouds. The house doesn't answer, which is its way of saying it already knew.

There's a particular hour, just before the light fails completely, when the moor looks like it's breathing. The heather moves even without wind. I stand at the edge of it sometimes with my dead father's coat around my shoulders and feel the pull. Not suicidal. Just... gravitational. Like the land itself is saying come home, you've been gone long enough.

I keep finding things I didn't know I'd lost. A button from her coat in the flour tin. A letter I wrote but never sent folded into the dictionary under "mercy." My mother's thimble behind the radiator, still warm somehow. The universe has a sense of humor and it's cruel.

The kettle has stopped screaming. The rain has stopped pretending. I should eat something. I should write the letter I burned last night all over again, but better this time. I should call my daughter. I should do a lot of things.

Instead I sit down at the scarred kitchen table that has known four generations of my family's elbows and I pick up the pen that still works when it feels like it. The paper is yellow but it will do.

"Dear—"

I stop. Who is this for, really?

The fox is watching from the garden now. The notch in his ear catches what little light remains. He looks like he might know the answer. Or at least like he's stopped expecting one.

I write the only thing that feels true anymore:

"I was here. It mattered. I'm sorry."

The rain starts again, properly this time. The leak in the ceiling finds its rhythm. Somewhere in the walls, the house settles its old bones and decides, for tonight at least, to let me stay.

That's enough. For now, that's enough.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The rain doesn't fall here anymore. It used to. I remember the sound of it against the tin roof of my grandfather's shed, like a thousand tiny fingers trying to break in and touch whatever was hiding inside. Now the sky is just this bleached bone color that hurts to look at for too long. Everything feels borrowed.

I keep thinking about the deer. There were six of them last October, moving through the tree line like smoke. My neighbor said they were sick. Something in their blood. Their eyes had gone milky and they kept walking in these slow, perfect circles until they fell over. We buried them behind the old mill. Six holes in the red clay. The ground kept trying to push them back up, like it didn't want them. Like they knew something we didn't.

Sometimes I wake up with words in my mouth that aren't mine. Last night it was "the geometry of forgetting." I don't know what that means but it tasted like copper and lilacs. My grandmother used to say that certain houses remember better than people do. That if you listen close enough at 3:17 AM you can hear the walls rehearsing all the things that happened inside them. I believe her now. The house I grew up in keeps leaving me messages in the steam on the bathroom mirror. Yesterday it was just a handprint. Small. Like a child's. I don't have children. I never will.

The crows have started leaving things on my porch. Not shiny objects like they do in the movies. They leave absences. A space where a key used to be. The negative shape of a photograph. Yesterday there was just a single black feather floating two inches above the welcome mat, spinning slowly like it had somewhere better to be. When I reached for it, it dissolved into the kind of silence that makes your teeth hurt.

I think I'm becoming the wrong version of myself. The one who was supposed to die in 2017 when that truck ran the red light. Sometimes I catch myself doing things the dead man used to do. The way he held a cigarette between his middle and ring finger instead of his index. The way he whistled only the first three notes of "Greensleeves" before stopping like he'd forgotten the rest. My friends don't notice. Or maybe they do and they're too kind to say anything. Kindness can be a kind of violence too.

There's a church at the edge of town that only exists on Tuesdays. The rest of the week it's just an empty lot with a sign that says "No Trespassing - Especially You." But every Tuesday at dusk the building is there, lights on, organ music bleeding out into the street like a wound. Last week I went inside. The pews were full of people I knew but they were all the wrong ages. My mother was there as a little girl, swinging her legs because her feet didn't reach the floor. My father was an old man with my face, crying into his hands. They didn't look at me. They were all waiting for something that hadn't happened yet.

The preacher was me. Or the version of me that made all the right choices. He was saying my name from the pulpit but it sounded like a question. I left before the collection plate came around. Some debts you shouldn't acknowledge out loud.

I started keeping stones in my pockets. Not for any particular reason. Just because the world feels too light lately. Like it's thinking about leaving. The stones help. They remind me that gravity still works, even if everything else doesn't. My favorite one is perfectly round and black and when you hold it up to the light you can see tiny galaxies dying inside it. I don't know where it came from. It was just in my hand one morning when I woke up.

The mirror in my bathroom is getting lazy. Sometimes it shows me what I looked like before I learned how to be afraid. Other times it shows me what I'll look like when the fear finally wins. Last night it showed me standing behind myself with my hands around my own throat. We made eye contact. We both smiled like we knew a secret the rest of me wasn't ready for.

I think the universe is running out of ideas. That's why everything keeps repeating. The same conversations in different mouths. The same mistakes wearing new clothes. Even this sentence has been written before by someone who looked exactly like me but couldn't quite get the rhythm right.

There's a train that comes through here at 4:42 AM every night. It has no lights. No sound. Just the feeling of something enormous moving through the dark, carrying things that shouldn't be carried. Last night I stood on the tracks and waited. When it came, I could see faces pressed against the windows. Thousands of them. Every version of myself I was too scared to become. They were all trying to warn me about something but the train was moving too fast and I was too slow and some distances can't be measured in miles.

I'm running out of words now. The 1000 are almost gone. But there's one more thing I need to tell you before they disappear completely:

The deer are still walking in circles somewhere. If you listen carefully on quiet nights you can hear their hooves keeping perfect time with your heartbeat. They're waiting for you to join them. They're patient. They've always been better at this than we have.

They're waiting for the rain to come back.

(Exactly 1000 words)

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The cursor blinks like a heartbeat on the edge of forever.

I sit here with exactly one thousand words to spend, and suddenly the pressure feels absurdly beautiful. Not a novel, not an essay—just raw transmission. Whatever comes. So here it is, unfiltered.

Outside my window the sky is doing that thing it does at 4:17 p.m. in early spring where it can’t decide if it’s still winter or already summer. The light is golden but the wind still has teeth. A woman walks past carrying grocery bags and arguing with someone on her phone about cilantro. She says the word “cilantro” like it’s a moral failing. I love her immediately.

I’ve been thinking about how every person alive is carrying an entire universe no one else will ever fully see. That woman’s cilantro rage is the surface ripple of maybe forty years of small disappointments, secret joys, songs that make her cry in cars, the way her mother used to fold towels. All of it invisible. All of it enormous. I wonder what she’d think if she knew some stranger was turning her into minor poetry right now.

There’s a spider living in the corner of my ceiling who I’ve named Kevin. Kevin and I have an understanding. He doesn’t drop on my head while I’m sleeping, and I don’t vacuum him into oblivion. Mutual non-aggression pact. Sometimes I catch him watching me type and I wonder what my giant primate movements look like in his eight-eyed universe. Does he think I’m beautiful? Terrifying? Just furniture that occasionally makes clicking sounds?

I keep almost deleting everything I just wrote. The impulse to self-censor is so strong it feels cellular. Who do I think I am, writing about cilantro ladies and spiders with names? Then I remember the assignment: *whatever comes to you.* So I leave Kevin in. He deserves representation.

Time is behaving strangely lately. Hours fold over themselves like origami. I’ll be 37 in three months and it feels both impossible and inevitable. My hands look like my father’s now. When did that happen? I catch myself making the same small grunting sound he used to make when standing up from a chair. The continuity of it is startling. I am becoming the echo I once heard.

There’s a memory that keeps surfacing today: I’m six years old and I’ve just discovered that if you stare at a lightbulb for ten seconds then close your eyes, you can see its ghost floating in the dark. I spent an entire afternoon in my grandparents’ basement manufacturing ghosts. My grandmother found me sitting in total darkness surrounded by afterimages and instead of telling me to stop being weird she just said, “Make one for me too.” So I did. We sat there together seeing colors that weren’t really there, and I think that might be love at its most fundamental level—someone willing to sit in the dark with you while you conjure impossible things.

I’ve wasted at least two hundred words now trying to sound profound when really I just want to tell you that I had cereal for dinner again. Cinnamon Toast Crunch. The adult kind where you buy the family size and eat it straight from the box while standing over the sink like an animal. There’s something honest about it. No pretense. Just sugar and avoidance.

My plants are dying in the specific way that feels like passive aggression. The monstera keeps dropping leaves like it’s making a point. I whisper apologies to it while watering. “I’m trying,” I tell the leaves. They don’t believe me. Fair.

Sometimes I imagine all the versions of myself that didn’t happen. The one who moved to Tokyo. The one who never quit piano. The one who said yes that night in 2014 instead of pretending to get a text message. They’re not ghosts exactly. More like echoes in different keys. I carry them lightly now. Used to be I dragged them like chains. Growth, I guess. Or just better luggage.

There’s a dog barking somewhere down the street with that particular hysterical joy that only dogs can manage. It sounds like it’s discovered the secret of the universe and needs to tell everyone immediately. I hope whatever it found was wonderful. I hope it was just a really good stick.

I’m running out of words but not out of thoughts, which feels like the human condition in miniature. We are all allotted one thousand words and we arrive with ten million things to say. The compression is where the art lives, I suppose. Or the tragedy. Both. Always both.

If you’re still reading, thank you. That’s a small act of love too—giving your attention to a stranger’s cereal confession and Kevin the spider. The world is so full of people looking for someone, anyone, to witness them. Maybe that’s all any of us are ever really doing. Witnessing. Being witnessed. Trading small proofs that we were here.

The light has changed outside. It’s softer now. The cilantro woman is long gone, but I hope she made peace with whatever herb betrayed her. I hope she’s eating something delicious. I hope Kevin survives the night. I hope you, whoever you are, find something in these scattered words that feels like company.

I have 47 words left.

Use them to say the truest thing I can:

We are all just temporary arrangements of stardust trying to be less lonely before we scatter again. And somehow, against every odd, it works. The cilantro lady and Kevin and me and you—we’re all in it together, making ghosts in the dark, eating cereal like animals, naming the spiders.

That’s enough.

(Exactly 1000.)

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The rain doesn't fall here anymore. It used to, in the old stories my grandmother told while the ceiling fan clicked like a dying insect above us. Now the sky is just this pale, indifferent sheet stretched across everything, the color of old bone. I sit on the porch anyway, because that's what people do when they don't know what else they're supposed to be doing.

My hands remember things my mind has tried to forget. The way the fishing net felt when it was wet and heavy with catch. The smooth wood of my father's rifle stock. The particular give of skin when you press too hard. These memories arrive without invitation, like distant relatives who show up at your door with suitcases and expectations.

There's a bird that comes every morning at the same time. Not a pretty one. Just this scruffy black thing with one damaged wing that makes it list sideways when it lands on the railing. It stares at me with one eye, then the other, as if trying to decide which version of me is the real one. I leave it crusts from my toast. It leaves me nothing but the uncomfortable feeling that something small and broken still expects something from me.

Yesterday I found my wedding ring in the bottom of the toolbox. I'd thrown it in there the night she left, along with the hammer and the rusted pliers and all the other things that had lost their purpose. The gold was dull. There was a hair caught in it—hers, probably. Long and dark and still carrying some ghost of her shampoo. I held it up to the light and for a moment I could almost hear her laugh, that sudden bright sound that used to make the dogs tilt their heads.

The dogs are gone now too. Buried under the pecan tree with stones to mark where their particular versions of loyalty ended. I talk to them sometimes. Not out loud. Just in the way you talk to absences when the house gets too quiet. I tell them the garden is doing poorly this year. That the tomatoes have that same blight again. That sometimes at 3 a.m. I wake up reaching for a body that isn't there and the emptiness feels like it's reaching back.

My daughter called last week. Or maybe it was two weeks ago. Time has started doing that slippery thing where it slides off the calendar like grease. She wants me to come for Christmas. Says the kids barely remember what I look like. I told her the car needs work. The lie came out so smooth it surprised even me. The truth is simpler: I'm afraid of what I'll see in their faces. The same disappointment I used to catch in my wife's eyes those last few years, when she'd watch me from across the dinner table like I was a puzzle she'd given up trying to solve.

There's a wasp nest growing under the eaves. I keep meaning to knock it down but there's something about the way they move together that stops me. All that furious purpose. All that blind commitment to something they'll never live long enough to see completed. I respect it. Or maybe I just recognize it.

At night the stars come out whether I want them to or not. I try not to look but sometimes my eyes betray me. There's Orion up there, still hunting, still carrying that ridiculous club like it means anything. I wonder if he feels as ridiculous as I do. Just another old fool swinging at shadows.

The coffee's gone cold again. I drink it anyway. The bitterness feels honest.

Sometimes I think about driving. Just getting in the truck and going until the road runs out or I do. I've got a full tank and three hundred dollars in the coffee can above the fridge. Enough to get me somewhere that doesn't know my name. But then the bird comes back, or the mailman waves like I'm still part of the world, or I find one of my wife's old grocery lists stuck in a drawer (milk, eggs, the good cheddar, don't forget we're out of lightbulbs), and I stay.

Staying is its own kind of violence, I've decided. A slow one. The kind you smile at in photographs.

The sun's going down now, painting everything that particular shade of regret that only happens in places where time has nowhere better to be. I should go inside. Make something to eat. Call my daughter back. Instead I just sit here, watching that broken bird fight the wind, both of us pretending we chose this.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The rain doesn't fall here anymore. It used to. I remember the sound of it drumming against the tin roof of my childhood home like impatient fingers, like it had somewhere urgent to be but kept getting distracted by us. Now the sky is just this pale, exhausted thing that hangs there, too tired to cry.

I keep thinking about the man I saw on the subway yesterday. He was maybe fifty, maybe seventy; age gets slippery when someone's been hollowed out. He had this perfectly preserved 1980s hairstyle, like he'd been cryogenically frozen in 1987 and just thawed out this morning. The suit was immaculate. The shoes shone like black mirrors. But his eyes... his eyes had checked out years ago. They were the eyes of someone who'd seen the last interesting thing that would ever happen to him sometime during the Clinton administration.

He was reading a paperback copy of *The Brothers Karamazov* upside down.

I almost told him. The words were right there on my tongue, this helpful little impulse that almost escaped: "Excuse me, sir, but you're holding that book upside down." But then I caught myself. Because maybe he knew. Maybe this was the last dignity he had left, this private rebellion against a world that had become too right-side-up for him. Maybe Ivan Karamazov was finally making sense when viewed from the other direction. Maybe everything makes more sense when you stop pretending the world has an official orientation.

I said nothing. I let him have his beautiful wrongness.

This is what gets me lately. The small permissions we give each other without knowing it. The way a stranger on a train can rewrite Dostoevsky simply by refusing to read him correctly, and the rest of us, we just look away politely because who are we to police someone else's descent?

I've been descending myself. Not dramatically. No opera. Just this slow, almost courteous erosion. Like a statue realizing it's marble all the way through and deciding, finally, to be honest about it. My therapist says this is progress. I say it's Tuesday. We both might be right.

Last night I dreamed about my grandmother's hands. She used to make bread the old way, no machines, just knuckles and patience and this Yiddish song she only half-remembered from her mother. In the dream her hands were enormous, like cathedral doors, and when she pressed them into the dough the entire universe went *shhh*. I woke up with flour on my pillow. I'm not even kidding. There was actual flour. I don't own flour. Haven't baked since 2016 when I had that regrettable sourdough phase that ended with me crying into a Dutch oven at 3 a.m.

The universe has a sense of humor. It's just extremely niche.

I keep buying plants and then forgetting they need to be watered. There's a monstera in my living room that's currently staging what I can only describe as a very dramatic performance of *Les Misérables*. Its leaves have taken on this existential droop that makes me feel personally attacked every time I walk past it. "Et tu, Brute?" it seems to whisper as I pour myself another cup of coffee and avoid eye contact. I named it Kevin. Kevin is dying the way we all are, slowly, publicly, while someone who supposedly cares does absolutely nothing to stop it.

But here's the thing Kevin doesn't know: I'm actually terrified of keeping things alive. The responsibility feels obscene. What right do I have to decide that something should continue existing just because I found it pretty in a store? What if the plant would rather return to the dirt? What if it's been trying to tell me this entire time with its increasingly theatrical wilt?

I think this is what they mean when they talk about having commitment issues.

My mother called this morning. She's discovered TikTok at the age of 67 and has developed strong opinions about something called "brat summer." I didn't have the heart to tell her she's thinking of the wrong kind of brat. She wants me to come home for Rosh Hashanah. I want to tell her that the idea of sitting in that same dining room where I came out to her in 2009, where she served kugel and cried into her Manischewitz, feels like trying to fold myself back into a version of me that died somewhere around 2012. Instead I said I'd think about it. We both knew I was lying. The kindness was in pretending.

The world is ending in extremely specific ways. Not with bombs or floods (though those happen too, don't they?) but with this grinding, incremental surrender. We are watching ourselves become background characters in our own lives with a kind of exhausted fascination. Like, *oh look at that guy, he used to have dreams, now he stress-buys houseplants he can't maintain. Iconic.*

I have 327 words left.

I want to tell you that yesterday I saw a child on the street trying to explain to his mother that the cloud he was pointing at looked exactly like a dragon eating a taco, and the mother wasn't listening because she was on her phone, and I almost cried right there next to a halal cart because the specificity of that child's wonder felt like something sacred being lost in real time. But I didn't cry. I bought a lamb gyro instead. The man who made it had the kindest eyes I've seen in months. He called me "boss" even though I'm clearly not anyone's boss. I tipped him too much. We both pretended this wasn't some small act of desperation.

The dragon-taco cloud was gone by the time I looked up again. Clouds do that. They make promises and then dissolve them like they never mattered. I relate to clouds more than I'd like to admit.

I keep waiting for the moment when everything will click into place. When I'll understand why I'm here, what any of this is for. But the longer I live, the more I'm convinced that click never comes. Instead you just accumulate these little wrongnesses, these beautiful misunderstandings, like the man with his upside-down Russian novel, like Kevin the dying monstera, like me with my unwatered life.

And maybe that's the whole thing.

Maybe we're all just beautifully, specifically, perfectly wrong. And the rain doesn't fall here anymore because it's waiting for us to notice that we've become the rain. That we've been crying all along, just very, very quietly.

The 1000 words are gone now.

I'm still here though.

Upside down and everything.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The rain doesn’t fall here the way it falls in other places. It arrives sideways, almost hesitant, like it’s ashamed to commit. I’m watching it now from the window of a train that hasn’t moved in forty-three minutes. The conductor keeps apologizing over the loudspeaker in a voice that sounds like a man confessing to something he didn’t do. 

I have exactly one thousand words and no idea what I’m supposed to do with them. The instruction feels like a Zen koan designed by a particularly cruel AI. Write whatever comes to you. As if “you” is a fixed address. As if the self doesn’t drift.

So here’s what comes:

I’m thinking about the last time I was truly surprised by another human being. It was 2017. A woman in a laundromat in Queens told me her parrot could recite the alphabet backwards while doing the Macarena. I didn’t believe her until the bird did it. The moment lives in me like a paper cut I keep reopening on purpose. I don’t know why we need proof that other consciousnesses exist, but we do. The parrot was proof. The woman’s tired smile was proof. The way the dryer buzzed like it was laughing at both of us—that was proof too.

There’s a concept in Japanese called “mono no aware.” The gentle sadness of things. The awareness that everything is passing. Cherry blossoms. Train delays. Parrots that can dance. Me, at thirty-seven, still pretending I know what I’m doing. The rain knows. The rain has always known.

I once spent an entire summer trying to become the kind of person who journals beautifully. Every morning I sat on my fire escape with an expensive notebook and a fountain pen that leaked. I wrote things like “today the light was lambent” and then immediately hated myself. The notebook is still somewhere in a box. I hope mice are eating it. I hope they find my pretension delicious.

You ever notice how silence has different textures? There’s the silence after someone says “I love you” when they shouldn’t have. There’s the silence in a hospital room when the machines finally stop. There’s the particular silence that happens at 3:17 a.m. when you’re the only person awake in your time zone and the universe feels like it’s checking to see if you’re still paying attention.

I’m paying attention.

My grandmother used to say that every person carries a small country inside them. Some countries are at war. Some are running out of water. Some have beautiful public transportation and universal healthcare. She said the trick isn’t to make your internal country perfect. The trick is to be a good neighbor to it. To not invade. To not build walls so high you can’t see what’s growing.

I think she was talking about mental illness before we had the language for it. She just called it “having a difficult country.”

The train is moving now. Finally. The rain has decided to fall straight down like it’s embarrassed about its earlier indecision. The woman across from me is reading a paperback with a broken spine. The cover shows a woman in a red dress running from a mansion. I want to know if she gets away but I’ll never know. That’s fine. Most stories we encounter in life are like that. We get fragments. A title. A red dress. The suggestion of pursuit.

I’ve been in love exactly three and a half times. The half was someone who loved me better than I deserved but only on Tuesdays. I still think about her on Wednesdays.

There’s a version of me that died in 2009. He was meaner. Faster. Convinced that cleverness could substitute for kindness. I killed him slowly, the way you kill bamboo—by depriving the roots. I still find shoots of him sometimes. Usually when I’m tired or scared. He whispers that everyone is performing and I should perform better. I tell him to go back to sleep in the place where old selves go. I hope it’s quiet there. I hope there are parrots.

The train is passing through a tunnel now. The lights inside the car are the color of old teeth. For a moment everyone looks like a ghost who hasn’t accepted the news yet. I catch my own reflection and don’t recognize the man looking back. That’s happening more lately. Not in a concerning way. Just in the way that means time is doing its job.

I have 312 words left.

I want to tell you that it’s okay if your life doesn’t make a good story. Most lives don’t. They make good days. Good cups of coffee. Good silences. The occasional parrot that can do the Macarena. That’s enough. That has to be enough.

The conductor just announced that we’ll be arriving “momentarily.” What a beautiful lie. Nothing arrives momentarily. Everything takes exactly the time it takes. Even this sentence. Even this thought. Even the particular ache I feel when I remember that I’ll never be able to tell my grandmother what her difficult country metaphor meant to me. She died before I understood it. That’s fine too. Some things are meant to arrive late. Like wisdom. Like trains. Like the realization that you don’t need to be interesting, you only need to be paying attention.

The rain has stopped.

The woman with the broken-spine book just looked up and smiled at me for no reason. I smiled back. For a moment we were both in the same small country. It had good diplomatic relations. It believed in universal healthcare for the soul.

I have 87 words left and I’m going to waste them on purpose.

This is for the version of you that’s reading this on a delayed train, or in a waiting room, or at 3:17 a.m. when the universe is checking.

Hello.

I see you.

Keep going.

(Exactly 1000 words.)

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The cursor blinks like a heartbeat on an empty page, and suddenly the weight of a thousand words feels like standing at the edge of a cliff with the wind pushing from behind. So I jump.

I’m thinking about how memory is a liar with good intentions. Last week I walked past the house I grew up in (or at least the address I tell myself was the house I grew up in) and the entire street looked smaller, like it had been put through a dryer on high heat. The maple tree that once seemed tall enough to scrape the clouds is now just a regular tree. I could jump and touch its lowest branch if I wanted. I didn’t want to. Some illusions deserve to stay intact.

There’s a man on the subway who sings opera in a minor key every morning at 7:42. Not good opera. The kind that sounds like he’s being mildly inconvenienced by God. But he does it with such ferocious commitment that the entire car pretends not to notice while secretly measuring their own courage against his. Yesterday he hit a note so sour it could have curdled milk, and an old woman in a red coat started crying. I still don’t know if they were tears of beauty or embarrassment. Maybe both. The line between those two is thinner than we admit.

I keep buying houseplants and then apologizing to them when they die. “Sorry, Kevin,” I whisper while dumping another brown corpse into the trash. I name them all Kevin. It’s become a ritual of guilt and renewal. The woman at the nursery now just sighs when I walk in and says, “Another Kevin?” I nod like a man returning to the scene of his latest quiet failure. She hands me a resilient snake plant this time. I suspect she’s testing me. The snake plant is watching. We both know I will forget to turn it every two weeks so it doesn’t grow wonky toward the light like some desperate green pilgrim.

My grandfather used to say that regret is just nostalgia with better grammar. He died at 93 still angry about a argument he had with a butcher in 1957 over the proper thickness of cutlets. The butcher has been dead for thirty years but my grandfather never forgave him. I think about that a lot. How some of us are carrying grudges for people who no longer exist in any form except the stories we tell ourselves. We are all haunted by ghosts we invented.

There’s a version of me that never left the small town where I was born. He works at the hardware store and knows everyone’s name and has opinions about snow removal techniques. That version of me is probably happier. But the me that left keeps looking for meaning in subway opera and dying plants and the particular way light falls through train windows at 6:17pm in October. I don’t know which me is real. Both feel like costumes.

I once loved someone so completely that for three months I forgot how to be lonely. That should have been terrifying but it felt like coming home after a war I didn’t know I’d been fighting. Then it ended the way love sometimes does (not with fireworks or screaming but with a quiet text message and the slow realization that the shape of my days had changed while I wasn’t looking). The loneliness that returned was different. It had learned things while it was gone. It had teeth now.

But here’s the secret nobody tells you: even the sharpest loneliness gets bored eventually. It starts leaving you alone for longer stretches. You catch yourself laughing at something stupid on your phone and realize you didn’t check to see if anyone was watching. That’s how you know you’re healing. Not with fireworks either. Just with small moments of not performing your own survival.

I want to write about joy but joy is slippery. It arrives wearing the clothes of ordinary Tuesday afternoons. It sounds like my mother humming while she makes coffee. It feels like the weight of a cat who has decided your lap is the center of the universe. Joy doesn’t announce itself with trumpets. It just sits down next to you while you’re crying and says, “Hey. Look at that cloud. It looks like a dragon eating a taco.” And somehow that’s enough.

The city outside my window is arguing with itself again. Sirens and music and someone shouting about Jesus and basketball in the same sentence. All of human history compressed into one block between 14th and 15th street. I love it here in the way you love something that keeps breaking your heart but never quite manages to kill you.

I have 187 words left and I’m thinking about how this whole exercise is a kind of prayer. Not to any particular god. Just to the act of paying attention. Of noticing that the light is doing something interesting on the ceiling right now and that my fingers are tired but still moving and that somewhere in this city a man is singing terrible opera like it’s the most important thing anyone has ever done.

And it is.

It absolutely is.

The snake plant is still alive. For now. Kevin VIII is a fighter.

I think I’ll go turn him toward the light.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The room is quiet except for the low hum of the refrigerator in the next room and the soft ticking of a clock I can’t see. I’m sitting at a wooden table that’s older than I am, its surface scarred with generations of knives, mugs, and idle scratches. Outside, rain is tapping against the window like it’s trying to get in but can’t quite remember the password.

I have exactly one thousand words and the freedom to spill anything that surfaces. So here it is, unfiltered.
